questions.question: What is null safety in Dart and what are nullable vs non-nullable types?
questions.answer:
Null safety (Dart 2.12+) means variables cannot be null by default — you must explicitly opt-in with the ? suffix.

• Non-nullable: String name = 'Alice'; — must always have a value
• Nullable: String? name; — can be null
• Late: late String name; — non-nullable but initialized later

Key operators:
• ?. (null-aware access): user?.email
• ?? (null coalescing): name ?? 'Guest'
• ??= (null assignment): name ??= 'Default'
• ! (non-null assertion): name! — throws if null at runtime

questions.question: What are the main data types in Dart?
questions.answer:
Dart is statically typed with these core types:

• int — 64-bit integer
• double — 64-bit floating point
• num — parent of int and double
• String — immutable UTF-16 string
• bool — true/false
• List<T> — ordered, indexed collection
• Map<K,V> — key-value pairs, keys unique
• Set<T> — unordered unique elements
• dynamic — disables type checking (avoid!)
• Object? — root of the type hierarchy
• void — no return value
• Never — function that never returns (throws/loops)

questions.question: Explain the difference between final and const in Dart.
questions.answer:
Both prevent reassignment, but differ in when the value is determined:

• final — set once at RUNTIME. Can hold values computed when the app runs.
• const — compile-time constant. Value must be known at compile time (literals, const expressions).

const objects are canonicalized — two identical const objects share the same memory instance.
In Flutter: use const for widget constructors to enable the framework's optimization (widget tree diffing skips const widgets).

questions.question: What are arrow functions, anonymous functions, and closures in Dart?
questions.answer:
Dart supports multiple function styles:

• Named functions — standard declaration with return type
• Arrow functions — single-expression shorthand using =>
• Anonymous functions (lambdas) — unnamed, often passed as arguments
• Closures — functions that capture variables from their enclosing scope

Closures are key to Flutter callbacks, stream handlers, and functional operations on collections.

questions.question: Explain Dart collections: List, Map, Set and their common operations.
questions.answer:
Three main collections in dart:core:

List<T> — ordered, indexed, allows duplicates
Map<K,V> — unordered key-value pairs, unique keys
Set<T> — unordered, unique elements

All support collection-if, collection-for, and spread operators for expressive construction.

questions.question: What are Dart enums and how do enhanced enums work?
questions.answer:
Dart has two types of enums:

Simple enum — a named set of constant values:
  enum Direction { north, south, east, west }

Enhanced enum (Dart 2.17+) — enums with fields, methods, and constructors. They behave like classes but with the enum constraint.

All enums automatically get:
• .name — the String name of the value
• .index — the position (0-based)
• EnumType.values — List of all values

Enhanced enums are ideal for representing rich constants (HTTP methods, status codes, app routes) without a separate class.

questions.question: Explain Dart 3 Records and Pattern Matching.
questions.answer:
Records (Dart 3.0+) are anonymous, immutable, fixed-size aggregations of values — like lightweight tuples. Unlike Map, records are statically typed.

Syntax: (value1, value2) for positional, (name: value) for named fields.

Pattern matching works with:
• switch expressions (exhaustive)
• if-case statements
• Destructuring in assignments

Records are great for returning multiple values from a function without creating a class.

questions.question: What is the difference between final and const in Dart?
questions.answer:
Both are immutable, but work differently:

• final: Runtime constant — value set once during execution
  - Can be initialized with runtime values
  - Instance variables can be final
  - Value known at runtime

• const: Compile-time constant — value must be known at compile time
  - Must be initialized with compile-time constant
  - Deeply immutable (entire object tree)
  - Creates canonical instances (same const objects are identical)
  - Better performance (less memory)

Key differences:
1. When value is determined (runtime vs compile-time)
2. Canonicalization (const reuses instances)
3. Deep vs shallow immutability

questions.question: Explain the cascade operator (..) and spread operator (...) in Dart.
questions.answer:
Cascade operator (..):
• Allows multiple operations on the same object
• Returns the original object, not the method result
• Useful for builder patterns and configuration

Spread operator (...):
• Unpacks elements from a collection into another
• ...? for null-safe spreading
• Useful for combining collections

questions.question: What are collections in Dart and what are their key differences?
questions.answer:
Dart has three main collection types:

1. List<T> — ordered, indexed, allows duplicates
   • Access by index: list[0]
   • Maintains insertion order
   • Can be fixed or growable

2. Set<T> — unordered, unique elements
   • No duplicates (automatically filtered)
   • Fast lookup (O(1) for contains)
   • No index access

3. Map<K,V> — key-value pairs
   • Keys must be unique
   • Fast lookup by key (O(1))
   • Unordered (use LinkedHashMap for order)

All support iteration, mapping, filtering with functional methods.

questions.question: What are typedef and function types in Dart?
questions.answer:
typedef creates type aliases for functions, making code more readable and reusable.

Function types:
• Functions are first-class objects
• Can be assigned to variables
• Passed as parameters
• Returned from functions

typedef benefits:
• Cleaner code
• Better documentation
• Reusable function signatures
• Type safety

questions.question: How do named and optional parameters work in Dart?
questions.answer:
Dart supports two types of optional parameters:

1. Named parameters — {param}
   • Called by name: func(param: value)
   • Can be marked required
   • Any order when calling

2. Positional optional — [param]
   • Called by position
   • Must be at end
   • Can have defaults

Default values can be provided for both types.

questions.question: Explain classes, constructors, and named constructors in Dart.
questions.answer:
Every class in Dart automatically extends Object. Constructor types:

• Default — same name as class, can use initializer shorthand (this.field)
• Named — ClassName.name(...), for alternative creation paths
• Factory — can return existing instances or subtypes; used for fromJson patterns
• Const — for compile-time constant objects
• Redirecting — delegates to another constructor with :this(...)

Initializer list (: field = value before body) runs before the constructor body — required for initializing final fields.

questions.question: What is the difference between abstract class, interface, and mixin in Dart?
questions.answer:
Dart uses a unified class system with keywords that define usage:

• abstract class — cannot be instantiated; can have abstract (no body) and concrete methods. Used as a base class via extends.
• interface — no keyword; any class can act as an interface via implements. You must implement ALL members (no implementation reuse).
• mixin — reusable code mixed into classes via with. Can have state, concrete methods, but cannot be instantiated.

Key rules:
• extends → single inheritance, reuses parent implementation
• implements → contract only, must reimplement everything
• with → multiple mixins allowed, copies members into class

questions.question: What are extensions in Dart and when would you use them?
questions.answer:
Extensions (Dart 2.7+) add methods/getters to existing types without modifying or subclassing them.

Use cases:
• Utility methods on String, int, List, DateTime
• Adding methods to third-party types you don't own
• Flutter: widget helper methods for cleaner widget trees
• Organizing helpers close to the type they extend

Limitations:
• Cannot add instance variables (only methods, getters, setters, operators)
• Cannot override existing methods
• Not visible unless imported

questions.question: How does operator overloading work in Dart?
questions.answer:
Dart allows overloading a fixed set of operators by defining them as methods:
==, <, >, <=, >=, +, -, *, /, ~/, %, ^, &, |, <<, >>, >>>, [], []=, ~, unary-

When you override ==, you should also override hashCode to maintain the contract: objects that are equal must have the same hashCode.

Use operator overloading to make custom classes work naturally with arithmetic or comparison operations (e.g. Vector, Money, Version).

questions.question: What is the difference between covariant and contravariant types in Dart?
questions.answer:
Dart generics are invariant by default — List<Dog> is NOT a subtype of List<Animal> even though Dog extends Animal. This prevents type errors at runtime.

covariant keyword — relaxes type safety on a specific parameter to allow subtype override. Useful when you know the runtime type is correct.

Sound type system: Dart uses sound null safety and generics. The type system guarantees no implicit null errors.

Key rules:
• Function return types are covariant (can return a subtype)
• Function parameter types are contravariant in theory (but Dart is unsound here for practicality)
• Use covariant when overriding with a more specific parameter type

questions.question: What are factory constructors and when would you use them?
questions.answer:
Factory constructors:
• Use the factory keyword
• Can return an instance from cache or subclass
• Don't automatically create a new instance
• Useful for singletons, caching, or returning subtypes

Use cases:
1. Singleton pattern
2. Object pooling/caching
3. Returning different subtypes
4. Factory pattern implementation
5. Constructing from different sources (JSON, etc.)

questions.question: Explain mixins in Dart and when to use them.
questions.answer:
Mixins provide a way to reuse code across multiple class hierarchies without traditional inheritance.

Key features:
• Use mixin keyword (or class that can be mixed in)
• Apply with with keyword
• Can use multiple mixins
• Linear order of application (right to left)
• Cannot be instantiated directly
• Can have fields, methods, and state

Use cases:
• Share behavior across unrelated classes
• Add functionality without deep inheritance
• Implement composition over inheritance

questions.question: What is the difference between extends, implements, and with in Dart?
questions.answer:
Three ways to relate classes in Dart:

1. extends — Inheritance
   • Single inheritance only
   • Inherits all members
   • Can override methods
   • super keyword to access parent

2. implements — Interface
   • Multiple interfaces allowed
   • Must implement ALL members
   • No code reuse
   • Type compatibility

3. with — Mixins
   • Multiple mixins allowed
   • Code reuse without inheritance
   • Applied left to right
   • Diamond problem solved

Can combine: class X extends Y with A, B implements C, D

questions.question: What are extension methods in Dart and how do you use them?
questions.answer:
Extension methods let you add functionality to existing types without modifying them or creating subclasses.

Features:
• Add methods to any type (even built-in types)
• Static resolution (compile-time)
• Cannot override existing methods
• Can have generic type parameters
• Scoped by import

Use cases:
• Add utility methods to core types
• Domain-specific helpers
• Cleaner API design
• Flutter widget helpers

questions.question: What is Flutter and how does it differ from other cross-platform frameworks?
questions.answer:
Flutter is Google's UI toolkit for building natively compiled apps from a single codebase for mobile, web, and desktop.

Key differentiators:
• Own rendering engine (Skia / Impeller) — doesn't use native UI widgets; Flutter draws every pixel itself → pixel-perfect, consistent across platforms
• Dart language — AOT compiled for production (fast), JIT for development (hot reload)
• Widget-based — everything is a widget, composable by design
• No JavaScript bridge — unlike React Native, Flutter talks directly to the GPU via its engine

vs React Native: Uses native UI components via JS bridge → platform-native look, but bridge overhead and platform inconsistencies
vs Xamarin: Shares business logic, native UI per platform — no shared UI layer
vs Flutter: Shares everything including UI, own renderer → fastest UI updates, consistent

Architecture layers: Dart app → Flutter Framework → Flutter Engine (C++) → Skia/Impeller → GPU

questions.question: What is the difference between StatelessWidget and StatefulWidget?
questions.answer:
StatelessWidget — immutable description of UI. Rebuild only when parent provides new data. Use when the widget's appearance depends only on its constructor arguments.

StatefulWidget — splits into Widget (immutable config) + State (mutable). Call setState() to schedule a rebuild. Use for user interactions, animations, network data.

Why the split? Widget objects are lightweight and cheap to recreate on every build. State objects are heavy and persist — they hold the actual data.

Lifecycle (StatefulWidget State):
createState → initState → didChangeDependencies → build → setState → build → deactivate → dispose

questions.question: What is BuildContext and why is it important?
questions.answer:
BuildContext is a handle to a widget's location in the widget tree. Every build() receives a context that represents THAT widget's position.

Key uses:
• Theme.of(context) — material theme data
• Navigator.of(context) — push/pop screens
• MediaQuery.of(context) — screen size, orientation, padding
• Scaffold.of(context) — show snackbar, open drawer
• Provider.of<T>(context) — state access

Critical pitfalls:
• Never use context after async gaps without checking mounted
• Each widget has its OWN context — don't use parent context in child
• Context from an ancestor might not have access to a descendant's inherited widgets

questions.question: What is the widget lifecycle in Flutter?
questions.answer:
StatelessWidget: Constructor → build()

StatefulWidget State lifecycle:
1. createState() — creates the State object
2. initState() — called ONCE after insertion into tree. Set up controllers, subscriptions.
3. didChangeDependencies() — after initState(), and when an InheritedWidget ancestor changes
4. build() — returns widget tree; called on setState() or when dependencies change
5. didUpdateWidget(oldWidget) — when parent passes new configuration (new widget props)
6. setState() — marks dirty, schedules rebuild
7. deactivate() — temporarily removed from tree (might be reinserted)
8. dispose() — permanently removed. Cancel subscriptions, dispose controllers.

questions.question: What is the difference between hot reload, hot restart, and full restart?
questions.answer:
Three ways to update a running Flutter app during development:

Hot Reload (r) — injects new code into the running Dart VM and rebuilds the widget tree. State is PRESERVED. Fastest — milliseconds. Works for most UI changes.

Hot Restart (R) — restarts the Dart VM, resets all state. Slower than hot reload but faster than a full restart. Needed when: changing main(), modifying initState, changing native code.

Full Restart — stops and relaunches the app from scratch. Required when: changing native code (Kotlin/Swift), adding plugins, changing build config.

Limitations of hot reload: doesn't work for changes to main(), native code, global variable initializers, or static fields.

questions.question: What is MediaQuery and how do you build responsive layouts?
questions.answer:
MediaQuery provides information about the device screen: size, pixel ratio, text scale, padding (safe area), orientation, and accessibility settings.

Responsive layout strategies:
• MediaQuery.sizeOf(context) — get screen dimensions
• LayoutBuilder — get available constraints from parent
• OrientationBuilder — react to portrait/landscape changes
• AdaptiveLayout / adaptive_breakpoints — Google's responsive package

Best practice: prefer LayoutBuilder over MediaQuery for layouts — it responds to the actual available space (accounts for sidebars, drawers) rather than the full screen size.

questions.question: Explain the difference between StatelessWidget and StatefulWidget lifecycle.
questions.answer:
StatelessWidget lifecycle:
1. Constructor called
2. build() called
3. Widget rendered
• build() called again only when parent rebuilds with different parameters
• Immutable — cannot change internal state

StatefulWidget lifecycle:
1. Constructor (widget)
2. createState() — creates State object (once)
3. initState() — initialization (once)
4. didChangeDependencies() — when InheritedWidget changes
5. build() — renders UI
6. didUpdateWidget() — when parent rebuilds with new widget
7. setState() — triggers rebuild
8. deactivate() — when removed from tree (might be reinserted)
9. dispose() — cleanup (once, when removed permanently)

State object persists across rebuilds, widget does not.

questions.question: What is the difference between hot reload and hot restart?
questions.answer:
Hot Reload:
• Injects updated code into running Dart VM
• Preserves app state
• Fast (< 1 second)
• Works for UI changes, most code changes
• Doesn't re-run main() or initState()

Hot Restart:
• Restarts the app from scratch
• Loses all state
• Slower (few seconds)
• Re-runs main() and all initialization
• Needed for: main() changes, global variables, native code

When hot reload doesn't work:
• Changing app initialization
• Adding/removing imports
• Changing class to mixin
• Modifying const to final

questions.question: Explain Keys in Flutter and when they are necessary.
questions.answer:
Keys help Flutter identify widgets uniquely in the widget tree.

Types of Keys:
• ValueKey — based on value
• ObjectKey — based on object identity
• UniqueKey — always unique
• GlobalKey — access widget state anywhere
• PageStorageKey — preserve scroll position

When you NEED keys:
• Reordering stateful widgets
• Modifying collection of widgets
• Preserving state when widget moves
• Accessing widget state from outside

Flutter's diffing algorithm uses keys to match old/new widgets.

questions.question: What is the difference between Container, SizedBox, Padding, and ColoredBox?
questions.answer:
Use the simplest widget that meets your needs — Flutter optimizes simpler widget trees.

• SizedBox — constraints only (width/height). Lightest spacer widget.
• Padding — only adds padding. More efficient than Container for this.
• ColoredBox — only sets background color. Cheaper than Container.
• DecoratedBox — decoration (border, shadow, gradient) without size.
• Container — combines sizing + padding + margin + decoration + transform. Creates multiple intermediary widgets internally. Use only when you need multiple features at once.

questions.question: Explain Row, Column, and Stack with their key properties.
questions.answer:
Row — horizontal layout (main axis = horizontal)
Column — vertical layout (main axis = vertical)
Stack — overlapping layers (z-axis)

Row/Column key properties:
• mainAxisAlignment: start, end, center, spaceBetween, spaceAround, spaceEvenly
• crossAxisAlignment: start, end, center, stretch, baseline
• mainAxisSize: max (fill space), min (wrap children)

Stack key properties:
• alignment — default position for un-positioned children
• fit — StackFit.loose (children choose size) or StackFit.expand
• Positioned — absolute positioning within Stack

questions.question: What is the difference between ListView, GridView, and CustomScrollView?
questions.answer:
ListView — scrollable list (single axis):
• ListView() — all children built immediately (small, fixed lists only)
• ListView.builder() — lazy rendering, efficient for large/infinite lists
• ListView.separated() — adds separators between items

GridView — 2D scrollable grid:
• GridView.count() — fixed column count
• GridView.builder() — lazy, efficient
• GridView.extent() — fixed max item cross-axis size

CustomScrollView — compose multiple scroll areas as slivers in one coordinated scroll view. Most flexible, most complex. Use SliverAppBar, SliverList, SliverGrid, SliverToBoxAdapter.

questions.question: How do you handle forms and validation in Flutter?
questions.answer:
Flutter's form system uses:
• Form — wraps form fields; provides a GlobalKey<FormState> to validate/save/reset
• TextFormField — combines TextField with Form integration; has validator callback
• FormField<T> — base class for any custom form field

Validation flow:
1. User taps submit
2. _formKey.currentState!.validate() — calls all field validators, shows error text
3. If valid → _formKey.currentState!.save() — calls onSaved callbacks
4. Collect data and proceed

Use AutovalidateMode.onUserInteraction to validate as user types.

questions.question: How does CustomPainter work and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
CustomPainter gives you direct access to a Canvas to draw anything — shapes, paths, gradients, charts — that built-in widgets cannot achieve.

Steps:
1. Extend CustomPainter and implement paint() and shouldRepaint()
2. Use CustomPaint widget, passing your painter
3. Use Canvas API: drawLine, drawCircle, drawPath, drawRect, drawArc, drawText, drawImage

shouldRepaint() controls when to repaint:
• Return false if the visual output hasn't changed (performance optimization)
• Return true only when the painter's properties that affect drawing have changed

Use canvas coordinates — top-left is (0,0), size.width × size.height is available.

questions.question: What is the difference between Expanded and Flexible widgets?
questions.answer:
Both control how children of Row/Column use available space:

Flexible:
• Child can be smaller than allocated space
• flex determines space ratio
• fit: FlexFit.loose (default) — child can shrink
• fit: FlexFit.tight — child must fill space

Expanded:
• Shorthand for Flexible with fit: FlexFit.tight
• Child MUST fill allocated space
• More common than Flexible

flex parameter (both):
• Default is 1
• Ratio of space compared to siblings
• flex: 2 gets twice the space of flex: 1

questions.question: What are the Material 3 button widgets and when do you pick each?
questions.answer:
Material 3 ships five button styles, each with a specific role on the page. Picking the right one is the difference between a clear UI and visual noise.

FilledButton (highest emphasis):
• Solid background, used for the PRIMARY action on a screen
• Examples: "Sign up", "Continue", "Pay now"

FilledButton.tonal (high-emphasis but secondary):
• Tinted background, used for important actions that aren't the primary
• Good when the screen has two important options and you don't want them visually competing

ElevatedButton (legacy primary):
• Drop shadow, originally THE primary button style in Material 2
• In Material 3, prefer FilledButton — ElevatedButton is for cases where elevation specifically matches the material

OutlinedButton (medium emphasis):
• Outlined, transparent fill — the secondary button on a page
• Cancel/Back/Skip alongside a FilledButton

TextButton (lowest emphasis):
• Text only, no border or background
• "Forgot password?", "Learn more", in-line actions in cards

IconButton:
• Square button containing only an icon
• Use `IconButton.filled`, `.filledTonal`, `.outlined` for the matching emphasis levels

Rules of thumb:
• At most ONE FilledButton per screen — that's the primary action
• Use the same button type for consistent CTAs across screens
• Don't sprinkle ElevatedButton everywhere — Material 3 favors flatter UI

questions.question: How do you display images in Flutter — assets, network, memory, file?
questions.answer:
Flutter's `Image` widget renders any image source via constructors that match the input.

Image.asset — bundled with the app:
• Add to pubspec.yaml under `flutter: assets:`
• Supports @1x, @2x, @3x variants for resolution-aware loading
• No network needed; instant

Image.network — fetched at runtime:
• Includes basic in-memory cache only; for offline + disk cache use `cached_network_image`
• `errorBuilder` for fallback when the URL fails

Image.memory — render bytes you already have:
• Useful for downloaded blobs, captured screenshots, base64-decoded images

Image.file — local files (path_provider, picked images):
• Mobile/desktop only; not on web

Key props for any source:
• `fit: BoxFit.cover/contain/fill/...` — how the image fits its box
• `width / height` — display size; combine with `cacheWidth / cacheHeight` to avoid decoding the full source for a thumbnail
• `loadingBuilder` for progressive loading spinners
• `semanticLabel` for accessibility

For SVGs use `flutter_svg` (Image doesn't render SVG); for animated formats (Lottie, Rive) use the dedicated packages.

questions.question: How does LayoutBuilder work and when should you use it?
questions.answer:
LayoutBuilder provides parent constraints to build child widgets responsively.

Key features:
• Receives BoxConstraints from parent
• Builds child based on available space
• Useful for responsive layouts
• Rebuilds when constraints change

Constraints:
• maxWidth, maxHeight — maximum available
• minWidth, minHeight — minimum required
• Use to build adaptive UI

Similar widgets:
• MediaQuery — entire screen size
• LayoutBuilder — parent-specific constraints
• OrientationBuilder — device orientation

questions.question: Explain Navigator 1.0 (imperative) vs go_router (declarative).
questions.answer:
Navigator 1.0 — imperative, stack-based:
• push/pop/pushReplacement/pushAndRemoveUntil
• Simple but struggles with deep links, web URLs, and complex flows

Navigator 2.0 (Router API) — declarative, URL-driven:
• RouterDelegate + RouteInformationParser — complex to implement manually
• go_router (Google-maintained) wraps Navigator 2.0 into a simple API

Use go_router for: any app with deep linking, web URL support, or complex navigation graphs. Use Navigator 1.0 only for very simple apps with no URL routing.

questions.question: How do you implement bottom navigation with persistent state in Flutter?
questions.answer:
Bottom navigation should preserve state in each tab — users expect the Home tab to stay scrolled where they left it.

Options:
• IndexedStack — renders all tabs at once, preserves state. Simple but higher memory use.
• AutomaticKeepAliveClientMixin — explicitly keep state alive in PageView/TabBarView
• go_router ShellRoute — best for deep linking + bottom nav

IndexedStack is the simplest: all children exist in the tree simultaneously, only the selected one is visible. State is never destroyed.

questions.question: How do you pass data between screens and get results back?
questions.answer:
Three approaches for passing data:

1. Constructor arguments (simple, type-safe)
2. Named route arguments
3. Router parameters (go_router)

Getting results back:
• Navigator.push returns Future
• Use .then() or await to get result
• Call Navigator.pop(result) from second screen

questions.question: What is PopScope (formerly WillPopScope) and how do you intercept the back button?
questions.answer:
PopScope (Flutter 3.12+) replaces the older WillPopScope. It lets you control whether a route can be popped and react when the user attempts to leave (system back button on Android, swipe-back on iOS, browser back on web).

Why the rename:
• WillPopScope used a `Future<bool>` callback. It blocked the pop while you decided. That model conflicts with predictive back gestures on Android 14+, where the system needs to know synchronously whether the route can be popped.
• PopScope splits the decision in two: `canPop` is a synchronous boolean; `onPopInvoked` is the async hook that fires AFTER the system has decided whether to pop.

Typical uses:
• Confirm before discarding unsaved form data
• Close a search/keyboard layer instead of the whole screen
• Submit analytics or save drafts on exit

Pattern:
• Set canPop = false while there's unsaved state
• In onPopInvoked, if didPop is false, show a confirmation; if user confirms, call Navigator.pop manually

Don't use it as a navigation stack manager — for that, use go_router or Navigator pages.

questions.question: How do Hero animations work, and what are the gotchas?
questions.answer:
A Hero is a widget shared between two routes that animates in flight when you push or pop. Both routes contain a Hero with the SAME `tag`. Flutter snapshots the source, removes both copies during the transition, paints the snapshot moving from source bounds to destination bounds, then drops it back into the destination.

Mechanics:
• Tag must match (a string, an int, or any value with stable equality)
• Flutter walks both routes' subtree, finds the matching tags, computes the source/destination rects, and animates
• The hero is REPARENTED into the overlay during transition — it's no longer in either page's tree

Gotchas you will hit:
• Two Heroes with the same tag on the SAME route → assertion error. Lists need unique tags (e.g. `Hero(tag: 'product-${product.id}', ...)`)
• A Hero whose child rebuilds during the flight may flicker — wrap heavy children in const or DCM-style stable widgets
• Hero with a different aspect ratio between source and destination needs a `flightShuttleBuilder` to avoid visual jumps
• Inside a SliverList, Heroes only fire when the source is visible — offscreen heroes can't snapshot and won't animate
• Modal sheets (showModalBottomSheet) by default disable hero animations because they use a different navigator unless you set `useRootNavigator: true`

Don't overuse: a Hero everywhere makes the app feel chaotic. Reserve it for thumbnails → detail images, avatars → profile pages, and other clear continuity hooks.

questions.question: How do you guard routes for authentication with go_router?
questions.answer:
go_router exposes `redirect` callbacks that run before a route is built. The router asks: given the current navigation target and current app state, where should the user actually go? Returning a path forwards the navigation; returning null lets the original target through.

Design:
• Drive the redirect from a Listenable (auth state). When auth changes, go_router re-evaluates the current location.
• Top-level redirect handles the global rule ("if not signed in, send to /login")
• Per-route redirect for finer control ("this admin section needs an admin user")
• Track the user's intended destination ("deep-linked into /cart") so you can return there after login

Gotcha:
• Don't put the redirect inside `builder` — that runs after the route is built; the redirect callback is the right hook
• Avoid redirect loops: always check that the current location isn't already the destination
• If your auth state is async (loading from secure storage), gate the whole router behind a splash route until you know

This pattern works equally well for paywalls, age gates, kid mode, and any rule that depends on app state.

questions.question: How do showDialog, showModalBottomSheet, and showMenu fit into the navigation stack?
questions.answer:
All three are convenience wrappers around Navigator.push that put a modal-style route on top of the current navigator. They look like overlays but they ARE routes — pop one and the stack underneath is exposed.

showDialog:
• Pushes a DialogRoute. Awaits its result (whatever you Navigator.pop with).
• `barrierDismissible: false` blocks taps outside; default true allows tap-to-dismiss
• `useRootNavigator: true` puts the dialog above the entire app, including any nested Navigators (bottom-nav tabs)

showModalBottomSheet:
• Pushes a ModalBottomSheetRoute
• `isScrollControlled: true` lets the sheet take any height (needed for keyboard-aware sheets)
• `useSafeArea: true` keeps content out of OS chrome
• `useRootNavigator: true` for the same reason as dialog — important when you have nested Navigators (ShellRoute, IndexedStack)
• `enableDrag: true/false` and `isDismissible` control gestures

showMenu (popup menu):
• Pushes a _MenuRoute pinned to a position
• Awaits the selected item; null if dismissed

Gotchas:
• Routes returned by these helpers participate in `PopScope` and `WillPopScope` like any other route
• Inside an async action, the BuildContext you used to call show* may unmount before the dialog returns — capture what you need (Navigator, Messenger) before await
• On the web/desktop, dialogs draw on the same window. There is no native window — full-screen modals are still routes
• The system back button pops the modal first, then the underlying screen — match user expectations

questions.question: How do you set up deep linking and universal links for a Flutter app?
questions.answer:
Deep links open a specific screen in your app from a URL. Three flavors with different reach:

1) Custom scheme (`myapp://product/42`):
• Cheapest to set up, works on iOS and Android
• Visible to the user as a non-standard URL; spams the share sheet on iOS
• Vulnerable to scheme hijacking — any app can claim the same scheme
• Rarely the right answer for production

2) Android App Links + iOS Universal Links (https URLs that open in your app when installed):
• Use real https URLs the user can share, paste into messages, browse from email
• Owners of the domain prove association via a hosted file:
  - iOS: `apple-app-site-association` JSON at `https://example.com/.well-known/apple-app-site-association`
  - Android: `assetlinks.json` at `https://example.com/.well-known/assetlinks.json`
• When the OS routes a tap on `https://example.com/product/42`, it checks the association file and opens the app if associated; otherwise the browser
• Cannot be hijacked

3) Web fallback:
• Same URL works in the browser via Flutter web or a server-side fallback page

Flutter wiring:
• `uni_links` or `app_links` package surfaces the cold-start URL and a stream of in-session URLs
• Wire those into go_router's `initialLocation` and a stream listener that calls `context.go(path)` on each new link
• On iOS you also need `NSUserActivityTypes` and the associated domain entitlement; on Android, intent-filter with autoVerify=true

QA: install/uninstall cycles matter — universal-link verification happens on install. Test with `xcrun simctl openurl` and `adb shell am start -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d https://example.com/product/42`.

questions.question: How do you build a custom page transition animation?
questions.answer:
Default routes use platform-appropriate transitions: MaterialPageRoute (slide-up on Android, slide-from-right on iOS), CupertinoPageRoute (always iOS-style). When you need something different — fade for splash → home, scale for a focused dialog-style page, custom physics — use PageRouteBuilder or extend PageRoute.

PageRouteBuilder lets you supply two builders:
• `pageBuilder(context, animation, secondaryAnimation)` — the destination widget
• `transitionsBuilder(context, animation, secondaryAnimation, child)` — wraps the child in transitions driven by `animation`

Animations:
• `animation` — drives this route's transition (0 → 1 on push, 1 → 0 on pop)
• `secondaryAnimation` — drives the previous route's transition out as this one comes in (and vice versa). Useful for "the previous screen scales down as the new one comes in"
• Both are clamped 0..1; wrap with `CurveTween`/`Tween` for non-linear animations
• `transitionDuration` and `reverseTransitionDuration` set the timings

With go_router:
• Use `CustomTransitionPage` from go_router — same shape as PageRouteBuilder, but plugs into the declarative router
• Per-route transition lets you keep the default elsewhere and customize only specific screens

Keep it tasteful: complex custom transitions on every screen feel slow. Reach for them when there's a meaningful continuity between screens (fade for splash, hero for image-detail), not for novelty.

questions.question: How does setState work and what are its performance implications?
questions.answer:
setState() marks the State as dirty and schedules rebuild of build() for the next frame.

Performance: setState() rebuilds the ENTIRE subtree under the widget calling it. This is wasteful if only a small portion of the UI changed.

Optimization strategies:
• Extract unchanged subtrees into separate widgets (especially const widgets)
• Keep stateful widgets small and focused — only wrap what actually changes
• Use const constructors on children — Flutter skips unchanged const subtrees
• Check mounted before calling setState after async operations

questions.question: What is InheritedWidget and how does it work?
questions.answer:
InheritedWidget is the fundamental mechanism for efficient state propagation down the widget tree. It's the foundation for Provider, Riverpod, and Theme.of(context).

How it works:
1. InheritedWidget sits in the tree above widgets that need the data
2. Descendants call context.dependOnInheritedWidgetOfExactType<T>() to subscribe
3. When the InheritedWidget is rebuilt with new data, only subscribed descendants rebuild
4. updateShouldNotify() controls which changes trigger rebuilds

This is O(1) lookup — Flutter stores InheritedWidgets in a hash map per Element.

questions.question: When should you use StatelessWidget vs StatefulWidget?
questions.answer:
Default to StatelessWidget. Reach for StatefulWidget only when the widget owns data that changes over time and the rebuild is driven from inside the widget itself.

Use StatelessWidget when:
• Output depends only on constructor arguments and ambient data (Theme, Localization, Provider)
• The widget renders something derived from external state but does not mutate it
• You can express "props in, pixels out" with no lifecycle

Use StatefulWidget when:
• You need initState/dispose for controllers, subscriptions, animations
• Local UI state that does not belong in app state (form input, expansion, scroll offsets)
• You depend on lifecycle hooks (didChangeDependencies, didUpdateWidget)

Rule of thumb: if you reach for setState only to mirror a value that already lives in a parent or in app state — lift it up instead. Stateful widgets that just forward state are an anti-pattern.

questions.question: What is ValueNotifier and when should you prefer it over setState?
questions.answer:
ValueNotifier<T> is a tiny ChangeNotifier with a single value. Combined with ValueListenableBuilder, it rebuilds only the subtree that subscribes — not the whole widget — without bringing in a state-management library.

Prefer ValueNotifier over setState when:
• Only a small leaf in the tree depends on a value (e.g. an icon, a counter, a checkbox)
• You want the rest of the screen to stay const and skip rebuilds
• You need to share the value sideways without rewriting parent widgets

Limitations:
• Equality is by ==. Mutating a List/Map in place will not notify — assign a new instance
• No transactional updates — for cross-field consistency use ChangeNotifier or a real store
• Don't forget to dispose() it (it is a Listenable)

questions.question: What does it mean to "lift state up" in Flutter?
questions.answer:
Lifting state up means moving a piece of state to the lowest common ancestor of the widgets that read or write it, then passing it down via constructor parameters and passing change callbacks back up.

Why it matters:
• Two siblings cannot share state directly — they must share through a common parent
• Avoids drift between mirror copies (the classic "_local = widget.value" bug)
• Keeps the source of truth in one place and predictable

How far up to lift:
• To the nearest ancestor that needs it — not higher
• If the state is needed across the app, you lift it all the way to a store/Provider/Riverpod root
• If you find yourself prop-drilling through 4+ layers, that's the signal to use an InheritedWidget-based solution

questions.question: What is the mounted check and why is it required after an async gap?
questions.answer:
Across an `await`, the widget owning the State may have been disposed. Calling setState() on a disposed State throws and leaks the BuildContext. The State.mounted flag tells you whether the State is still attached to the tree.

Rules:
• After every await in a State method, check `if (!mounted) return;` before touching state or BuildContext
• Never store BuildContext across awaits — capture what you need before the gap (e.g. ScaffoldMessenger.of(context))
• Cancel timers, subscriptions, and pending requests in dispose() so callbacks never fire on a dead State

Analyzer flag: enable `use_build_context_synchronously` lint — it catches the most common offenders automatically.

questions.question: What is the difference between initState and didChangeDependencies?
questions.answer:
Both run before the first build, but they answer different questions.

initState:
• Runs exactly once when the State is inserted into the tree
• You CANNOT call inheritedWidget lookups (Theme.of, MediaQuery.of) here — they require the element to be linked
• Right place for: creating controllers, AnimationControllers, ValueNotifiers, kicking off one-shot Futures

didChangeDependencies:
• Runs immediately after initState AND every time an inherited dependency changes (theme switched, locale changed, parent Provider rebuilt)
• Right place for: re-subscribing to a stream from Provider.of(context, listen: true), reacting to MediaQuery changes

didUpdateWidget is the third hook — runs when the parent rebuilds with a new widget instance of the same type. Use it to react to constructor arg changes.

questions.question: What is InheritedNotifier and when does it beat a plain InheritedWidget?
questions.answer:
InheritedNotifier<T extends Listenable> is a built-in InheritedWidget that wraps a Listenable (typically ChangeNotifier or ValueNotifier). When the listenable notifies, dependents rebuild — without you having to swap the entire InheritedWidget instance.

Why reach for it:
• You hold mutable state in a ChangeNotifier and want to expose it to descendants
• You don't want the boilerplate of updateShouldNotify and rebuilding the parent every time the state changes
• You're building a tiny in-house alternative to Provider for a contained feature

When NOT to:
• Cross-cutting app state that lives outside the widget tree — pick Provider/Riverpod
• You need .select-style fine-grained subscriptions — InheritedNotifier rebuilds all dependents on every notify

questions.question: What problem do widget Keys solve, and when do you need GlobalKey vs ValueKey?
questions.answer:
Keys preserve State across rebuilds when widgets get reordered or swapped. Without keys, Flutter matches widgets to existing Elements by RUNTIME TYPE and POSITION — if you reorder a list of identical-type widgets, the State objects stay in their old positions and the data attached to them ends up on the wrong widget.

Key types:
• ValueKey<T> — match by a value (an id, a name). Use for items in a reorderable list, swap-target switches, AnimatedList.
• ObjectKey — match by identity of an object (===). Useful when items don't have a stable id but you do have a stable object reference.
• UniqueKey — never matches anything — forces a fresh element. Use to deliberately reset state (e.g. force a new AnimatedSwitcher transition).
• GlobalKey — uniquely identifies a widget across the entire tree. Lets you read its State or BuildContext from outside. EXPENSIVE — moves the element when re-parented; use sparingly. Common cases: Form, Scaffold (legacy), measuring a render box.

Rule: don't reach for GlobalKey just to call methods on a widget — pass callbacks down instead. GlobalKey is for cross-tree access or for state that must survive re-parenting.

questions.question: Explain Future, async/await, and error handling in Dart.
questions.answer:
A Future<T> represents a value available at some point. Like a Promise in JS.

States: uncompleted → completed with value | completed with error

async/await is syntactic sugar:
• async marks a function — it returns a Future implicitly
• await pauses execution until the Future completes
• try/catch for error handling; finally always runs

Dart event loop: single-threaded with microtask queue (high priority, Futures) and event queue (I/O, timers). await yields to the event loop.

questions.question: What is a Completer in Dart and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
A Completer<T> creates a Future that you can complete manually — useful when you need to bridge callback-based APIs with Futures.

Without Completer, async/await handles most cases. Use Completer when:
• Converting callback APIs to Futures
• Creating a Future whose completion is controlled externally
• Implementing one-time events that multiple awaiters can subscribe to

Completer has:
• .future — the Future to await
• .complete(value) — resolve with a value
• .completeError(error) — resolve with an error
• .isCompleted — check if already completed

questions.question: How do you handle multiple concurrent async operations?
questions.answer:
Multiple approaches for concurrent async operations:

1. Future.wait() — wait for all to complete
2. Future.any() — wait for first to complete
3. Stream.merge() — combine multiple streams
4. Parallel isolates — true parallelism

Error handling:
• Future.wait() fails fast by default
• Use eagerError: false to wait for all
• Catch individual futures before combining

questions.question: Explain Dart's event loop, microtask queue, and event queue.
questions.answer:
Dart is single-threaded per isolate. Concurrency comes from an event loop that drains two queues, in this priority order:

1) Microtask queue — high priority, drained completely before any event-queue work
   - Populated by: `scheduleMicrotask`, `Future.microtask`, the resolution of synchronous Futures, `Future.value(x)` (often), DOM-like callbacks on web
   - Use sparingly — too many microtasks starve I/O and animations

2) Event queue — lower priority
   - Populated by: `Future.delayed`, Timer ticks, network I/O, file I/O, platform messages, user input
   - One event runs to completion, then microtasks drain, then the next event runs

What `await` actually does:
• Pauses the async function
• Schedules the continuation as a microtask after the awaited Future completes
• Yields control back to the event loop, which can drain other microtasks before resuming

Practical implications:
• `await Future.value(42)` does NOT block — it returns 42 "after" letting any pending microtasks run
• `Future.delayed(Duration.zero)` is the canonical way to yield to the event queue (lower priority than microtasks)
• Heavy synchronous work between awaits still blocks the UI; the event loop can't preempt synchronous code
• For real parallelism (image processing, big sorts), spawn an isolate — the event loop on the main isolate stays free

questions.question: How do timeouts and retries work for Futures?
questions.answer:
Robust async code accepts that things take too long or fail transiently. Two simple primitives cover most needs:

Timeout — `future.timeout(duration, onTimeout: ...)`:
• Returns a new Future that completes with a TimeoutException if the source doesn't complete in time
• `onTimeout` lets you supply a fallback value instead of throwing
• The original work is NOT cancelled — Dart Futures aren't cancellable. The timeout just stops waiting.

Retries:
• No built-in helper, but a small loop with exponential backoff is the right shape
• Cap retries (typically 3–5) and the backoff (e.g. 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s with jitter)
• Retry only on RETRYABLE errors — TimeoutException, network errors, 5xx responses. NEVER on 4xx — those are permanent.
• Add jitter to avoid thundering herds when many clients retry simultaneously

For production-grade behavior, use the `retry` package (`retry: ^3.x`) or write a small wrapper. Combine with timeout to bound total time.

Key gotcha: chaining `.timeout()` on a Future you're awaiting only times out THAT awaiter. If the underlying network operation can't be cancelled (e.g. a Dio request), the request continues in the background; if it eventually completes after timeout, its result is dropped (or its error becomes an unhandled error). Use a real cancellation mechanism (Dio's CancelToken) when you need the work itself to stop.

questions.question: What is fire-and-forget and when is it safe to use unawaited()?
questions.answer:
A "fire-and-forget" Future is one whose result you don't await — analytics events, log writes, prefetches in the background. The Dart analyzer warns about this with `unawaited_futures` because forgetting to await a Future is a common bug source. `unawaited(future)` is the explicit "yes, I really mean to drop this" annotation.

What you give up by not awaiting:
• Errors thrown by the Future become "unhandled async errors" — they disappear or, worse, crash the app on Flutter
• You no longer know when (or whether) the work finished
• You can't compose downstream actions on the result

Guidelines:
• Always attach a `.catchError` to a fire-and-forget call, OR wrap it so errors are captured by your global handler
• Don't fire-and-forget anything user-facing (the user can't tell whether the action succeeded)
• Don't fire-and-forget side effects with ordering (e.g., "log A then B") — order is not guaranteed
• In a State, ALWAYS check `mounted` before touching state in the eventual callback

Safe candidates: analytics, logging, cache warm-up, telemetry, optional prefetches. Unsafe: writes the user expects to persist, navigation, error-recovery work that depends on the result.

questions.question: How do .then(), .catchError(), and chaining compare to async/await?
questions.answer:
Both styles produce the same Future. They differ in readability and a few tricky semantics.

.then() / .catchError():
• Each call chains a continuation. `.then` runs on success; `.catchError` runs on error
• `.whenComplete(fn)` runs on either, like try/finally
• Prone to deeply nested chains for sequential operations
• Useful when you don't want to mark a function async (rare)

async/await:
• Linear, top-to-bottom code; try/catch reads naturally
• Stack traces are usually clearer (the original throw site is preserved)
• Wraps the function's return in a Future automatically; you can't accidentally forget to return it
• Almost always the right default in modern Dart

Semantics that catch people:
• `.catchError` only catches errors from the SOURCE Future plus any earlier `.then` callbacks. A NEW `.then` after `.catchError` runs on success of the recovered chain.
• `.catchError` returns the same generic type — you must return a value of type T inside it; otherwise the chain may break
• `await` rethrows; `.catchError` swallows unless you rethrow
• Mixing styles is fine but easy to get confused — pick one inside a function

If you find yourself building a long chain of `.then(.then(.then`, rewrite the function as async/await. There is essentially no performance difference.

questions.question: What is FutureBuilder and what are its common pitfalls?
questions.answer:
FutureBuilder<T> rebuilds itself based on the state of a Future. It's the "hello, async" widget of Flutter — and the source of more bad code than almost any other.

It does:
• Listens to the supplied Future, rebuilds via `connectionState` and `data`/`error`
• Plays nicely with build()-driven UI for one-shot async data
• Initial data via `initialData` parameter

What it doesn't do:
• Cache. EVERY rebuild that creates a new Future restarts the whole load. If the parent rebuilds for any reason, FutureBuilder re-fetches.
• Cancel. There's no way to cancel the underlying work; if your parent rebuilds before completion, the old Future still runs.
• Manage lifetime. The Future lives in the surrounding State.

Common pitfalls:
• Calling the API directly inside `build()` — every rebuild kicks off a new call. Always store the Future in a `late final` field of a StatefulWidget or in a Notifier.
• Forgetting initialData and seeing the loading flicker every time the parent rebuilds
• Not handling all three states (waiting / done with data / done with error) — error and "done with null" are different
• Treating it like a state-management tool. For complex flows use Riverpod's AsyncNotifier, BLoC, or a Notifier — they handle caching, cancellation, and refresh.

In 2025-era code, AsyncNotifier or a focused Notifier+state is usually a better choice than FutureBuilder beyond the most trivial fetch-once-and-show pattern.

questions.question: How do you serialize concurrent access to a shared resource in Dart?
questions.answer:
Even though Dart is single-threaded per isolate, async functions interleave at every `await`. Two methods that look like they touch the same resource sequentially can actually be running in alternation, causing classic race conditions: lost writes, double-fires, inconsistent state.

Typical races:
• Auth refresh: two requests get 401, both call /refresh, both write back tokens
• File write: two saves to the same file race; the OS sees interleaved writes
• Cache fill: two callers ask for the same key while empty, both call the network, both fill the cache (wasted work)

Techniques:

1) Single-flight pattern — share an in-flight Future:
• Keep a `Future<T>?` field. If it's non-null, return it. Otherwise start the work and store it. Clear when done.
• Trivial to implement; covers the auth-refresh / cache-fill cases

2) Mutex / Lock — `package:synchronized`:
• `Lock` provides `synchronized<T>(action)` — guarantees that only one action runs at a time
• Use when you need actual serial ordering (e.g. database write transactions)

3) Queue / drain — collect requests, process one at a time:
• A small Stream + listen pattern; useful for ordered side effects ("send these analytics events one after another")

4) Optimistic concurrency — version your state:
• Read with a revision; write only if revision matches. Useful in distributed scenarios.

Don't reach for isolates here. Isolates solve a different problem (CPU work). For event-loop interleaving, an in-process lock is the right tool.

questions.question: What are Streams in Dart? Explain single-subscription vs broadcast streams.
questions.answer:
A Stream<T> delivers a sequence of async events over time. Like an async iterator.

Single-subscription (default):
• Only ONE listener at a time
• Can pause/resume the producer
• Use for: file reading, HTTP body, socket

Broadcast:
• MULTIPLE simultaneous listeners
• Cannot pause producer
• Use for: user input events, pub/sub, ChangeNotifier-style

StreamController — creates and exposes a stream you can push events into.
async* generator — simplest way to create streams with yield.

questions.question: How do you use StreamController and explain stream transformations?
questions.answer:
StreamController gives you a stream + a sink to push events into.

Transformations (all return a new Stream):
• map() — transform each event
• where() — filter events
• take(n) / skip(n) — limit or skip events
• distinct() — skip duplicate consecutive events
• debounceTime() — wait for silence (rxdart)
• throttle() — rate limit
• switchMap() — cancel previous inner stream when new event arrives
• asyncMap() — map with async operations
• expand() — one-to-many transform
• scan() — accumulate (like fold but streaming)

questions.question: What is StreamSubscription and how do you manage its lifecycle correctly?
questions.answer:
Calling `stream.listen(...)` returns a StreamSubscription. It is the handle to the active subscription — keep it, or you can't stop, pause, or cancel.

Methods:
• `cancel()` — stop receiving events; for single-subscription streams, frees the producer too
• `pause([Future resumeSignal])` — pause delivery; events buffer or backpressure depending on the source
• `resume()` — resume delivery
• `onData / onError / onDone` — replace the handlers post-construction

Lifecycle rules:
• If the State that owns the subscription is unmounted, the subscription must be cancelled in dispose() — otherwise the callback fires on a dead state and crashes (or, worse, leaks objects via a closure over `this`)
• `cancel()` is idempotent and returns Future<void> — wait for it if you need to be sure cleanup ran
• On a single-subscription stream, cancel propagates to the producer (e.g. async* generator stops at its current yield, file handle closes)
• On a broadcast stream, your cancel only removes you — other subscribers and the producer continue

A common bug: using `stream.listen(onData)` and forgetting the return value — you can't cancel later. Always assign to a field if the subscription needs to outlive the listen call.

questions.question: When do you use `await for` vs `listen` on a stream?
questions.answer:
Two ways to consume a stream — both delegate to the same subscription mechanism, but they shape your code very differently.

`await for (final x in stream) { ... }`:
• Sequential — the loop body MUST complete before the next event is delivered
• Backpressure — if your loop body is slow, the producer is paused (single-subscription) or events buffer (broadcast)
• Easy to read; reads like a for-loop
• Cannot easily attach error handlers other than try/catch around the loop
• Use when the consumer needs to do bounded work per event in order

`stream.listen(onData)`:
• Reactive — onData fires per event; your `listen` returns immediately
• Events arrive even if the previous handler hasn't finished — you handle concurrency yourself
• Lets you pass `onError`, `onDone`, `cancelOnError`
• Must keep the subscription handle for cancel/pause
• Use when you want fine-grained control or async handlers that should run concurrently

Gotchas:
• `await for` on a broadcast stream is fine but you can't easily share that loop with another consumer — listen is more flexible for fan-out
• `await for` cannot be cancelled from outside — break out of the loop on a sentinel event
• Inside an async* function, `await for` paired with `yield` lets you transform a stream cleanly

questions.question: How do you handle errors in a Stream pipeline?
questions.answer:
Errors on a stream are first-class events delivered through the error channel — they don't throw at the listen call site. The handler chain offers several places to react.

Where errors land:
• `stream.listen(onData, onError: ...)` — handler receives (Object error, StackTrace stack)
• `stream.handleError((e, s) {...})` — pipeline operator that catches and either swallows or rethrows
• `await for` — wrap in try/catch; if cancelOnError is true (default), the iteration stops on first error
• `transformer` — convert errors into typed events (e.g. wrap into `Result.err`)

Cancel-on-error:
• Default for `await for` and `Stream.first`
• Default for `stream.listen` is `cancelOnError: false` — loop continues; pass true if you want it to stop on first error

Design tips:
• For a Bloc/Riverpod pipeline, transform errors INTO state. Don't let the stream simply close on a transient network blip — emit `Failed(e)` and let the next user action retry.
• Use `handleError` to scope which errors the rest of the pipeline sees. Common pattern: `handleError((e) { if (e is FormatException) ...; else throw e; })`
• Don't `print` — emit to your logger and decide whether to rethrow

Gotcha: a thrown synchronous error inside an async* function becomes an error event on the resulting stream. If your generator throws inside a loop, surrounding code that does `listen(onError: ...)` catches it; surrounding code that does `await for` catches it via try/catch.

questions.question: What does rxdart bring on top of dart:async — BehaviorSubject, switchMap, debounce?
questions.answer:
rxdart wraps dart:async with the Rx-style operators that are standard in other reactive libraries (RxJava, RxJS). The core stream model is the same — rxdart's classes are still Streams — but it gives you operators that are tedious to hand-roll.

BehaviorSubject<T>:
• Like a broadcast StreamController that always replays the LAST value to a new listener
• Useful for state-like streams: a UI that subscribes mid-flow still sees the current value
• Combined with `.value` getter for synchronous reads

ReplaySubject<T>:
• Replays the last N (or all) values to new listeners
• Useful for event logs and stateful caches

Key operators not in dart:async:
• `debounceTime` — emit only after a period of silence (great for search input)
• `throttleTime` — emit at most once per period
• `switchMap` — when a new event arrives, cancel the in-flight inner stream from the previous event (search-as-you-type cancellation pattern)
• `combineLatest` / `combineLatest2`...— combine the latest values from multiple streams into one
• `startWith(value)` — prepend an initial value
• `withLatestFrom` — pair each event with the latest from another stream
• `bufferTime` / `windowTime` — group events by time window

When NOT to add rxdart:
• You're using Riverpod and most operators have equivalents in providers + Notifiers — extra dependency may not be worth it
• Single-screen, tiny pipeline — handcraft the missing operator with async*

Gotcha: BehaviorSubject is broadcast — multiple listeners share the same subscription. Closing the subject is on you.

questions.question: How do you combine multiple streams — merge, zip, combineLatest?
questions.answer:
Three combinators cover most multi-stream patterns. Same names show up in RxDart, RxJS, and dart:async helpers.

merge:
• Forward EVERY event from EVERY input stream, in arrival order
• Use when: "show any error from any source" / "collect events from multiple chats"
• `Rx.merge([a, b, c])` (rxdart), or `StreamGroup.merge` (dart:async)

zip:
• Pair the i-th event of each input. Output is `[a₀, b₀, c₀]`, then `[a₁, b₁, c₁]`, ...
• If one input runs out, output stops — slowest input is the bottleneck
• Use when: "render output i = f(streamA[i], streamB[i])"
• `Rx.zip2(a, b, (x, y) => ...)` etc.

combineLatest:
• Whenever ANY input emits, emit a tuple of the LATEST value from each input
• Output starts only when EVERY input has emitted at least once
• Use when: "derived state — recompute when any input changes"
• `Rx.combineLatest2/3/...`

Other useful combinators (rxdart):
• `concat([a, b, c])` — drain a, then b, then c (sequential)
• `withLatestFrom(other)` — emit when source emits, paired with other's latest
• `startWith(value)` — prepend a seed value

For Riverpod users: most of this can be expressed with derived providers (each `ref.watch` is a combineLatest under the hood). For BLoC, rxdart's combinators are the standard tool.

questions.question: What is backpressure and how do single-subscription vs broadcast streams handle it?
questions.answer:
Backpressure is the question "what happens when the producer outpaces the consumer?". Streams behave very differently depending on type and consumption mode.

Single-subscription stream:
• Producer is paused when the consumer pauses or is slow inside `await for`
• `pause(Future)` on the subscription tells the producer to stop until the future completes
• Built-in backpressure — the producer's `controller.add(...)` blocks (or buffers, depending on controller config) when paused
• Examples that respect this naturally: HTTP body streams, file IO, async generators that `await` between yields

Broadcast stream:
• NO backpressure — the producer doesn't know about specific listeners and can't pause itself
• Each listener sees every event when it's emitted; if the listener can't keep up, events queue inside the engine and memory grows
• Use this for fast, small events (UI events, pubsub messages) — never for high-volume data streams

When you have a fast producer and slow consumer:
• Convert the source to a single-subscription stream (don't use broadcast just for convenience)
• Buffer or window — `bufferTime`, `bufferCount` — collapse N events into one batch your consumer can process
• Sample / throttle — drop intermediate events you can ignore (cursor positions, sensor data)
• Sink-based push with a bounded queue — apply your own quota and drop or coalesce when full

Common mistake: using a `StreamController.broadcast()` for an event log that fans out to dozens of subscribers, while one subscriber writes to disk synchronously. Memory grows during disk slow-downs because broadcast has no backpressure. The fix is either (a) a faster consumer, (b) a per-listener bounded queue, or (c) a different fan-out mechanism (a database with notifications).

questions.question: How do you decide between Stream and Future for an API?
questions.answer:
Future = ONE event at SOME point. Stream = ZERO-or-more events over time. The choice shapes everything from your error handling to your testing.

Return a Future when:
• The operation has a single result — fetch a user, post a form, read a file
• The caller wants to compose with `await`
• Errors are exceptional (a thrown error stops the awaited expression)

Return a Stream when:
• The operation produces multiple values over time — websocket messages, sensor readings, paginated results yielded as you fetch them
• The caller wants to react incrementally — partial UI rendering, progress indicators
• Backpressure or pause/resume matters — file streaming, video frames

Paginated APIs are a great test:
• Fetch one page → return Future<Page<T>> (caller calls again for the next page; explicit)
• Iterate ALL items → return Stream<T> (consumer can `await for` and stop when satisfied; built-in backpressure)
• Both can be useful in the same codebase; choose by the consumer's needs

When in doubt, prefer Future. Streams are more powerful but more complex — you commit your callers to subscription management. Streams that emit exactly one event are usually a sign you should have returned a Future.

Mixing the two:
• Future.asStream() — promote a Future into a single-event stream (rarely needed)
• Stream.first / Stream.last — convert a stream's first/last event into a Future
• Stream.toList() — drain the whole stream into a Future<List<T>> (only when you know it's bounded)

questions.question: What are Isolates in Dart and when should you use them?
questions.answer:
Dart is single-threaded, but Isolates provide TRUE parallelism. Each isolate has its own memory heap and event loop — no shared state, no race conditions. Communication is via message passing (SendPort/ReceivePort).

Use isolates for heavy CPU work that would block the UI thread:
• Parsing large JSON (>1MB)
• Image processing / resizing
• Cryptography / hashing
• Complex algorithms

APIs (simplest to most powerful):
• Isolate.run() (Dart 2.19+) — one-shot, simplest
• compute() (Flutter) — wraps Isolate.run, for functions
• Isolate.spawn() — full control, for long-lived isolates

questions.question: How do generics and type bounds work in Dart?
questions.answer:
Generics let a class or function declare placeholder types that the caller fills in. Dart generics are reified — at runtime you can still ask `value is List<int>` and the answer is correct (unlike Java's erasure).

Key points:
• Type parameters: `class Box<T> { T value; }` — `T` is filled in by the caller
• Bounds: `class SortedBox<T extends Comparable<T>>` — restricts T to types that implement Comparable
• Generic methods: `T first<T>(List<T> xs) => xs[0];` — type is inferred from the argument
• Use `extends` for upper bounds. Dart has no lower bounds.
• Variance: Dart collections are covariant by default — `List<int>` is assignable to `List<num>`. This is unsound but ergonomic; the analyzer catches the worst cases.

questions.question: What are extension methods and when should you use them?
questions.answer:
Extensions add methods, getters, setters, and operators to existing types — including types you don't own (String, DateTime, List, third-party classes). They're resolved statically at compile time, so they cost nothing at runtime.

Good uses:
• Domain-specific helpers on primitives (e.g. `String.toSlug()`, `DateTime.isToday`)
• Adapter sugar around third-party APIs you can't change
• Replacing scattered top-level utility functions with discoverable methods

Limitations:
• Cannot add fields (only methods and computed getters/setters)
• Cannot override existing methods — original wins
• Resolved by static type, not runtime type — `(x as Object).extMethod()` won't see the extension

questions.question: What are mixins and how do they differ from inheritance and interfaces?
questions.answer:
A mixin is a reusable bundle of methods you fold into a class without single-inheritance constraints. A class can mix in many mixins; it can extend only one class.

Mechanics:
• `mixin Logger { void log(String s) => print(s); }` — declared as a mixin, can never be instantiated
• `class Service with Logger {}` — Service gets log() as if it were defined on it
• `mixin Counter on ChangeNotifier` — `on` requires the host class to extend ChangeNotifier so the mixin can call notifyListeners()
• Method resolution is linearized: in `class C extends B with M1, M2`, M2's method overrides M1's, which overrides B's

Mixin vs interface vs inheritance:
• Inheritance — "is-a" relationship, single parent, shares state
• Interface (`implements`) — contract only, no implementation reuse
• Mixin (`with`) — reuses implementation across an unrelated tree, multiple at a time, no "is-a" claim

questions.question: What are async* and sync* generators and when should you use them?
questions.answer:
Generators produce sequences lazily. Dart has two flavors:

sync* — returns Iterable<T>:
• Yields values synchronously, on demand
• Use for in-memory pull-based sequences (paginate, traverse a tree, build combinations)
• `yield` adds one value, `yield*` delegates to another iterable

async* — returns Stream<T>:
• Yields values asynchronously over time
• Use for streams you build by awaiting other futures (websocket fan-out, paginated API loop, file reading)
• Cancellation: when the stream subscription is cancelled, the function is paused at its current yield. Important to release resources in `try { ... } finally { ... }`.

When NOT to use:
• If you have everything in memory already — return a List or a plain Stream.fromIterable instead
• If your stream is single-event — return a Future, not an async*

questions.question: What are enhanced enums (Dart 2.17+) and what new tricks do they enable?
questions.answer:
Since Dart 2.17 enums can have fields, constructors, getters, methods, and even implement interfaces. Same compile-time guarantees as classic enums (exhaustiveness, identity), but they replace most cases where people used to define a sealed class hierarchy by hand.

What you get:
• Constant constructors with named/positional args — encode metadata next to the value
• Methods and getters — push behavior onto the enum itself instead of switch helpers
• Implements — enums can implement an interface (but cannot extend a class or be `with`-mixed except for specific Object hierarchies)
• values getter and `name` for free

When to prefer over a sealed class:
• Fixed, finite set of named cases with similar shape
• You want exhaustive switches and constants
• Your cases don't carry per-instance variable data — the whole point is constant identity

questions.question: What are Records and Pattern matching (Dart 3.0+) and what problems do they solve?
questions.answer:
Dart 3 added two things that work hand-in-hand:

Records — anonymous, immutable, structurally-typed tuples:
• `(int, String)` is a positional record; `({int id, String name})` is named
• Created with parentheses: `final r = (1, 'a');`
• Accessed with `.\$1, .\$2` for positional, `.id, .name` for named
• Equality is structural — two records with the same fields are ==
• Replaces ad-hoc Map<String, dynamic> returns and unnamed Pair<A, B> classes

Patterns — destructure in `if`, `switch`, `var`, function args:
• `if (response case (200, var body)) { ... }`
• `switch (shape) { case Circle(:final r) => ...; case Square(:final side) => ... }`
• Refutable in switch (forces exhaustiveness on sealed types), irrefutable in var

Together they make multi-value returns and case analysis safe and concise.

questions.question: What are sealed classes and how do they enable exhaustive switches?
questions.answer:
A `sealed class` is an abstract class that the compiler knows the COMPLETE list of subtypes for, because all subtypes must be declared in the same library. That closure unlocks exhaustiveness checking in switch expressions: omit a case and you get a compile error, not a runtime surprise.

Why it matters for state modeling:
• Replaces the classic `if (x is Loading) ... else if (x is Loaded) ...` chains that silently break when you add a new case
• Fits perfectly with patterns — destructure each subtype in its own arm
• Together with records you get a discriminated-union style without a third-party library

Rules:
• `sealed` types cannot be instantiated directly — only their concrete subtypes
• Subtypes must live in the SAME library file as the sealed parent (or in part-of files)
• Mixing `sealed` with `final`/`base` controls how external libraries can implement or extend your hierarchy

questions.question: What is dart:ffi and when should you reach for it instead of platform channels?
questions.answer:
dart:ffi (Foreign Function Interface) lets Dart call into native C ABI directly — no MethodChannel, no MainThread hop, no JSON serialization. It's the same mechanism used by sqlite3, image, crypto, and many performance-sensitive plugins.

FFI vs MethodChannel:
• Latency — FFI is a direct C call (sub-microsecond). MethodChannel hops to the platform thread and serializes the message (~ms).
• Threading — FFI can run on any isolate / any thread. MethodChannel is async and platform-thread-bound.
• API surface — FFI talks to C symbols. MethodChannel talks to Java/Kotlin/Swift/ObjC. If you need Android Activity APIs, MethodChannel still wins.
• Memory — FFI exposes raw pointers. You allocate, you free. There is no GC across the boundary.

When to use FFI:
• Wrapping a C/C++ library you ship as a binary (image codecs, ML, crypto, hashing)
• Hot-loop work on big buffers — avoid the per-message overhead
• Reusing existing native code with a stable C ABI

When NOT to:
• Calling platform-specific Kotlin/Swift APIs — those are not C; use MethodChannel or Pigeon
• One-off, infrequent calls — channels are simpler and safer

questions.question: Explain Provider: ChangeNotifier, Consumer, context.watch vs context.read vs context.select.
questions.answer:
Provider wraps InheritedWidget with a simpler API for DI and state management.

Core components:
• ChangeNotifier — mutable state class; call notifyListeners() to trigger rebuilds
• ChangeNotifierProvider — inserts ChangeNotifier into the tree
• Consumer<T> — rebuilds only its builder subtree when T notifies
• context.watch<T>() — subscribe; rebuilds the CURRENT widget when T notifies
• context.read<T>() — one-time read, NO subscription (for callbacks)
• context.select<T,R>() — subscribe to a specific slice; rebuilds only when that slice changes

Rule: read() in callbacks, watch()/Consumer in build().

questions.question: Explain ProxyProvider and how to combine providers.
questions.answer:
ProxyProvider creates a provider that depends on other providers. It rebuilds when its dependencies change.

Use cases:
• A service that needs a dependency injected at runtime
• A provider whose value is derived from another provider
• Repository that needs an authenticated HTTP client

ProxyProvider variants:
• ProxyProvider<A, T> — depends on 1 provider
• ProxyProvider2<A, B, T> — depends on 2 providers
• ChangeNotifierProxyProvider — produces a ChangeNotifier that depends on others

questions.question: Why pick Provider over Riverpod or vice versa?
questions.answer:
Provider and Riverpod are made by the same author. Riverpod is essentially "Provider 2.0" — it fixes inherent design issues that the original architecture couldn't fix without breaking changes. The right answer for a NEW project is almost always Riverpod, but Provider is still common in production codebases and there are real trade-offs.

Provider — pros:
• Smaller, simpler API surface
• Tightly tied to BuildContext — natural for Flutter veterans
• Easier on-ramp for the team that already knows InheritedWidget mental model
• Plenty of documentation and community examples

Provider — cons:
• Compile-time safety holes — `Provider.of<T>(context)` throws ProviderNotFoundException at RUNTIME if T isn't in the tree
• Reading providers outside widgets is awkward (you need a BuildContext)
• No built-in auto-dispose; lifecycle management is manual
• Harder to test cleanly because you need a widget tree to access state

Riverpod — pros:
• Compile-time safe — provider references are typed identifiers; no missing-provider runtime error
• No BuildContext needed — read providers from anywhere (services, isolates, tests)
• Auto-dispose, family modifier, AsyncNotifier, code generation
• Better testing story — just override providers

Riverpod — cons:
• More concepts to learn (StateNotifier vs Notifier vs AsyncNotifier; refs; family; autoDispose)
• Larger API surface
• Migrating from Provider isn't trivial in a big app

When Provider is still the right call:
• Existing app already on Provider — don't rewrite for the sake of it
• Tiny apps where the simpler model is enough
• Team familiar with Provider and not ready to switch

In most other cases, especially greenfield apps, Riverpod is the better default in 2025.

questions.question: What is MultiProvider and what are the lifecycle controls (lazy / dispose)?
questions.answer:
MultiProvider flattens nested Provider widgets into one declaration. Functionally identical to nesting; just easier to read.

Lifecycle controls every Provider exposes:

• `lazy: true` (default) — the value is created on first read. Useful if creation is expensive and not always needed.
• `lazy: false` — the value is created at the moment the Provider mounts. Use for warm-up work (analytics init), or to surface initialization errors immediately.
• `dispose: (context, value) {...}` — called when the Provider is removed from the tree. Mandatory cleanup site for non-ChangeNotifier resources.
• `create: (context) => Service()` — runs once for the Provider's lifetime; not on every rebuild
• `ChangeNotifierProvider` automatically calls `value.dispose()` for you. For plain Provider you must wire dispose by hand.

Nesting matters:
• Each Provider can `read` providers DECLARED EARLIER in the tree via the BuildContext passed to `create`/`update`
• Order in MultiProvider's `providers` list is the order in the tree
• ProxyProvider forms a dependency chain — Service B depends on A, place A first

Gotcha: a ChangeNotifierProvider of a service that also creates Streams or listens to other notifiers must clean those up in its own dispose() — only the notifier itself is disposed automatically. Failing to do so leaks the inner subscriptions.

questions.question: How do StreamProvider and FutureProvider work, and when should you use them?
questions.answer:
Provider's package ships convenience wrappers for streams and futures, so you don't have to wrap them in a ChangeNotifier yourself.

FutureProvider<T>:
• Subscribes to a Future and exposes its result via Provider
• Until the Future completes, the provider exposes `initialData` (or the previous value)
• Errors set the exposed value to the error and re-throw on read
• Good for: configuration loaded once at app start; data the rest of the tree treats as static

StreamProvider<T>:
• Subscribes to a Stream and exposes the latest event
• Updates the tree on each new event
• Good for: real-time data sources — websocket feeds, Firestore queries, sensor streams

When they're a great fit:
• You have a stream/future from a third-party library and want a single read site to consume it
