• Updates are coarse (Future emits once; Stream emits at human-perceivable intervals)
• You don't need fine-grained control over loading/error states beyond "show value or fallback"

When to reach for something heavier:
• You need explicit loading vs. error vs. data states with three different UIs — use a Notifier (ChangeNotifier) holding an enum/sealed state, or migrate to Riverpod's AsyncNotifier
• Multiple consumers expect different snapshot semantics — broadcast vs. replay
• You need cancellation or in-flight refresh

Gotcha: FutureProvider doesn't re-run the Future when the parent rebuilds. If you need to refresh, swap the Future via `ChangeNotifierProvider` controlling a token, or use `key: ValueKey(refreshToken)` to remount.

questions.question: How do you test a screen that depends on Provider?
questions.answer:
Provider tests come in two flavors:

Unit tests — test the ChangeNotifier in isolation:
• Construct the notifier with mock dependencies
• Call its methods, assert state
• No widget tree, no BuildContext, fastest tests

Widget tests — test the screen with mocked providers:
• Wrap the widget under test in `ChangeNotifierProvider.value` (or .factory)
• Provide a mock or fake notifier
• Pump and assert UI

Mocking strategies:
• `mocktail` / `mockito` to mock dependencies of the notifier
• A hand-rolled fake that extends the real class and overrides specific methods
• A FakeChangeNotifier that exposes setters for test setup

Gotchas:
• Don't use `Provider.of` in test setup with `listen: true` — it requires a parent provider widget. Use `ChangeNotifierProvider.value` to inject your fake.
• `pumpAndSettle` waits until the tree is idle — needed after triggering an async action
• If the notifier loads on construction (e.g., calls `load()` in its constructor), you may need to wait for that to settle before asserting initial UI

With MultiProvider screens, build the same MultiProvider in the test setup but with mocked services, so the dependency wiring stays realistic.

questions.question: What is ListenableProvider and when do you reach for it instead of ChangeNotifierProvider?
questions.answer:
Most code uses ChangeNotifierProvider because ChangeNotifier is the most common form of Listenable. ListenableProvider is the more general version: it provides ANY Listenable — not necessarily a ChangeNotifier — and it does NOT auto-dispose.

Use ListenableProvider when:
• Your state holder is something other than ChangeNotifier — e.g., AnimationController, ValueNotifier, or a custom Listenable
• You want manual control over disposal (you don't want the provider to call dispose() at all)
• Combining several listenables under one Provider (e.g., a `Listenable.merge([a, b])` for widgets that should rebuild on either)

Why not use ChangeNotifierProvider for everything:
• ChangeNotifierProvider auto-disposes — but auto-dispose may be wrong if your notifier is shared across screens or reused later
• Plain Listenable is lighter — no dispose() requirement on the listener side

ValueListenableBuilder vs Provider:
• If you have a ValueNotifier and one or two consumers, ValueListenableBuilder is simpler — no provider needed
• If many widgets need it across a subtree, ListenableProvider gives you DI + tree-walked access

A bigger pattern: prefer ChangeNotifierProvider when in doubt. Reach for ListenableProvider only when you have a specific reason — disposal control, non-ChangeNotifier source, or merged listenables.

questions.question: How do you avoid unnecessary rebuilds with context.select and Selector?
questions.answer:
Watching a notifier rebuilds the consumer EVERY time the notifier calls `notifyListeners()` — regardless of which field changed. For coarse state (a User with many fields, a Cart with line items), most rebuilds are wasted. Two tools narrow the subscription.

context.select<T, R>:
• `final name = context.select<UserProvider, String>((u) => u.name);`
• Rebuilds only when the SELECTED value (`name`) changes by ==
• Concise inline form; no extra widget
• Usable inside any build() that has the right context

Selector<T, R>:
• A widget; takes `selector` and `builder`
• Same logic as context.select, but you get a `child` parameter for static parts of the subtree
• Useful when the selected value is wrapped around an expensive subtree

Key rule for both: equality. Selectors compare the OLD selected value to the NEW with ==. If your selector returns a List or Map by reference (a brand-new instance each time), every rebuild looks "different" and the optimization is wasted.

Fixes:
• Return primitive values where possible (length, name, status)
• Override == on classes returned from selectors (or use freezed/equatable)
• For selecting a List, return its length or a tuple of stable values

If .select isn't enough — your notifier is too coarse. Split it.

questions.question: What problems with Provider does Riverpod aim to solve?
questions.answer:
Provider is a thin layer over InheritedWidget. That coupling produced several real-world annoyances that Riverpod was built to remove:

1) ProviderNotFoundException at runtime
• Provider.of<T>(context) only checks at runtime that T was put above context. Misroute the widget and you get a crash.
• Riverpod uses a global ProviderScope; each provider is a typed identifier; missing values are compile errors or explicit defaults.

2) BuildContext coupling
• To read a Provider you need a BuildContext. That's painful in services, tests, and isolates that don't have one.
• Riverpod's `Ref` and `ProviderContainer` give you a context-free way to read state.

3) No auto-dispose
• Provider doesn't dispose state when consumers go away. You manage lifetimes manually.
• Riverpod has `.autoDispose` — when no widget watches a provider, it's torn down automatically.

4) Combining providers is awkward
• ProxyProvider exists but is verbose for chains of dependencies and rebuilds.
• Riverpod just lets one provider `ref.watch` another — readable and reactive.

5) Async state is a second-class citizen
• Provider has FutureProvider/StreamProvider but no first-class loading/error/data states.
• Riverpod has `AsyncValue<T>` and `AsyncNotifier` — pattern-matchable, exhaustive, and consistent across all async operations.

6) Testing
• Provider tests need widget trees and runtime registration to swap implementations.
• Riverpod tests use `ProviderContainer` with `overrides` — no widget tree needed; pure-Dart unit tests work.

7) Family / parameterized providers
• Provider has no built-in way to parameterize a provider by argument (`getProduct(id: 42)`).
• Riverpod's `family` modifier handles this cleanly with proper caching and disposal per parameter.

When Provider is still fine: small or legacy apps; teams already comfortable with the simpler model. For new code in 2025, Riverpod is the better default.

questions.question: Explain BLoC pattern: Events, States, Bloc vs Cubit.
questions.answer:
BLoC (Business Logic Component) separates UI from business logic using events and states.

Cubit — simplified BLoC:
• No events — call methods directly to emit new states
• Less boilerplate, good for simple state

Bloc — full event-driven:
• Explicit Event classes → better auditing, logging, undo/redo
• on<Event>() handlers registered in constructor
• More testable, more explicit flow

Both:
• BlocBuilder — rebuild UI based on state
• BlocListener — side effects (navigation, snackbar)
• BlocConsumer — builder + listener combined
• MultiBlocProvider — provide multiple blocs

questions.question: What is BlocObserver and how do you use it for logging and analytics?
questions.answer:
BlocObserver is a global listener for all Bloc/Cubit events, state changes, transitions, and errors. Set it once in main() — it applies to every Bloc in your app.

Use cases:
• Logging all state transitions (debugging)
• Sending analytics events
• Crash reporting (Sentry, Firebase Crashlytics)
• Performance monitoring

override methods:
• onCreate — bloc created
• onEvent — event added (Bloc only)
• onChange — state changed
• onTransition — event → state (Bloc only)
• onError — error occurred
• onClose — bloc closed

questions.question: What are event transformers in BLoC and what do droppable / restartable / sequential / concurrent do?
questions.answer:
Event transformers control HOW events flowing into an `on<Event>` handler are processed. By default, events are processed CONCURRENTLY — multiple in-flight handlers can be running at once. For most flows that's wrong. The bloc_concurrency package ships four standard policies.

concurrent (default):
• Each event spawns its own async handler in parallel
• Latest emit can come from any of them — race conditions in user-facing flows
• Use only when handlers are truly independent and order doesn't matter

sequential:
• Process events one at a time, in arrival order — handler N waits for N-1 to complete
• Use for: write paths where ordering matters (cart updates, save sequences)

droppable:
• If a handler is currently in flight, DROP new events until it finishes
• Use for: "submit" buttons — extra taps shouldn't fire extra requests

restartable:
• When a new event arrives, CANCEL the in-flight handler and start fresh with the new event
• Use for: search-as-you-type, autocomplete, navigation cancellation. Equivalent to switchMap in Rx.

Debouncing on top:
• Combine with rxdart-style debounce: events.debounceTime(300ms).switchMap(mapper)
• bloc_concurrency lets you compose: e.g. pre-debounce events then route to restartable

Gotcha: emit() after a cancel raises StateError. With `restartable`, check `emit.isDone` before emitting in long chains so a cancelled handler doesn't try to emit on a closed emitter.

questions.question: What's the difference between BlocBuilder, BlocSelector, and BlocConsumer (plus buildWhen)?
questions.answer:
All three subscribe to a Bloc/Cubit; they differ in what triggers a rebuild and whether they can run side effects.

BlocBuilder<B, S>:
• Rebuilds the builder on every state change
• Optional `buildWhen: (prev, next) => bool` — gate rebuilds on a custom predicate (returns true to rebuild)
• Use for the common case: "render UI from current state"

BlocSelector<B, S, T>:
• Selects a derived value `T` from state and rebuilds only when `T` changes by ==
• Cleaner when the widget cares about one field of a big state
• Like Provider's Selector or Riverpod's `provider.select`

BlocConsumer<B, S>:
• Combines builder + listener in one widget
• `listener: (context, state) {...}` for side effects (navigation, snackbar) — does NOT cause rebuild
• `listenWhen` and `buildWhen` independently gate listener vs builder calls
• Use when both UI and side-effect logic depend on the same state

Why buildWhen matters:
• Reduces unnecessary rebuilds without splitting the bloc — sometimes coarse state is fine if you predicate the rebuild
• Default: rebuild on every state — wasteful for big states

Guideline: BlocSelector for one-field reads, BlocBuilder + buildWhen for multi-field logic, BlocConsumer when listener is involved. Don't put navigation in builder — that's a render-time side effect, the wrong place.

questions.question: How do BlocProvider, MultiBlocProvider, and BlocProvider.value differ?
questions.answer:
Three ways to inject blocs into the widget tree, each with a specific role.

BlocProvider<B>:
• `BlocProvider(create: (_) => B(), child: ...)`
• Owns the bloc's lifecycle — created when the provider mounts, closed when it unmounts
• Use for: a fresh bloc scoped to a screen or feature subtree

BlocProvider<B>.value:
• `BlocProvider.value(value: existingBloc, child: ...)`
• Reuses an existing bloc instance — does NOT close it on unmount
• Use for: passing a bloc to a child route (e.g., a modal sheet) that needs the same instance, navigating with an already-bootstrapped bloc

MultiBlocProvider:
• Flattens nested BlocProviders into a single declaration with a `providers: [...]` list
• Identical behavior to nesting; just nicer to read
• Order matters: providers earlier in the list are visible to providers after them via `context.read`

Lifecycle gotchas:
• A bloc created by BlocProvider is closed when the widget is unmounted. If you push a new screen with `Navigator.push`, the parent's BlocProvider is still mounted — but a child route pushed without inheriting the provider can't see it. Use `BlocProvider.value(value: context.read<B>(), child: NextScreen())` to forward.
• Don't share a bloc between two unrelated subtrees — each should own its lifecycle. If you really need shared state, that's app-level, not bloc-level.
• `lazy: false` on BlocProvider creates the bloc immediately, useful when the bloc kicks off bootstrap work in its constructor and you want it running before the first frame

For go_router: each `GoRoute` builds a fresh widget tree on navigation; wrap individual routes with their bloc providers, or use a `ShellRoute` with shared providers higher in the tree.

questions.question: How do you persist BLoC state across restarts with hydrated_bloc?
questions.answer:
Out of the box, blocs lose state when the app restarts. `hydrated_bloc` adds JSON serialization to local storage for any subset of your blocs — perfect for theme, language, draft text, last-used filters.

How it works:
• Extend HydratedBloc / HydratedCubit instead of Bloc / Cubit
• Implement `fromJson(Map<String, dynamic>)` and `toJson(State)` — return null from toJson to skip persistence for that state
• HydratedStorage is initialized once in main() with a directory (typically `getApplicationDocumentsDirectory`)
• The current state is automatically saved after every state change and rehydrated on next app launch

When to persist (and when not to):
• Persist: theme, language, onboarding status, draft text, last filter, last route, simple non-sensitive flags
• Don't persist: anything that could be stale and harmful (auth state should be derived from a fresh token, not a cached AuthSuccess), any sensitive data (use flutter_secure_storage instead), large data (use a real database)

Gotchas:
• Schema migrations: if the State shape changes, fromJson must handle old shapes or return null to fall back to initial. Bumping a version field inside the JSON is the easiest mitigation.
• Errors during fromJson are silent by default — log them; otherwise you'll silently regress to initial state and not understand why
• Test mode: HydratedStorage.storage = HiveDirectoryStorage(directory: ...) for tests; or override per-test

For app-wide preferences not tied to a bloc, plain SharedPreferences or flutter_secure_storage may be a simpler fit — hydrated_bloc shines when the data IS the bloc state.

questions.question: How do you test a Bloc with bloc_test?
questions.answer:
bloc_test ships a `blocTest` helper that scripts a bloc through events and asserts the resulting state stream. It's the standard way to unit-test bloc business logic with no widget tree.

Setup:
• `blocTest<B, S>(...)` — build the bloc, optionally seed initial state, fire events, assert states
• `act` — function that calls bloc.add or cubit method
• `expect` — list of states (or matchers) the bloc should emit
• `wait` — hold for async transformers (debounce, switchMap)
• `verify` — additional assertions after `expect`
• `setUp` / `tearDown` for shared mocks

Key patterns:
• Use `mocktail` to fake repositories. Stub `when(() => repo.fetch()).thenAnswer((_) async => [...])`
• For sealed-state hierarchies, use isA<T>() matchers to assert types without comparing every field
• Test order-sensitive events with sequential transformers — bloc_test waits for them deterministically
• Snap a sealed-state's full equality via `equatable` or `freezed` so `expect` can compare value-by-value

For ground truth on UI-level effects, complement with widget tests using BlocProvider.value over a fake bloc.

questions.question: How do you communicate between Blocs without coupling them?
questions.answer:
Blocs should not directly call each other — that creates a tight coupling that's hard to test and reason about. There are three accepted patterns to coordinate behavior.

1) Listen and react via BlocListener:
• In a widget, place a BlocListener<A, AState> that, on a specific transition, calls `bloc<B>().add(...)`
• Keeps both blocs unaware of each other; the WIDGET orchestrates them
• Best for UI-level coordination (auth changes → reset cart, language changes → reload feed)

2) Subscribe to another bloc's stream inside a bloc:
• A bloc can `subscribe()` to another bloc's `stream` in its constructor and add internal events as a result
• Tighter coupling than option 1, but no widget mediation needed
• Cancel the subscription in `close()` to avoid leaks

3) Shared repository / service:
• Both blocs depend on a common service (RepositoryProvider) that emits a stream of changes
• Each bloc reacts independently to the service's stream
• Cleanest decoupling — the blocs don't know each other exists
• Best for cross-feature state (user session, sync engine status, network connectivity)

Guideline: prefer (3) for app-wide signals, (1) for screen-level coordination, (2) only when (1) and (3) don't fit.

Never do: import bloc B from inside bloc A and call methods on it. That makes A untestable without B and creates a directed dependency you'll regret.

questions.question: How do you handle errors in a Bloc — try/catch, addError, and onError?
questions.answer:
Error handling in BLoC has three layers, each with its own purpose.

1) try/catch inside the handler:
• Standard Dart — the right place to translate a thrown exception into an emitted error state
• Lets you keep the bloc emitting valid states (`Failure(...)`) instead of letting an unhandled exception bubble up
• Use for: expected failures (network down, validation mismatch, 4xx responses)

2) addError(error, stackTrace):
• A method on Bloc/Cubit that records an error WITHOUT changing state
• Useful when you want to log/report the error but the user-visible state has already moved on
• Calls flow into the bloc's `onError` and BlocObserver.onError
• Use sparingly — when an exception is unexpected and you want telemetry but no UI change

3) Override onError in the Bloc / globally in BlocObserver:
• Bloc.onError — last-chance hook for THIS bloc
• Bloc.observer.onError — application-wide; great for crashlytics, sentry
• Always call super.onError so the chain continues

Design rules:
• Model expected failures as state. UI knows how to render `Failure(message)` — let try/catch translate exceptions into them.
• Reserve `addError` and BlocObserver.onError for unexpected exceptions and telemetry.
• Never let a handler throw silently. If it does, the bloc enters a broken state and subsequent emits may behave oddly.
• On hot reload during development, an unhandled error can wipe state in surprising ways — guard with try/catch even in early prototypes.

BlocObserver is also where you forward errors to Crashlytics/Sentry — set it up once in main and you have a single capture point for all bloc-level errors.

questions.question: How do you handle REST APIs and JSON serialization in Flutter?
questions.answer:
Steps:
1. Choose HTTP client: http (simple), Dio (interceptors, multipart, cancellation)
2. Make request and handle status codes + exceptions
3. Deserialize JSON → Dart models
4. Show loading/error/data in UI

Serialization approaches:
• Manual fromJson/toJson — fine for small projects
• json_serializable — code generation, type-safe (recommended)
• Freezed — immutable + copyWith + pattern matching (production grade)

Dio advantages over http: interceptors, request cancellation, form data, response transformers, base URL.

questions.question: How do you implement pagination and infinite scroll in Flutter?
questions.answer:
Pagination patterns:

1. Offset-based: ?page=2&limit=20 — simple, works everywhere
2. Cursor-based: ?after=cursor123 — better for real-time data, no skipped/duplicated items
3. Keyset: ?last_id=500 — efficient for large datasets

Flutter implementation:
• ScrollController — detect when user reaches the bottom
• ListView.builder with a loading indicator at the end
• Track state: items, isLoading, hasMore, error

questions.question: How do you implement request/response interceptors in Flutter?
questions.answer:
Interceptors allow you to:
• Add headers to all requests (auth tokens)
• Log requests/responses
• Handle errors globally
• Retry failed requests
• Transform data

Implementation:
• Dio package has built-in interceptors
• For http package, create wrapper class
• Can chain multiple interceptors

questions.question: How do you upload files (multipart/form-data) in Flutter?
questions.answer:
File uploads use multipart/form-data, which packages each field — including binary file content — into a single request body. Both `http` and `dio` support it; Dio's API is more ergonomic.

Key pieces:
• MultipartFile — wraps a file (path, bytes, or stream) plus filename and contentType
• FormData — collection of fields and multipart files
• `Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=...` is set automatically; do NOT set it yourself or you'll break the boundary

Sources for the file:
• `image_picker` for camera/gallery on mobile
• `file_picker` for any file type, including web (where you only get bytes, not paths)
• Streamed uploads (large files) — use MultipartFile.fromStream + setting filename

Progress:
• Dio's `onSendProgress: (sent, total) {...}` fires throughout the upload — wire it to a progress indicator
• For long-running uploads, use a CancelToken and let the user cancel

Server-side hooks:
• Multipart sends fields as form parts named `field`, files as `file` (or whatever you choose); make sure the server's parser matches your field names
• For upload-then-confirm patterns (S3 presigned URLs), there is no multipart at all — you PUT the bytes directly to the signed URL with `Content-Type` matching what was signed

questions.question: How do you cache HTTP responses in a Flutter app?
questions.answer:
Caching reduces network traffic, makes the app feel instant on repeat views, and enables limited offline use. Three common layers, each suitable for different cases.

1) HTTP cache headers (server-driven):
• Server sends `Cache-Control: max-age=N` and an `ETag`/`Last-Modified`
• Client sends `If-None-Match` next time; server returns 304 Not Modified if unchanged
• Built into the standard browser stack; on Flutter you need a package — `dio_cache_interceptor` is the canonical choice

2) Application-level cache:
• Store the parsed model in a local DB (sqflite, Hive, Isar)
• Strategy: stale-while-revalidate — return cached result immediately, refresh in the background, swap in new data
• Best when the data has natural expiry ("refresh products every 15 min") or you want offline-first

3) Image cache:
• `cached_network_image` adds a disk cache so images survive app restarts
• `precacheImage` warms decoded bitmaps in memory before the user navigates

Picking:
• REST GETs that change rarely → HTTP cache or app-level stale-while-revalidate
• Real-time data → don't cache; subscribe to a stream instead
• Authenticated responses → use private cache scopes; do not share between users

Gotcha: cache poisoning. Don't cache 4xx/5xx responses; don't cache responses that contain user-specific data with a public scope; key the cache by full URL + relevant query params + auth user.

questions.question: How do you connect to a WebSocket and handle reconnection?
questions.answer:
WebSockets give you a long-lived bidirectional channel — push from server, send from client, no polling. The two parts that matter in production are: structuring the lifecycle and reconnecting on failure.

Libraries:
• `web_socket_channel` — the official lightweight client; simple Stream-based API
• `socket_io_client` — for Socket.IO servers (which are NOT plain WebSocket)

Lifecycle:
• `WebSocketChannel.connect(uri)` opens the connection
• `channel.stream` is a Stream of incoming events; subscribe and handle messages
• `channel.sink.add(message)` sends; `sink.close()` closes
• Treat the channel like a single-subscription stream — wrap in your own service if you need fan-out

Reconnection:
• WebSockets DO break — flaky networks, server restarts, idle proxies. Always plan for reconnect.
• Pattern: state machine `Disconnected → Connecting → Connected → Disconnected (with backoff)`
• Exponential backoff with jitter, capped (e.g., 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 30s, 60s)
• Heartbeat / ping: send a ping every 20–30s; if no pong in time, treat as disconnect
• Resume gracefully: server should send last-seen sequence numbers so client can request missed events

App lifecycle:
• On `AppLifecycleState.paused` (Android backgrounding, iOS app switcher): close cleanly
• On `resumed`: reconnect from scratch
• Otherwise the OS may kill your socket without notice and you'll send into a broken pipe

Authentication:
• Pass token in the connection URL (`wss://api.example.com/socket?token=...`) or first message
• On token refresh, reconnect — there's no "in-flight rotate" for ws auth

questions.question: How do you map DioException types into typed domain errors?
questions.answer:
Catching `DioException` everywhere bleeds the HTTP layer into your business logic. The clean pattern: a single error mapper translates Dio failures into a typed hierarchy your repositories and view models can handle without knowing about HTTP at all.

DioException.type variants:
• `connectionTimeout` — TCP/TLS handshake didn't complete in time
• `sendTimeout` / `receiveTimeout` — slow upload/download
• `badResponse` — server returned 4xx/5xx; check `error.response?.statusCode`
• `cancel` — your code cancelled via CancelToken
• `connectionError` — DNS, network down, no route
• `badCertificate` — TLS validation failure
• `unknown` — anything else; usually wraps another exception in `error.error`

Typical domain hierarchy:
• `NetworkException` — connectivity / timeout / unknown
• `ServerException(int statusCode, String? message)` — 5xx
• `BadRequestException(message, fields)` — 4xx with structured payload
• `UnauthorizedException` — 401, often triggers a refresh-token flow
• `ForbiddenException` — 403, no retry
• `NotFoundException` — 404
• `ConflictException` — 409 / unique-key violation
• `Cancelled` — user-initiated

Where to map:
• In a Dio interceptor's `onError` — keep the entry point clean
• OR in a single helper called from each repository method (`api.runOrThrow(() => dio.get(...))`)
• Either way, callers see only domain exceptions

questions.question: How do you design an offline-first sync engine for a Flutter app?
questions.answer:
Offline-first means the app works without a network and syncs in the background. The user never waits for the network on a write — local changes are applied immediately, then reconciled.

Building blocks:

1) Local source of truth — a real database (sqflite, drift, isar). The UI reads from it.
2) Mutation queue — every write (create/update/delete) is journaled to a `pending_ops` table with a unique id and idempotency key.
3) Apply locally first — the write also updates the main tables, marking the row as `dirty` (or `synced=false`).
4) Sync engine — a background worker that drains the queue: sends each pending op to the server, receives the canonical version, and reconciles.
5) Pull updates — periodically (or via WebSocket / push), fetch server changes since a `cursor`/`updated_at` and apply them locally.
6) Conflict resolution — last-write-wins is simple but loses data; field-level merge (CRDTs / OT / version vectors) is correct but complex. Pick based on data sensitivity.

Rules:
• Every mutation must have an idempotency key. The server uses it to dedupe — if the client retries after a crash, the server doesn't double-apply.
• The UI never reads from the network directly. It only reads from the local DB. The network feeds the local DB.
• When sync fails for non-retryable reasons (4xx), surface to the user and stop retrying.
• Use connectivity_plus to know when to wake the sync engine.

Gotchas:
• Do not store arbitrary Future<void> in the queue — store data: `op_type`, `payload`, `idempotency_key`. Reconstruct the request from data on each retry.
• Order matters for related writes (create user → create user's first note). Persist a per-aggregate sequence number.
• Schema changes between client versions need a queue migration, not just a schema migration.

questions.question: When and how do you use GraphQL in a Flutter app?
questions.answer:
GraphQL is a single endpoint where the client describes the data it wants in a typed query language. The server returns exactly that — no over-fetching, no under-fetching. It pairs well with Flutter when:

• You have a backend that already speaks GraphQL (Apollo / Hasura / Hot Chocolate / your own)
• Different screens need different shapes of data (deep, nested) and you'd otherwise create a sprawl of REST endpoints
• You want a strongly-typed client without writing fromJson by hand

Main library: `graphql_flutter` (built on `graphql` + `gql`). It ships:
• HttpLink + AuthLink + WebSocketLink for transport
• Cache-first / network-first / cache-and-network policies
• Query, Mutation, Subscription widgets
• Code-gen via `graphql_codegen` (or `ferry` ecosystem) for type-safe operations

When NOT to use:
• Your backend is a plain REST CRUD; adding GraphQL doesn't pay for itself
• Tiny app with two endpoints — REST is simpler
• File uploads — possible, but REST/multipart is more straightforward

Gotchas:
• Cache normalization — graphql_flutter normalizes by `__typename` + `id`. Without `id` in your queries, cache updates are messy.
• Pagination — Relay-style `connection { edges { node { id } } pageInfo }` is the conventional shape; design your queries around it from day one.
• Subscriptions need a WebSocket transport (`WebSocketLink`); reconnection and auth come with the same caveats as plain WebSockets.

questions.question: Compare SharedPreferences, SQLite (sqflite), Hive, and flutter_secure_storage.
questions.answer:
Use the right tool for the right job:

SharedPreferences:
• Simple key-value, small amounts of data
• Good for: settings, flags, onboarding state

sqflite (SQLite):
• Full relational database with SQL
• Good for: complex queries, relationships, offline data

Hive:
• NoSQL key-value in "boxes", very fast, pure Dart
• Good for: app state, offline cache, simple objects

flutter_secure_storage:
• Encrypted storage — Keychain (iOS) / Keystore (Android)
• REQUIRED for: tokens, passwords, sensitive PII
• NEVER store auth tokens in SharedPreferences

questions.question: What are the typical pitfalls of SharedPreferences and how do you avoid them?
questions.answer:
SharedPreferences is the simplest store but has sharp edges that bite teams in production:

• Async-only on first call — `getInstance()` is a Future. Don't gate the first frame on it; load preferences in main() before runApp() or expose them via a ValueNotifier so the UI can render a default while they hydrate.
• Not reactive — SharedPreferences does not notify listeners. Wrapping it in a ValueNotifier or a stream is on you.
• Wrong types fail silently — calling getString on a key stored as int returns null. Always pick a typed getter that matches what you wrote.
• No transactions — multiple `set*` calls are independent. Reboot mid-write and you keep the partial state. For multi-key writes use a single JSON-encoded blob or move to sqflite/Hive.
• Cross-platform encoding gotchas — booleans on iOS UserDefaults survive across reinstalls in some configurations; assume they don't and write idempotent migration code.
• Plaintext storage — never put tokens or secrets here. Use flutter_secure_storage.

questions.question: How do you handle schema migrations in sqflite?
questions.answer:
sqflite passes you `version` callbacks: onCreate runs once on a fresh database, onUpgrade runs when the user opens a higher version than what's on disk, and onDowngrade runs in the reverse case (default policy is to wipe — opt out explicitly).

Migration discipline:
• Bump the `version` integer in `openDatabase` whenever you change schema
• Keep migrations linear and idempotent — apply them by version number, never "if column missing"
• Migrations must be safe to interrupt and re-run; SQLite has transactions, so wrap each step in db.transaction
• ALTER TABLE in SQLite is limited (no DROP COLUMN before 3.35) — for column drops/renames you may need the rebuild dance: create a new table, copy, drop old, rename
• Test migrations from every prior version up to the latest in CI by opening a seeded old DB

questions.question: How do Hive type adapters and box encryption work?
questions.answer:
Hive stores objects in "boxes" — append-only files keyed by string or int. To persist your own classes, Hive needs a TypeAdapter that knows how to read/write each field. You can write adapters by hand or generate them from annotations.

Adapters:
• Each class gets a unique typeId (0–223 reserved for your app)
• Generator scans @HiveType + @HiveField annotations and emits a *.g.dart file
• Field numbers are forever — never reuse or change them. To remove a field, leave the number reserved and stop reading it.

Encryption:
• Hive supports AES via `Hive.openBox(name, encryptionCipher: HiveAesCipher(key))`
• Generate a 256-bit key once (Hive.generateSecureKey()) and store it in flutter_secure_storage — NOT in SharedPreferences
• Encryption protects data at rest; if you also want process-memory protection, you need a different solution (rare)
• On rotation, you must read with the old key and write with the new one in a one-shot migration

questions.question: How does flutter_secure_storage actually protect data on iOS vs Android?
questions.answer:
flutter_secure_storage is a thin Dart wrapper that delegates to the platform's secure store:

iOS — Keychain Services:
• Hardware-backed where available (Secure Enclave on A7+ devices)
• Defaults to `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlock` — readable after first device unlock until reboot. Configurable to AfterFirstUnlockThisDeviceOnly, WhenUnlocked, or WhenPasscodeSetThisDeviceOnly
• Survives app reinstall by default — explicitly delete on first run if you don't want this
• Synced via iCloud Keychain only if you opt in

Android — EncryptedSharedPreferences (newer) or Keystore-wrapped XML (older):
• Use AndroidOptions(encryptedSharedPreferences: true) — backed by AndroidX Security with Keystore-wrapped AES keys
• On API < 23 the Keystore is software-only — treat data as protected from casual access, not from a determined attacker with root
• Key rotation and biometric-bound keys require additional setup

Things to know:
• Reads/writes are async and can throw on locked devices, biometric failures, or backup restore corruption — code defensively
• Don't store huge blobs here — Keychain has soft limits and the API was built for credentials, not files
• Wipe-on-uninstall is NOT guaranteed on iOS; if it matters, delete keys explicitly on a first-launch flag

questions.question: When should you read and write files directly with path_provider?
questions.answer:
path_provider gives you platform-correct directories. Bare file IO is the right tool when your data is a blob, not key-value or relational rows.

Key directories:
• getApplicationDocumentsDirectory — survives uninstall on iOS only if backed up; default for user-generated content (notes, drafts)
• getApplicationSupportDirectory — for non-user app data (caches you'd rather keep), hidden from iOS Files
• getTemporaryDirectory — OS may purge anytime; for derived/transient data (image thumbnails, downloads in progress)
• getApplicationCacheDirectory — explicit cache location, OS may evict on storage pressure
• getExternalStorageDirectory (Android only) — public storage; needs scoped storage handling on modern Android

When to pick this over a database:
• Storing big binary data — exported PDFs, downloaded media, model files
• Round-tripping JSON dumps for backup/restore
• Sharing a file with another app via share_plus or open_filex
• You already have a serialization format and don't need queries

If you need search, partial updates, or thousands of small records — go to sqflite/Hive/Isar instead. File IO doesn't scale to that.

questions.question: When would you choose Drift over raw sqflite?
questions.answer:
Drift (formerly Moor) is a typed wrapper over sqflite/sqlite3 with a code-gen layer. It compiles your schema and queries into Dart classes you can call without writing strings.

Why pick Drift:
• Type-safe queries — `db.tasks.select().where((t) => t.done.equals(false)).get()` instead of raw SQL with column-name typos
• Generated table classes from a single source of truth — schema lives in Dart or .drift files, the generator emits everything
• First-class streams — any query can be converted into a Stream<List<Row>> that auto-emits when the underlying tables change
• Migrations API with versioned steps and a built-in test harness
• Works on web (sql.js), desktop (sqlite3), mobile (sqflite/sqlite3) with the same code

When to skip Drift:
• You already have a small handful of queries and don't want a build_runner step
• You're integrating with a legacy schema where raw control matters more than typing
• Bundle size matters and you can't afford the generated code

Drift vs Hive: Drift = relational queries, joins, aggregates. Hive = quick key-value or object box, no SQL. They solve different problems.

questions.question: What are Isar and ObjectBox, and when do they beat sqflite?
questions.answer:
Isar and ObjectBox are NoSQL object databases written in native code (Rust for Isar, C++ for ObjectBox) with Dart bindings. They sit above flat-file stores like Hive but below relational stores like sqflite/Drift.

Why they're fast:
• Memory-mapped reads — no copying, no serialization on read
• Indexed properties — composite, full-text, and case-insensitive indexes available
• Transactions are first-class — wrap reads or writes in `isar.writeTxn(...)`
• Reactive queries — subscribe to a query and get a stream of updates

Where they shine over sqflite:
• Hot paths with thousands of writes/sec (event logs, sync engines, offline-first apps)
• Complex object graphs you'd otherwise have to flatten into joins
• Full-text search on user content without standing up FTS5 manually

Trade-offs:
• Bigger bundle (native libs)
• Less mature ecosystem than SQLite — fewer integrations, no SQL knowledge transfer
• Schema is in Dart, migration story is simpler but less tested under odd conditions

Rule of thumb: SQLite/Drift for relational data and shared schema with backend. Isar/ObjectBox for performance-sensitive object stores or when SQL is overkill.

questions.question: What goes wrong with concurrent writes to a local database, and how do you avoid it?
questions.answer:
Local databases on mobile look single-user, but they get hammered concurrently in practice: a sync engine writing in the background, the UI saving a draft, a notification handler updating a row, all on different isolates and Futures.

Problems you will hit:
• Lost updates — two reads-then-writes on the same row, last write wins, the first edit is silently lost
• Write conflicts — SQLite locks the database during a write; long readers block writers and vice versa. Without WAL mode, this serializes everything
• Cross-isolate access — opening the same SQLite file from multiple isolates without coordination crashes or corrupts (sqflite uses a single-instance protocol; sqlite3 + isolate workers needs explicit setup)
• Async reentry — one Future awaits in the middle of a transaction; another Future starts a new transaction and deadlocks behind the first

Defenses:
• Always wrap multi-statement writes in db.transaction(...) — it's the only way to get atomicity
• Enable WAL mode (`PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`) for concurrent reader+writer
• Centralize writes through a single class so the order is enforceable; serialize background sync writes through a Mutex/Lock if you need stricter ordering
• For object DBs (Isar/Hive), keep transactions short — don't await unrelated work inside a write txn
• Use optimistic concurrency for last-write-wins fields (a `revision` integer): write only if revision matches the one you read

questions.question: What are the three types of tests in Flutter and when to use each?
questions.answer:
Testing pyramid (more → fewer as you go up):

Unit Tests — fastest, most numerous:
• Single function, class, or method in isolation
• Mock all dependencies
• Run with: flutter test test/unit/

Widget Tests — medium speed, medium coverage:
• Single widget or small widget tree
• testWidgets() + WidgetTester
• Can simulate user interactions (tap, scroll, type)
• Run with: flutter test test/widget/

Integration Tests — slowest, fewest, highest confidence:
• Full app flows on real device or emulator
• Uses integration_test package or patrol
• Covers E2E user journeys

Also: Golden Tests — screenshot comparison for catching UI regressions.

questions.question: How do you write golden tests in Flutter?
questions.answer:
Golden tests (snapshot tests) compare a widget's rendered output pixel-by-pixel against a saved "golden" reference image. They catch unintended visual regressions.

Workflow:
1. Write test with matchesGoldenFile()
2. Run flutter test --update-goldens to generate reference images
3. Commit the golden files to source control
4. CI runs tests — fails if pixels differ

Gotchas:
• Golden images are platform-dependent (macOS vs Linux vs Windows render slightly differently)
• Run goldens on a fixed platform (usually Linux in CI)
• Use goldenFileComparator to set tolerance for minor anti-aliasing differences

questions.question: How do you test widgets that depend on InheritedWidgets (Theme, MediaQuery, etc.)?
questions.answer:
When testing widgets that depend on InheritedWidgets:

1. Wrap in MaterialApp or WidgetsApp for Theme/MediaQuery
2. Use MediaQuery.withClampedTextScaling() for MediaQuery
3. Provide mocked InheritedWidgets
4. Use testWidgets and pumpWidget

Common inherited dependencies:
• Theme
• MediaQuery (size, orientation, padding)
• Navigator
• Localizations
• Provider

questions.question: How do you choose between mocktail and mockito for mocking dependencies?
questions.answer:
Both let you fake classes for tests; the choice mostly comes down to ergonomics and null safety.

mocktail (modern, recommended):
• No code generation — just `class MockX extends Mock implements X {}`
• Works great with sealed classes, records, and Dart 3 features
• Stubbing is closure-based: `when(() => repo.fetch()).thenAnswer((_) async => [...])`
• Forces you to register fallback values for non-nullable arg matchers (`registerFallbackValue(...)`)
• Smaller API surface, less boilerplate

mockito (older, build_runner-based):
• `@GenerateMocks([X])` annotation, `dart run build_runner build` produces `MockX`
• Generated `mocks.mocks.dart` files clutter the test/ directory
• Requires casts and `as Mock` plumbing in places
• Still useful for legacy projects already invested in it

Guidelines:
• New projects → mocktail. Less ceremony, no codegen.
• Mocking concrete classes (not interfaces): mocktail handles it (`class MockDio extends Mock implements Dio {}`); mockito needs `@GenerateNiceMocks([MockSpec<Dio>()])`.
• For TIME and external systems prefer fakes (hand-written stubs implementing the same interface). Mocks should verify CALL CONTRACTS; fakes provide BEHAVIOR.

Common pitfalls regardless of library:
• Stubbing arg matchers without registerFallbackValue → cryptic errors at test run
• Re-stubbing on the same method without `reset()` — old stubs linger
• Mocking too deeply — mocking a Dio Response is brittle; prefer a fake API client

questions.question: What's the difference between pump, pumpAndSettle, pumpFor, and runAsync in widget tests?
questions.answer:
Widget tests run inside a fake clock — `Future.delayed(Duration(seconds: 5))` doesn't wait 5 seconds, it just schedules a timer the test can advance. The pump variants control how time advances.

tester.pump([Duration]):
• Runs the next frame. With no argument, advances 0 ms (just rebuilds).
• With a Duration, advances the clock by that duration and runs intervening frames.
• Use to step through animations frame by frame, or to flush a single setState.

tester.pumpAndSettle([Duration interval]):
• Repeatedly pumps until the binding reports no scheduled frames
• Default `interval: 100ms`; default total timeout: 10 minutes (yes, really)
• Use to skip past a finished animation or wait for the tree to be idle
• ❌ Will hang forever on infinite animations (CircularProgressIndicator, repeating Lottie)

tester.pumpAndSettle(Duration timeout):
• Same as above but with explicit cap; if not idle by then, throws

tester.pump(timeout) cycles vs. pumpFrames:
• Use `tester.pumpFrames(target, totalDuration)` for animations of known length

tester.runAsync<T>(action):
• Executes `action` against the REAL clock. Use it for code that must use a real Future (HTTP-like SDKs, timers under the hood).
• Inside `runAsync`, you cannot call pump — you're outside the test scheduler.
• Required when your code uses `dart:io` HttpClient under the hood, or anything that schedules outside the FakeAsync

Gotchas:
• `pumpAndSettle` near a CircularProgressIndicator hangs — if your loading state shows one, replace with an indeterminate finder before settling, or pump for a fixed duration.
• Network mocks should resolve their Futures via the test scheduler (`Future.value(...)`). Real I/O needs runAsync.

questions.question: How do you write integration tests with integration_test or patrol?
questions.answer:
Integration tests run on a real device or emulator and exercise the whole app — UI, business logic, networking (typically faked at the boundary). They catch the bugs unit and widget tests can't.

integration_test (official):
• Same Test API as widget tests, but runs against a real Flutter engine
• Lives in `integration_test/` directory
• `IntegrationTestWidgetsFlutterBinding.ensureInitialized();` in main
• Run: `flutter test integration_test`
• Limited to Flutter UI — cannot interact with native dialogs, system permissions, push notifications, biometric prompts

patrol (community, more powerful):
• Adds NATIVE control: tap system permission dialogs, type into native text fields, use the system back button reliably, scroll Android settings
• Critical for full E2E flows that touch permissions or share/intent flows
• Uses its own test runner (`patrol test`) that wraps integration_test
• Heavier setup; runs slower than vanilla integration_test

When to use which:
• Pure-Flutter flows — integration_test is enough
• Anything involving permissions, push, share, app settings — patrol
• CI cost matters → keep integration_tests fast, use patrol only for the few E2Es that absolutely need it

Fundamentals (both):
• Replace network with a fake (in-process server, recorded fixtures)
• Wipe storage between tests
• Mock time/date to keep results deterministic
• Tag flakies and run them off the critical path

questions.question: How do you mock HTTP/Dio in tests without real network calls?
questions.answer:
Real HTTP is forbidden in CI — flaky, slow, and depends on staging up-time. Three layers of fake at the network boundary, from least to most invasive:

1) Inject a fake repository
• Replace the data source (UserRepository) with a hand-written Fake. The Dio instance never gets called.
• Cleanest separation; widget tests don't even know HTTP exists.
• Use this when you can.

2) Mock Dio's adapter (http_mock_adapter / DioAdapter)
• Replace `dio.httpClientAdapter` with a controlled adapter that matches request paths and returns canned responses
• Lets you assert that the right URL/method/body was hit
• Use when testing the data layer itself (the repository → API code path)

3) Local in-process server
• Run `shelf` on a random port; point the app's base URL at it
• Realistic — exercises real serialization and headers
• Heavier setup; valuable for integration tests where you want as much real code as possible

Which to use when:
• Unit/widget tests of UI/Bloc → fake repository (option 1)
• Repository tests → DioAdapter (option 2)
• Integration tests of full flows → in-process server (option 3)

Gotcha: tests that check `requestOptions.headers` need the SAME interceptor stack as production. Don't strip the auth interceptor in tests — verify it adds the header.

questions.question: How do you measure code coverage and gate it in CI?
questions.answer:
Coverage tells you which lines of source ran during tests. It's a useful indicator and a terrible target — chase covered behaviour, not number-go-up.

Flutter generates `coverage/lcov.info` when you run `flutter test --coverage`. From there:

• Render an HTML report locally with `genhtml` for browsing
• Upload to Codecov / Coveralls — they comment on PRs with diffs
• Gate CI: fail the build if coverage drops below a threshold or the PR adds untested code

What to exclude:
• Generated files (`.g.dart`, `.freezed.dart`, `.gr.dart`) — they don't carry your logic
• `main.dart` boot — usually thin glue with little to test
• Auto-generated GraphQL/protobuf serialization
• Use `// coverage:ignore-line` and `// coverage:ignore-file` annotations sparingly

What to chase:
• Domain logic and reducers (Bloc on<>, Notifier methods) — high value, fast tests
• Error paths — coverage often misses 4xx and 5xx branches; tests there are very valuable
• Pure utility functions — easy to test, easy to break

What to deprioritize:
• 100% widget coverage — golden + integration cover the rendered output more meaningfully
• Trivial getters/setters — no bug surface

CI pattern that works in practice:
• `flutter test --coverage` on PRs
• `dart pub global activate remove_from_coverage` to strip generated files
• Codecov action with a `target: 80%, threshold: 1%` for the diff (a PR can lower coverage by at most 1%)
• Don't fail builds when coverage drops because you wrote a comment — measure the diff, not the absolute
questions.code_example:
# .github/workflows/test.yml
name: Test + Coverage
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0' }
      - run: flutter pub get
      - run: flutter test --coverage

      # Strip generated files from the report
      - run: |
          dart pub global activate remove_from_coverage
          dart pub global run remove_from_coverage:remove_from_coverage \
            -f coverage/lcov.info \
            -r '\.g\.dart$' \
            -r '\.freezed\.dart$' \
            -r '\.gr\.dart$' \
            -r 'lib/main\.dart$' \
            -r 'lib/generated/.*'

      - name: Upload to Codecov
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
        with:
          files: coverage/lcov.info
          fail_ci_if_error: true

# ── Local report ──
# flutter test --coverage
# genhtml coverage/lcov.info -o coverage/html
# open coverage/html/index.html

# ── codecov.yml — gate the diff, not the absolute ──
# coverage:
#   status:
#     project:
#       default:
#         target: auto                  # don't enforce a fixed % 
#         threshold: 1%                  # allow 1% drop max
#     patch:
#       default:
#         target: 80%                    # new code must be >= 80%
# ignore:
#   - lib/main.dart
#   - lib/generated/**
#   - '**/*.g.dart'
#   - '**/*.freezed.dart'

// ── Inline ignores ──
int expensiveCompute(int n) {
  // coverage:ignore-line — debug helper, not worth testing
  if (kDebugMode) print('compute($n)');
  return n * 2;
}

questions.question: How do you test a screen that uses Riverpod or BLoC?
questions.answer:
Both libraries provide first-class test support; the patterns are very similar — inject mocks/fakes at the dependency boundary, then assert state OR rendered UI.

Riverpod:
• `ProviderContainer` for unit tests — no widget tree needed
• `overrides:` swaps any provider for a fake (e.g. mock the apiClientProvider, the rest of the graph still composes)
• In widget tests, wrap with `ProviderScope(overrides: [...], child: ...)`
• Pure-Dart logic tests are very fast — no `pumpWidget` overhead

BLoC:
• `blocTest` from package:bloc_test for Bloc/Cubit logic — script events, expect states
• In widget tests, use `BlocProvider.value(value: mockBloc, ...)` to inject
• `MockBloc` and `whenListen(stream, initialState: ...)` simulate state sequences without running the real bloc

Shared rules:
• Test logic in unit tests; test rendering in widget tests; don't test logic by inspecting widgets — slow and brittle
• Reset between tests — fresh ProviderContainer / fresh Bloc / clean storage
• Don't reach into private state. Test the observable behavior (state stream, rendered UI).
• Cancel subscriptions and dispose containers in tearDown to avoid leaks across tests

Both ecosystems play well with mocktail for the dependency layer; use bloc_test specifically for the bloc-flow assertions.

questions.question: Explain Clean Architecture in Flutter with layers and responsibilities.
questions.answer:
Clean Architecture (Robert C. Martin) organizes code in concentric layers with the Dependency Rule: outer layers depend on inner layers, NEVER the reverse.

Three layers (inside out):

1. Domain (innermost, pure Dart):
   • Entities — core business objects (no framework dependencies)
   • Use Cases — single business operations
   • Repository interfaces — abstract contracts

2. Data (implements domain contracts):
   • Repository implementations
   • Remote data sources (API)
   • Local data sources (database/cache)
   • Data models (JSON-capable)

3. Presentation (outer):
   • Blocs/Cubits/ViewModels
   • Pages and Widgets
   • Only knows domain objects through use cases

Testability: domain is tested with zero dependencies; data with mocked sources; presentation with mocked use cases.

questions.question: How do you structure a large Flutter app by features (feature-first)?
questions.answer:
Feature-first (vertical slicing) groups files by FEATURE rather than by layer.

Layer-first (horizontal):
lib/models/ lib/repositories/ lib/blocs/ lib/screens/
Problem: Adding a feature requires touching 4+ folders.

Feature-first (vertical):
lib/features/auth/ lib/features/cart/ lib/features/profile/
Each feature folder contains its own models, bloc, repo, screens, widgets.

Benefits:
• High cohesion — related code sits together
• Easy to delete/add features
• Teams can own features independently
• Clear boundaries → better for micro-frontends / modularization

Convention: shared code lives in lib/core/ or lib/shared/

questions.question: Compare MVC, MVP, MVVM, and MVI patterns and how they map to Flutter.
questions.answer:
All four organize the relationship between UI and business logic but differ in who calls whom and how data flows.

MVC (Model-View-Controller):
• Controller mediates between Model and View — V calls C, C updates M, M notifies V
• Most general, often vague in modern apps; modern "MVC" frameworks usually mean MVP or MVVM

MVP (Model-View-Presenter):
• Presenter holds the logic; View is dumb and only renders / dispatches user actions
• View has an interface (`MyView { showLoading(); showUser(User u); }`) the Presenter calls
• Common in Android Java; in Flutter it has been mostly displaced by MVVM/Bloc

MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel):
• ViewModel exposes observable state (`Stream<State>` / `ChangeNotifier`)
• View binds to that state and rebuilds reactively — no manual presenter calls
• Closest fit for Flutter: Riverpod's Notifier, Provider's ChangeNotifier, BLoC/Cubit, GetX, MobX
• `ConsumerWidget`/`BlocBuilder`/`Observer` are the binding

MVI (Model-View-Intent):
• Single state object per screen, unidirectional data flow: Intent → reducer → State → View
• Inspired by Redux/Elm; in Flutter it's BLoC's standard event/state model — name aside, you're already there

In Flutter today: "MVVM" or "BLoC pattern" are the two terms you'll hear most. They both fit the framework's reactive nature; choose based on team experience and library preference.

questions.question: What does it mean to apply SOLID principles in Flutter?
questions.answer:
SOLID is a set of object-oriented design principles. Applied loosely in Flutter:

Single Responsibility — every class does one thing:
• A widget renders UI; not networking, not business logic
• A repository fetches data; not formatting it for display
• Tells you when to split: when describing the class needs the word "and"

Open/Closed — open for extension, closed for modification:
• Add new subclasses of a sealed family without rewriting consumers
• Use the Strategy pattern (PaymentStrategy interface) so a new payment processor doesn't change the checkout class

Liskov Substitution — subtypes should be substitutable for their base:
• If repo: UserRepository, swapping in MockUserRepository should not break callers
• Don't tighten preconditions or weaken postconditions in a subclass

Interface Segregation — many small interfaces beat one big one:
• Don't make consumers depend on methods they don't use
• Split `RepositoryFat` into `Reader`, `Writer`, `Cacher` — clients depend only on what they use

Dependency Inversion — depend on abstractions, not concretions:
• Bloc depends on `AuthRepository` interface, not on `DioAuthRepositoryImpl`
• Inject the implementation via constructor (or DI framework)
• Lets you mock for tests and swap implementations per environment

Pragmatic notes:
• SOLID is a guide, not a religion. Over-applying creates fragmentation (every class is an interface, every method has its own file).
• Single Responsibility and Dependency Inversion are the two that pay off most consistently in Flutter apps.

questions.question: How do you split a large Flutter app into modular packages or workspaces?
questions.answer:
Once a Flutter codebase passes ~50k lines or 3+ teams, packing everything into one `lib/` becomes painful: long compile cycles, accidental cross-feature imports, no enforced boundaries. Splitting into local packages is the standard remedy.

Approaches:

1) Monorepo with local packages (Melos / Dart 3 workspaces):
• Each feature is its own `package:my_app_<feature>` in `packages/`
• A top-level `app/` package depends on them and ties everything together
• Use Melos to bootstrap, run scripts across packages, manage versions; or, in Dart 3.5+, use built-in workspaces (`pubspec.yaml` `workspace:`)
• Boundaries: package A can only import from package B if it explicitly depends on it — no accidental imports

2) Plugin packages for native code:
• Anything that ships its own Kotlin/Swift goes into a Flutter plugin package — even if it's only used by your app
• Cleaner Android/iOS module separation

3) Pure-Dart packages for shared logic:
• `domain/` (entities, use cases) and `core/` utilities are great candidates
• They have no Flutter dependency, so they can be tested fast and reused outside the app (e.g., a CLI or web)

What to split:
• Each independently-owned feature: auth, billing, search, etc.
• Cross-cutting: theming, networking client, logging
• Generated code: Pigeon / GraphQL / API stubs

What NOT to split:
• Tiny app — splitting adds ceremony for no gain
• Tight coupling between two "features" — fix the coupling first, then split

Gotcha: package boundaries push you to make APIs explicit. That's the point — but expect a refactor as you formalize public surfaces.
questions.code_example:
# ── Dart 3.5+ workspaces (recommended for new monorepos) ──
# Top-level pubspec.yaml
name: my_app_workspace
environment: { sdk: ^3.5.0 }
workspace:
  - app
  - packages/auth
  - packages/billing
  - packages/core
  - packages/domain
  - packages/networking

# Each package has its own pubspec, but `flutter pub get` from the root resolves them together.
# Cross-package deps are real version-pinned dependencies; the analyzer enforces them.

# packages/auth/pubspec.yaml
name: my_app_auth
resolution: workspace
dependencies:
  flutter: { sdk: flutter }
  my_app_domain: any        # pure-Dart domain layer
  my_app_networking: any

# app/pubspec.yaml
name: my_app
resolution: workspace
dependencies:
  flutter: { sdk: flutter }
  my_app_auth:    any
  my_app_billing: any
  my_app_core:    any

# ── Or: Melos (still common, supports older SDKs) ──
# melos.yaml
name: my_app
packages:
  - app
  - packages/**
scripts:
  analyze:
    run: flutter analyze
    exec:
      concurrency: 4
  test:
    run: flutter test
    exec:
      concurrency: 2

# Bootstrap (once, after clone)
# dart pub global activate melos
# melos bootstrap
# melos run analyze
# melos run test

# ── Module structure ──
# packages/auth/lib/
#   auth.dart                    ← public surface (barrel file)
#   src/                         ← internal code, NOT exported
#     auth_repository.dart
#     auth_repository_impl.dart
#     auth_bloc.dart
#     pages/login_page.dart
#
# packages/auth/lib/auth.dart
export 'src/auth_repository.dart' show AuthRepository;
export 'src/auth_bloc.dart'         show AuthBloc, AuthState;
export 'src/pages/login_page.dart'  show LoginPage;
# Implementation classes stay private.

# ── App composition ──
# app/lib/main.dart
# import 'package:my_app_auth/auth.dart';
# import 'package:my_app_billing/billing.dart';
# void main() => runApp(MyApp());

questions.question: How do you map between domain entities and data DTOs cleanly?
questions.answer:
The data layer talks JSON; the domain layer talks Dart. Putting `fromJson`/`toJson` directly on your domain entities couples business logic to the wire format — a bad idea for anything beyond a tiny prototype.

Clean separation:

• Domain entity (`User`) — pure Dart. Constructor, fields, equality, no JSON.
• Data DTO (`UserDto`) — knows how to (de)serialize. Lives in the data layer.
• Mapper — converts DTO ↔ entity. Either an extension method, a top-level function, or a static method on the DTO.

Why this pays off:
• You can refactor the JSON shape without breaking domain or UI
• You can drop fields that aren't business-relevant (debug timestamps, internal IDs)
• You can add fields the wire doesn't carry (computed, defaulted, or denormalized from another DTO)
• You can have multiple DTOs (REST + GraphQL + cached) all mapping to the same entity

When to skip:
• Throwaway prototype, tiny single-feature app — the DTO/entity overhead may not pay
• When using freezed throughout and the API exactly matches your domain shape — though you'll regret it the first time the API changes

Gotcha: do NOT inherit DTO from entity to save typing — that breaks the layer rule (DTOs should not leak into domain code). Use composition or a mapper.

questions.question: How do you design a typed AppError hierarchy that flows from data layer to UI?
questions.answer:
Every layer in a Flutter app can fail differently. If you let exceptions bubble up untyped, your UI ends up with `if (e.toString().contains('401'))` — fragile and unmaintainable. A small sealed AppError tree, mapped at each layer boundary, gives you UI-friendly errors with no leaks.

Design:

1) Domain defines `sealed class AppError` with subtypes that the UI knows how to render:
   - NetworkError, TimeoutError, ServerError(int), UnauthorizedError, ForbiddenError, NotFoundError, ValidationError(fields), CancelledError, UnknownError

2) Data layer maps low-level exceptions (DioException, SocketException, FormatException) into AppErrors. The repository's signature throws AppError, never DioException.

3) Domain use cases just rethrow — they're already typed.

4) Presentation uses a sealed switch to decide what to do per error:
   - UnauthorizedError → redirect to login
   - ValidationError → highlight fields
   - NetworkError → show offline banner with retry
   - default → generic toast

Why sealed:
• Pattern matching is exhaustive — adding a new error class fails every switch in the codebase until handled
• No untyped string parsing
• Encourages thinking about user-visible categories instead of HTTP codes

Don't model business outcomes as errors:
• If "username already taken" is a normal flow, return a Result<Ok|Conflict>, don't throw. Reserve errors for unexpected, exceptional cases.
• Validation errors are an edge case — they're often modeled as both. Pick one and stay consistent.

questions.question: How do feature flags and staged rollouts fit into a Flutter app's architecture?
questions.answer:
Feature flags decouple deploy from release: ship the code dark, flip a switch later for some or all users. Done right, they're a multiplier — risky changes ship safely, A/B tests are cheap, rollbacks are instant.

Layers:

1) Flag store — where the truth lives:
• Remote config (Firebase Remote Config, GrowthBook, LaunchDarkly, ConfigCat) — server-side, can target by user/cohort/version
• Bundled defaults — a JSON shipped with the app so the first launch (no network) has reasonable behavior
• Local overrides — hidden settings screen for QA to flip flags without server access

2) Flag client — a thin Dart abstraction:
• `FeatureFlags.checkoutV2Enabled` returns bool
• Caches the latest fetched values in memory + secure storage so a transient network hiccup doesn't toggle features
• Refreshes on app foreground and after auth changes

3) Branching points — where flags are read:
• At the composition root: pick which Notifier/Bloc to instantiate
• In feature modules: hide menu items, choose code path
• Use sparingly inside hot paths — flag lookups are cheap but if-soup ages badly

4) Cleanup discipline:
• Every flag has a lifetime: experiment → ramp → remove
• Tag the code (`// FLAG: checkout_v2 — remove after 2026-06-01`)
• Schedule a cleanup task (this app's `/schedule` skill is exactly for this — open a removal PR after the ramp)

Gotchas:
• Don't gate critical security checks behind a flag — bypass risk
• Make sure flag state is part of analytics events so you can correlate metrics to variant
• Test BOTH branches in CI; otherwise the disabled branch quietly bit-rots until you flip and discover it's broken

questions.question: How do you set up dependency injection at scale (composition root, scopes, environments)?
questions.answer:
Dependency injection in a real app needs three things beyond "wire constructors in main()":

1) A composition root
• A single place where the dependency graph is constructed
• In Flutter that's `main.dart` or an `inject.dart` initializer
• Avoid resolving dependencies inside business code; resolve at the root and pass constructor args from there

2) Scopes
• App scope — singletons living for the lifetime of the app: Dio, AuthRepository, Logger
• User scope — recreated when a different user signs in: PreferencesNotifier, UserSpecificCache
• Screen / feature scope — owned by a route or a state holder; auto-disposed when no longer used
• Riverpod's `.autoDispose` and family modifier model these naturally; get_it has manual scopes via `pushNewScope`

3) Environments / flavors
• Override providers per build flavor (dev / staging / prod) — e.g., a fake API in dev, real one in prod
• Runtime overrides for QA/feature flags ("force the slow-net implementation to test loading states")
• Tests are just another environment — override the same providers with fakes

Guidelines:
• Bind interfaces, not concrete types — your bloc shouldn't see `DioAuthRepositoryImpl`, only `AuthRepository`
• Don't eagerly instantiate singletons that aren't always needed; both Riverpod and get_it support lazy by default
• Document the graph: a one-page README listing what's app-scoped, user-scoped, screen-scoped saves new joiners hours
• Avoid the service-locator anti-pattern of `MyService.instance.method()` baked into business code — it's just a global with extra steps

questions.question: Implement and explain the Repository Pattern in Flutter.
questions.answer:
The Repository Pattern abstracts data access behind an interface. The rest of the app works with the interface — it doesn't care if data comes from an API, a database, or a cache.

Benefits:
• Swap implementations without touching business logic
• Inject mocks in tests
• Centralize caching and offline logic
• Single source of truth for data

Pattern: Domain defines abstract interface → Data layer provides concrete implementation.

questions.question: Explain common GoF design patterns in Flutter: Singleton, Factory, Observer, Strategy.
questions.answer:
Design Patterns in Flutter context:

Singleton — single instance throughout app life (service locator, config)
Factory — create objects via a method that can return subtypes (platform-specific services)
Observer — publish/subscribe (ChangeNotifier, Streams are the pattern!)
Strategy — plug-in interchangeable algorithms at runtime
Builder — build complex objects step-by-step
Command — encapsulate operations as objects (Actions, undo/redo)

questions.question: Explain Dependency Injection in Flutter. Compare get_it vs injectable vs Riverpod.
questions.answer:
Dependency Injection (DI) provides dependencies to a class from outside rather than creating them internally — enabling testability, flexibility, and loose coupling.

Approaches in Flutter:

get_it — service locator (global registry):
• Simple, no code generation
• Not compile-time safe (runtime lookup)
• Great for simple to medium apps

injectable (wraps get_it):
• Adds code generation with annotations
• @injectable, @singleton, @lazySingleton, @module
• Compile-time safe via generated code

Riverpod:
• First-class DI built into state management
• Compile-time safe, auto-dispose, testable
• Best for new Flutter apps

questions.question: How do you apply the Adapter pattern to wrap an inconsistent third-party API?
questions.answer:
An Adapter wraps a class with one interface and exposes it through another. Useful when you depend on a third-party library or legacy API whose surface doesn't match the contract the rest of your app expects — and you don't control either side.

When to reach for it:
• Two analytics SDKs you want to behind a single `Analytics` contract
• A payment SDK with weird typed callbacks; you want a clean `Future<Result> charge(...)` API
• A platform SDK with platform-specific data types — wrap them into your domain types

Forms in Dart:
• Class adapter — extends both your interface and the foreign class. Rare; Dart's no multiple inheritance limits this.
• Object adapter (typical) — implements your interface, holds the foreign object as a field, and translates calls

The adapter is the "anti-corruption layer". It keeps the foreign vocabulary out of your domain code; if the third party releases a breaking v2, only the adapter changes.

questions.question: How do you implement the Decorator pattern in Dart, and when do mixins or extensions cover the same need?
questions.answer:
Decorator wraps an object to add behavior without changing the wrapped object's class. The wrapper implements the same interface and forwards calls to the inner instance, sandwiching extra logic around them.

Classic uses:
• Logging / metrics around a repository or HTTP client
• Caching layer in front of a network client
• Retry / circuit-breaker around any service
• Authorization checks in front of a domain service

Why you'd pick it over inheritance:
• Cross-cutting concerns can be composed (Auth + Logging + Cache stacked)
• You can swap or remove a decorator without touching the wrapped class
• You can decorate at runtime (different decorators per environment / per feature flag)

Dart-specific alternatives:
• Mixin — folds behavior into the class itself; can't be composed at runtime, but cleaner when the behavior is intrinsic
• Extension — adds methods without changing the type. Doesn't work for overriding interface methods. Good for static helpers, bad for cross-cutting concerns that need polymorphism.
• Decorator class — when the behavior must be optional, runtime-composable, and visible through the same interface, this is still the right tool

questions.question: How do you implement the Command pattern with undo/redo support?
questions.answer:
A Command encapsulates an operation as an object with an `execute()` method (and optionally an `undo()`). Storing commands instead of just calling functions buys you:

• Undo/redo stacks — push executed commands onto a history; pop and call undo()
• Macro-recording — replay a sequence of commands later, in tests, or for telemetry
• Decoupling sender from receiver — a button doesn't know what "delete user" does, only that it triggers a Command bound to its handler
• Auditing / logging — log every command executed; great for compliance trails

When NOT to:
• Trivial UI actions — wrapping every button in a Command is overkill
• Only when you need replay, undo, or composition

Dart-friendly shape:
• Sealed class hierarchy of commands
• `CommandRunner` keeps undo and redo stacks
• Each command captures the BEFORE state it needs to restore — never re-derive in undo()

questions.question: How does the State pattern compare with Dart 3 sealed classes?
questions.answer:
Both express "this entity behaves differently in each of N modes". They live at different levels of formality and trade off in interesting ways.

State pattern (classic GoF):
• An object delegates behavior to a `State` field that implements an interface
• The state object can swap itself out (`context.state = NextState()`)
• Each state knows which states are reachable from it — transitions live in code, not in a switch table
• Best when each state has rich, nontrivial behavior of its own

Sealed class + pattern matching (Dart 3):
• A `sealed` parent with concrete subtypes; the surrounding code switches on which subtype is current
• Compiler checks exhaustiveness — adding a new state forces every switch to handle it
• States become value objects; transitions are usually expressed in the surrounding code (a Notifier/Cubit/Bloc)
• Best for state that's mostly data with little intrinsic behavior

Which to pick:
• Many transitions, each state has rich behavior, want behavior co-located with state → State pattern
• Mostly inert data shapes that drive UI/logic externally → sealed classes
• In Flutter app code with Riverpod/Bloc/Cubit, sealed classes win 9 times out of 10 because the state itself is just data and the Notifier holds the logic.

questions.question: What is the BLoC pattern, and how does it differ from the Observer pattern in general?
questions.answer:
BLoC (Business Logic Component) is a specific Flutter incarnation of the broader Observer pattern, with three opinionated rules:

• Inputs are events — discrete, named, immutable. UI dispatches them.
• Outputs are states — also immutable. UI rebuilds from them.
• In between is a single class that maps events to states, often via stream transformations

Observer pattern in general:
• Some Subject notifies any number of Observers when something changes
• Observers can themselves be anywhere — UI, services, other components
• ChangeNotifier, Stream, ValueNotifier are all Observer implementations

What BLoC adds on top:
• A name and shape for inputs (events) and outputs (states)
• Cancellation of in-flight work when a new event supersedes (e.g. switchMap on search)
• Replayability — the event log can be recorded and replayed for tests, time-travel debugging
• A clear test surface — give the BLoC events, expect states; no widget test needed

When Observer is enough:
• Simple, local UI state (a checkbox, a tab index) — ChangeNotifier or ValueNotifier is plenty
• You don't need event/state history

When BLoC pays off:
• Complex screens with many inputs and async loading flows
• You want to test business logic without rendering UI
• You need debounce / switchMap-style cancellation in business logic, not in widgets

questions.question: When should you use the Service Locator pattern vs constructor injection?
questions.answer:
Both deliver dependencies to the code that uses them, but they trade off in different directions.

Service Locator (get_it / GetIt):
• A global registry holds all your services
• Code looks them up by type at the call site: `final repo = sl<UserRepository>()`
• Pros: zero plumbing, accessible anywhere (tap handlers, isolates, free functions)
• Cons: dependencies are HIDDEN at the call site (you must read the code to know what it needs); harder to mock by-test (must reset the global registry); ordering of registrations matters

Constructor Injection (Riverpod / Provider, or hand-rolled):
• Each class declares its dependencies as constructor args
• Pros: dependencies are EXPLICIT (the constructor signature is the contract); easy to test (pass mocks directly); compile-time safe
• Cons: more plumbing — you have to thread dependencies through constructors; needs a composition root or a DI framework to wire it up

Rule of thumb in Flutter:
• New code, especially anything testable: Riverpod / explicit constructor injection — modern and compile-time safe
• Cross-cutting concerns that genuinely need to be reachable from everywhere (logger, analytics): a service locator is fine, but ONLY for those
• Legacy code or teams that prefer simplicity: get_it works perfectly; just be disciplined about test setup

Mixing the two is fine in practice: Riverpod for application services and view models; get_it for plugins or true singletons that don't fit Riverpod's lifecycle.

questions.question: Explain Big O notation and common algorithm complexities.
questions.answer:
Big O describes how an algorithm's time or space scales with input size n.

Common complexities (best → worst):
• O(1) — constant: hash map lookup, array access
• O(log n) — logarithmic: binary search, balanced BST
• O(n) — linear: loop through array, linear search
• O(n log n) — linearithmic: merge sort, heap sort
• O(n²) — quadratic: nested loops, bubble sort
• O(2ⁿ) — exponential: subset enumeration, naive recursive Fib
• O(n!) — factorial: permutations, brute-force TSP

Dart built-in complexities:
• List.add() = O(1) amortized; List.insert(0) = O(n)
• Map/Set operations = O(1) average (hash-based)
• List.sort() = O(n log n) (Timsort)

questions.question: Implement a linked list and explain when to use it vs List.
questions.answer:
Linked List — each node holds data + pointer to next.
• O(1) insert/remove at head
• O(n) access by index
• No capacity limit (no reallocation)
• More memory per element (pointer overhead)

Dart's List is a dynamic array:
• O(1) amortized add (doubles capacity on resize)
• O(1) index access
• O(n) insert/remove in the middle

Use linked list when: frequent O(1) head/tail insertions/removals, implementing stacks/queues. Use List for everything else in practice.

questions.question: Implement Stack and Queue in Dart and explain common interview problems.
questions.answer:
Stack — LIFO (Last In, First Out): push, pop, peek — all O(1)
Queue — FIFO (First In, First Out): enqueue, dequeue — all O(1)

Both can be implemented with Dart's built-in ListQueue from dart:collection.

Common interview problems that use stacks:
• Valid parentheses
• Evaluate RPN expressions
• Next greater element
• Min stack (with O(1) getMin)

Common problems with queues:
• BFS traversal
• Level-order tree traversal
• Sliding window maximum

questions.question: Explain the two-pointer and sliding window techniques with examples.
questions.answer:
Two-pointer — use two indices to traverse a sorted array or string. Avoids O(n²) nested loops.

Sliding window — maintain a "window" (subarray/substring) and slide it across the data. Useful for problems asking for max/min/sum/count within a contiguous range.

Both reduce O(n²) brute-force solutions to O(n) — critical for interview performance questions.

questions.question: How do you traverse a binary tree (DFS/BFS) and what are the patterns for common tree problems?
questions.answer:
Trees are second nature in interviews. The two traversal families cover most questions:
