DFS (Depth-First Search) — go deep before wide. Three orderings:
• Pre-order   — visit node, then left, then right (e.g. clone a tree)
• In-order    — left, then node, then right (sorted order on a BST)
• Post-order  — left, right, then node (e.g. delete tree, compute sums bottom-up)

BFS (Breadth-First Search) — level by level. Use a queue. Best for shortest path, level-order, finding the closest match.

Implementation choices:
• DFS — easy as recursion; in deep or pathological trees use an explicit stack to avoid Dart's recursion-limit
• BFS — always iterative with a Queue (`ListQueue` from dart:collection)

Common problems that map to one of these:
• Max/min depth → BFS (early stop on min depth) or post-order DFS
• Same tree / mirror tree → simultaneous pre-order DFS
• BST validate → in-order DFS with running prev pointer (sorted check)
• Lowest common ancestor → post-order DFS
• Serialize/deserialize → pre-order DFS with null markers
• Right side view / level averages → BFS with level snapshots

questions.question: What are the canonical graph algorithms (BFS shortest path, Dijkstra, Topological sort)?
questions.answer:
Graph problems repeat in interviews. Three algorithms cover most of them:

BFS shortest path (unweighted graph):
• Use a queue. Track distance per node. When you pop a node, all nodes one step further go in next.
• Time O(V + E), space O(V)
• Examples: shortest steps in a grid, fewest transformations, web of friends

Dijkstra (non-negative weights):
• Like BFS but with a priority queue keyed by accumulated cost
• Don't use it on graphs with negative weights — use Bellman-Ford
• Time O((V + E) log V) with a binary heap
• Examples: routing with travel times, cheapest flight

Topological sort (DAG only):
• Linear ordering where each edge u→v puts u before v
• Two algorithms: Kahn's (BFS-based, with in-degrees) and DFS-based (post-order reverse)
• Detect cycles: if you can't produce all nodes, there's a cycle
• Examples: build/dependency order, course prerequisites, task scheduling

Dart specific notes:
• `dart:collection` ships ListQueue (BFS) but no priority queue — use the `collection` pub package's `HeapPriorityQueue` for Dijkstra
• Adjacency: `Map<Node, List<Edge>>` is idiomatic for sparse graphs

questions.question: How do you recognize when a problem is dynamic programming, and how do you go from recursion to DP?
questions.answer:
DP applies when:
1. The problem has OPTIMAL SUBSTRUCTURE — the answer is built from answers to smaller subproblems
2. Subproblems OVERLAP — naïve recursion would re-solve the same input many times

If both hold, you can either memoize (top-down) or fill a table (bottom-up). If only #1 holds, you have a divide-and-conquer problem (mergesort), not DP.

The upgrade path:
1. Write the recursive brute force first. Don't optimize. Get correctness on a small input.
2. Identify the STATE — the minimal info that decides the answer (often: indices, remaining capacity, last-picked-element)
3. Add a `memo` map keyed by that state — top-down DP. Same code, exponential→polynomial.
4. Optionally rewrite as a table (bottom-up) — same complexity, no recursion stack, often easier to reason about iteration order.
5. Optimize space — many DPs only need the last 1–2 rows of the table.

Classic problems:
• Knapsack (0/1 and unbounded)
• Longest common subsequence / Edit distance
• Coin change (count or min coins)
• Climbing stairs / Fibonacci variants
• Word break / palindrome partitioning
• Best time to buy/sell stock — k transactions

Interview tip: state the state and the recurrence in words BEFORE writing code. "dp[i][w] = the best value using items 0..i with capacity w" — that single sentence is often what they want to hear.

questions.question: When and how do you use a HashMap, HashSet, or LinkedHashMap in Dart?
questions.answer:
Map and Set in Dart are DEFAULT-ORDERED (LinkedHash) — they preserve insertion order. That makes them strictly more predictable than the unordered hash structures in some other languages, at a tiny per-op cost. Knowing the variants matters when correctness depends on order or when you need raw speed.

Variants:
• `<K, V>{}` / `Map.from`           — LinkedHashMap, insertion-ordered, default
• `HashMap<K, V>()` (dart:collection) — unordered, slightly faster
• `LinkedHashMap` — explicit insertion-ordered
• `SplayTreeMap` — sorted by key (O(log n) ops)
• `<T>{}` / `Set.from`               — LinkedHashSet, insertion-ordered, default
• `HashSet<T>()` — unordered
• `SplayTreeSet` — sorted

When to pick:
• Counting / dedup / lookup tables → default Map / Set is plenty (LinkedHash)
• You need to iterate in sorted order without paying re-sort costs → SplayTreeMap/Set
• You don't care about order and want a tiny perf win → HashMap/HashSet
• You need a fixed iteration order tied to insertion → LinkedHashMap (the default; explicit when documenting intent)

Gotcha — using a custom class as a Map key:
• Without overriding == and hashCode, identity equality is used (two equal-looking objects with different references won't match)
• Override BOTH and keep them consistent — equal objects must hash to the same bucket
• Or use freezed / equatable to generate them

questions.question: When is recursion in Dart safe, and when must you switch to an explicit iterative approach?
questions.answer:
The Dart VM has a finite call stack — typically a few thousand frames in production, larger in debug. Recursion that goes deeper than the stack tolerates throws `StackOverflowError`. Some functions are also fine with mutual recursion but become problematic when input shape pushes recursion to extremes.

When recursion is safe:
• Tree traversals where the tree is balanced (depth ~ log n)
• Algorithms with depth bounded by a small constant (e.g. parse expressions, walk JSON)
• Functional pipelines on small inputs

When recursion is NOT safe:
• Linked-list traversal that may exceed thousands of nodes — depth is O(n), n unbounded
• File system or directory walks on real filesystems with deep nesting
• Graph traversal with no upper bound on path length

Swap to iteration with:
• An explicit stack (`List<T>` works as a stack via add/removeLast) for DFS
• A `ListQueue` for BFS
• Tail-call shape: collect intermediate results in a loop with an accumulator

Dart does NOT do tail-call optimization. Even a perfectly tail-recursive function can overflow the stack — switch to a loop, do not rely on TCO.

A good interview cue: when the input size could be "the whole user's filesystem", "a chat message thread of unknown length", "all rows in a CSV" — go iterative from the start.

questions.question: How do Platform Channels work? Implement a MethodChannel example.
questions.answer:
Platform Channels allow Flutter (Dart) to communicate with native code (Android/Kotlin, iOS/Swift).

Three channel types:
• MethodChannel — request/response. Flutter calls native method, awaits result. Best for: battery level, camera, sensors.
• EventChannel — continuous stream FROM native. Best for: accelerometer, location updates.
• BasicMessageChannel — raw message passing with a custom codec.

Both sides must use the SAME channel name string. Messages are serialized via StandardMessageCodec (supports: null, bool, int, double, String, Uint8List, List, Map).

questions.question: How do you write a Flutter plugin from scratch?
questions.answer:
A Flutter plugin is a Dart package that wraps native platform code (Android + iOS) using platform channels. Publishing a plugin makes it reusable across projects.

Plugin structure:
• lib/ — Dart API (what app developers use)
• android/ — Kotlin implementation
• ios/ — Swift implementation
• test/ — unit and integration tests
• example/ — runnable demo app

Create with: flutter create --template=plugin --platforms=android,ios my_plugin

The Dart side defines the interface and delegates to native via MethodChannel.

questions.question: What is EventChannel and when do you use it instead of MethodChannel?
questions.answer:
EventChannel is for a continuous, push-based stream of events from native to Dart. Where MethodChannel is request/response, EventChannel is publish/subscribe.

When it fits:
• Sensor data — accelerometer, gyroscope, GPS
• Bluetooth or BLE notifications
• Hardware buttons, NFC reads, system battery state changes
• Foreground service progress (download, audio playback position)

Mechanics:
• Dart calls `receiveBroadcastStream()` — native receives `onListen(arguments, EventSink)`
• Native pushes events via `events.success(payload)`; errors via `events.error(...)`
• When the Dart subscription is cancelled, native receives `onCancel(arguments)` — release resources here
• Multiple Dart listeners share one native subscription on the broadcast stream — design for fan-out

When not to: a one-shot value (use `MethodChannel.invokeMethod`); high-frequency hot data over 1kHz (use FFI + ring buffer instead — channel serialization will dominate cost).

questions.question: What is Pigeon and why use it over hand-written MethodChannels?
questions.answer:
Pigeon is the official Flutter code-gen tool for type-safe platform channels. You declare the interface once in Dart, and Pigeon emits Dart, Kotlin, and Swift bindings that talk to each other through the existing channel mechanism.

Why it beats raw MethodChannel:
• Type safety on BOTH sides — no untyped Map<String, dynamic>, no string method names sprinkled across the codebase
• Refactor support — rename a method and the analyzer fails on every caller in Dart, Kotlin, and Swift simultaneously
• Custom data classes are codegen'd as @HostApi/@FlutterApi DTOs — equality, copyWith, JSON-style codec for free
• Bidirectional — `@HostApi` is Dart→native, `@FlutterApi` is native→Dart callbacks
• Async by default — generated methods return Future on Dart side, suspend/Combine-style on native

When to use raw channels:
• Quick spike or one-off prototype
• Existing plugin you don't own

In anything beyond a couple of methods, Pigeon is the right default.

questions.question: How does the threading model work for platform channels, and how do you avoid blocking the UI?
questions.answer:
Channels look async to Dart, but the native side runs on the platform thread (main thread on iOS, main thread on Android by default). If your Kotlin/Swift handler does CPU or IO work synchronously, the platform thread blocks — and on Android that means jank for the entire app.

Threading rules:
• Dart: every `invokeMethod` is async — returns a Future. The send is non-blocking; the wait is.
• Android: handlers run on the platform's main thread. Move heavy work off — `kotlinx.coroutines` with Dispatchers.IO, or AsyncTask-style executor pools — and call `result.success()` from any thread.
• iOS: handlers also run on the main queue. Push work to a background queue with DispatchQueue, and call back on the main queue (Flutter's BinaryMessenger requires it on iOS).
• Result must be called exactly once. Calling twice is an exception. If your handler returns synchronously without calling result, Dart hangs forever.

Further: heavy native callbacks fanning into Dart with EventChannel can saturate the platform thread too. Throttle on the native side or batch events.

questions.question: How should you handle errors across a platform channel?
questions.answer:
Errors are first-class on channels — both sides have an explicit way to signal failure. Wire them up correctly and your Dart code can branch on a typed PlatformException; skip them and exceptions become Map-shaped soup.

Dart side:
• Native success → Future completes with the value
• Native `result.error(code, message, details)` → Future throws `PlatformException(code, message, details)`
• Native `result.notImplemented()` → throws `MissingPluginException` (e.g. running on a platform where the plugin isn't registered)
• A timeout is on you — channel calls have no built-in deadline

Native side:
• Always call result exactly once. Catch your exceptions and convert them to result.error.
• Use stable string codes (NETWORK_TIMEOUT, PERMISSION_DENIED) — not localized messages. Localize in Dart.
• Include enough detail in `details` for diagnostics, but never put PII or tokens there.

Design tip: model expected failures (no permission, no network) as result.error with a known code; reserve thrown exceptions for bugs.

questions.question: When should you choose dart:ffi over a MethodChannel?
questions.answer:
MethodChannel and FFI both cross the Dart/native boundary, but they solve different problems.

MethodChannel:
• Talks to Kotlin/Swift/ObjC — anything written for the platform's Java VM or Objective-C runtime
• Each call serializes through the StandardCodec and crosses to the platform thread — typical latency on the order of milliseconds
• Best for: SDKs you must integrate (Firebase, Maps, Camera) and platform UIs (permission dialogs, system UI)

dart:ffi:
• Talks to plain C ABI — pure native libraries (.so/.dylib/.dll)
• Direct call into C — sub-microsecond latency, no serialization, no thread hop
• Memory is on you — calloc/free, raw pointers, no GC across the boundary
• Best for: high-throughput pure logic in C/C++/Rust (codecs, image processing, hashing, ML)

Decision rule:
• Need to talk to Activity / View / UIWindow / system permissions → MethodChannel (or Pigeon over MethodChannel)
• Need to call a CPU-heavy library at high frequency → FFI
• Need both? Many production apps do — FFI for the hot path, MethodChannel for the platform-API parts

Note: many official plugins (sqlite3, image, native_video_player) already use FFI under the hood — you don't need to reach for it directly unless you ship your own native code.

questions.question: How do you call platform code from a background isolate?
questions.answer:
Platform channels are bound to a specific BinaryMessenger, which by default lives on the root isolate. Background isolates (Isolate.spawn / Isolate.run / compute) cannot just instantiate a MethodChannel — they have no messenger and would silently fail.

Flutter 3.7+ ships BackgroundIsolateBinaryMessenger which lets a background isolate piggy-back on the root isolate's messenger:
• On the root isolate: capture `RootIsolateToken.instance!`
• Send it to the background isolate as part of its initial message
• In the background isolate, call `BackgroundIsolateBinaryMessenger.ensureInitialized(token)` BEFORE creating any MethodChannel
• After that, MethodChannel calls work as normal — the message hops to root, then to platform, then back

Why you might still avoid this:
• It still serializes through the root isolate. If the root is busy, the channel call queues.
• For pure CPU work, move the work to the isolate but NOT the platform calls — fetch raw bytes on root, hand them to the isolate, return processed bytes.
• For background fetch / WorkManager scenarios, the isolate is created BY native code; it must register a Dart entry point and call ensureInitialized before its first platform call.

questions.question: What is BasicMessageChannel and when does it beat MethodChannel?
questions.answer:
BasicMessageChannel is the lowest-level channel: it sends arbitrary messages with no method-call envelope and a configurable codec. MethodChannel is just a convention on top of it — "a Map with a method name and arguments".

When it's the right tool:
• Symmetric, peer-to-peer messaging — both sides send and receive on equal footing (no "caller" vs "handler")
• High-volume binary payloads where StandardMessageCodec is wasteful (use BinaryCodec to skip Map wrapping)
• Custom serialization — you have a JSON or Protobuf representation already and don't want to repack into the standard codec
• Bridging non-method-call protocols (raw events, command pipelines)

Codec choices:
• StandardMessageCodec — default; supports primitives + List + Map
• StringCodec — UTF-8 strings only (lightweight)
• JSONMessageCodec — passes through dart:convert
• BinaryCodec — passes raw ByteData; you serialize yourself (Protobuf, FlatBuffers)

When NOT to: vast majority of apps. MethodChannel is more discoverable; Pigeon is even better for typed interfaces. Reach for BasicMessageChannel only when you have a specific reason.

questions.question: What causes jank in Flutter and how do you optimize performance?
questions.answer:
Jank = dropped frames (< 60fps / >16ms per frame). The UI thread must complete layout+paint in 16ms.

Common jank causes:
1. Heavy computation on the UI thread (should use isolates)
2. Excessive widget rebuilds (setState rebuilds too much)
3. Large images not sized/cached properly
4. Missing const constructors
5. Synchronous I/O
6. Memory pressure causing GC pauses

Optimization tools:
• Flutter DevTools → Performance tab, Rebuild counts
• Flutter Inspector → Highlight repaints
• --profile mode: flutter run --profile
• Timeline events, flame charts

questions.question: How do you detect and fix memory leaks in Flutter?
questions.answer:
Common sources of memory leaks in Flutter:

1. StreamSubscription not cancelled in dispose()
2. AnimationController not disposed
3. TextEditingController / FocusNode not disposed
4. Timer not cancelled
5. GlobalKey held in static variables
6. Closures capturing large objects
7. Image cache growing unbounded

Detection tools:
• Flutter DevTools → Memory tab → heap snapshots
• Leak Tracker package (flutter_test)
• Observatory → allocation profiling

questions.question: How do you profile a Flutter app and read DevTools timeline correctly?
questions.answer:
Performance work without measurement is folklore. Always start by profiling, never by guessing.

The rules:
• Always profile in `--profile` mode. Debug mode is up to 10x slower for irrelevant reasons (assertions, no AOT) and `--release` strips the timeline.
• Run on a real low-end device when possible — your iPhone 15 Pro hides problems your users on a 2018 Android will hit.
• Reproduce a steady scenario (scroll the same list, replay the same animation) so the timeline is comparable across runs.

DevTools tabs and what they tell you:
• Performance — frame timeline. Green = under budget, yellow/red = jank. Click a frame to see the build/layout/paint/raster cost
• CPU profiler — flame chart of where Dart code spent time. Wide bars are hot functions; sample at high frequency for short events.
• Memory — heap snapshots, GC pauses. A growing heap between identical actions usually means a leak.
• Inspector → "Highlight Repaints" — shows which layers actually repainted; surprising repaints point to missing RepaintBoundary or non-const subtrees.
• Inspector → "Show Performance Overlay" — two stacked bars (UI / GPU) on the running app, frame-by-frame.

Reading the timeline:
• A red frame on UI bar = build/layout took too long → check the CPU profile for that frame
• A red frame on GPU bar but green UI = paint/raster too expensive → check for backdrop filters, large images, opacity layers
• Pattern of intermittent yellow = GC; check Memory tab for allocation rate

questions.question: How do you cut unnecessary rebuilds — const, selector pattern, RepaintBoundary?
questions.answer:
Most jank in Flutter apps is caused by widgets rebuilding when they didn't need to. Three tools, each at a different level, do most of the heavy lifting.

1) const constructors:
• A widget annotated `const` with const-constructible arguments is identity-compared by Flutter — if the same const reaches the same slot twice, the framework skips the entire rebuild of that subtree
• Lint `prefer_const_constructors` and `prefer_const_constructors_in_immutables` catches the easy cases — turn them on

2) Fine-grained reads (selector pattern):
• Watching a coarse store rebuilds the consumer on any change. With Riverpod, use `ref.watch(provider.select((s) => s.field))`. With Provider, use `Selector<T, Sel>`. With BLoC, use `BlocSelector` or `buildWhen`.
• If your selector doesn't help, the underlying state is too coarse — split it into focused providers/cubits

3) RepaintBoundary:
• Different from rebuild: a repaint boundary forces Flutter to allocate a separate compositing layer. If a sibling animates and your subtree is expensive to paint, putting your subtree under a RepaintBoundary stops it from being repainted just because the sibling changed.
• Don't sprinkle it everywhere — each boundary costs memory. Add one only at known-expensive boundaries with measured wins.

Other high-leverage moves:
• Lift `setState` as low in the tree as possible — only the StatefulWidget rebuilds, not its parents
• Avoid creating new closures or new objects in `build()` — extract to fields or `final` locals where possible
• ListView/GridView.builder, not ListView with a children list, for any list bigger than what fits on screen

questions.question: How do you optimize image rendering — sizing, caching, and codecs?
questions.answer:
Images are the single biggest performance lever for content-heavy apps. The two failures: loading a much larger image than you display (memory + decode time), and re-decoding the same image on every rebuild (CPU, GC).

Downsizing — the most important rule:
• `Image.network`/`Image.asset` accepts `cacheWidth`/`cacheHeight`. If your widget shows a 200×200 thumbnail, set `cacheWidth: 400` (2x for high-DPI). Without this, Flutter decodes the full 4000×4000 source into RAM.
• Pair with `BoxFit.cover` for clean display — the cached size is the decoded size, not the displayed size.

Caching:
• Default `ImageCache` keeps decoded bitmaps in memory. Limit it (`maximumSize`, `maximumSizeBytes`) to prevent uncontrolled growth on image-heavy screens.
• `cached_network_image` adds a disk cache so reopens are instant and offline-ish. Configure its size separately.
• ListView.builder of images: scrolling out and back in re-displays from cache; only first display pays decode cost.

Codecs and formats:
• Prefer WebP/AVIF over JPEG/PNG when your CDN supports it — same quality, 20–50% smaller, faster decode
• SVG via flutter_svg adds runtime cost; for large/complex SVGs, ship pre-rendered raster assets
• Lottie / Rive for animated assets — much cheaper than playing GIF/WebM in flutter

When rendering is still slow:
• Use `RepaintBoundary` to isolate the image so neighbor rebuilds don't repaint it
• `precacheImage` during page transitions — decode the next page's hero image while the current one is still on screen
• Display a low-res placeholder (frame_blur, BlurHash) until the full image is ready
• If your list is image-heavy, consider FadeInImage with a memory-cached placeholder

questions.question: When does Opacity / BackdropFilter / shadows hurt, and how do you fix it?
questions.answer:
Some Flutter widgets force expensive raster work — they look harmless but turn the GPU into the bottleneck. If your `--profile` shows raster bar > UI bar, this is where to look.

Opacity widget:
• Wraps its subtree in a saveLayer compositing operation — every pixel is painted into an offscreen buffer, then alpha-blended onto the parent
• Cost is roughly proportional to the painted area
• Fixes:
  - For text/icons, use `color.withOpacity(...)` directly (shader blend, cheap)
  - For images, use `Image.opacity` parameter (cheap)
  - For animation, use `FadeTransition` / `AnimatedOpacity` — they smartly disable saveLayer when alpha is 0 or 1

BackdropFilter (frosted glass):
• ImageFilter.blur creates a separate compositing pass — VERY expensive on the GPU, especially for large blur sigmas
• Fixes:
  - Use a smaller blurred area (only the affected region, not full screen)
  - Pre-render a static blurred snapshot when the underlying content rarely changes
  - Cache via RepaintBoundary so the blur isn't recomputed when nothing under it changed
  - Reduce blur sigma; use stylized colors instead of true blur

DropShadows on lots of items:
• Each shadow is a separate paint op; lists of shadowed cards multiply that cost per frame
• Fixes:
  - Hardcode the shadow into a 9-slice background image where possible
  - Use a single subtle border or a flat "tinted background" instead of shadow
  - For long lists, limit shadows to focused items (selected/hover) only

ClipPath / ClipRRect:
• Clips often force a saveLayer too, particularly with antialiasing on. Prefer `Clip.hardEdge` if visual quality allows; better, replace with a Container decorated with rounded BoxDecoration (no saveLayer required).

questions.question: What's the difference between Skia and Impeller and how do shader compilation jank avoidance work?
questions.answer:
Flutter has two rendering backends:

Skia — the original 2D engine (Chrome, Android). Mature, broad. On Android Skia uses runtime shader compilation: the first time it draws a particular blend/clip combination, it compiles the shader on the GPU thread, which can take 5–50ms — visible as a single dropped frame on first scroll/animation. Solution: SKSL warmup files captured during a scripted dry-run and shipped with the app, replayed at startup so all shaders are pre-compiled.

Impeller — Flutter's newer renderer:
• Default on iOS since 3.10
• Default on Android since 3.16 (with fallbacks for older devices)
• Uses precompiled shaders shipped with the engine — eliminates first-run shader jank entirely
• Better consistency across frames (less GC pressure on platform-thread allocations)
• Slight differences in pixel rendering vs Skia — usually invisible, but golden-image tests may need updates

What you should know:
• On modern Flutter, you usually don't need SKSL warmup — Impeller solves it. If you still see first-frame jank, check whether your build uses Impeller (`--enable-impeller` on Android, default on iOS).
• If you must support old Android devices that fall back to Skia, capture and ship SKSL warmups.
• Impeller has a few known regressions (custom shaders, rare BackdropFilter cases) — track engine release notes when upgrading Flutter SDK.

General shader/raster cost reduction tips also apply to both: avoid huge BlendMode pipelines, avoid shadows on hundreds of objects, prefer hard-edge clips.
questions.code_example:
# Capture SKSL warmup (Skia only)
# 1) Run on a real device in --profile mode
flutter run --profile --cache-sksl --purge-persistent-cache
# 2) Exercise all the screens/animations that produced jank
# 3) Quit the app, then save the cache
flutter screenshot --type=skia --observatory-url=<observatory_url>  # not the right cmd, illustrative
# Real workflow uses 'flutter run --cache-sksl' followed by:
flutter build apk --bundle-sksl-path=flutter_01.sksl.json

# Verify Impeller is on (Android)
flutter run -d <android> --enable-impeller
# AndroidManifest <meta-data
#   android:name="io.flutter.embedding.android.EnableImpeller"
#   android:value="true"/>

# Inspect renderer at runtime
// import 'dart:ui' as ui;
// print(ui.PlatformDispatcher.instance.implicitView!.platformDispatcher.runtimeType);
// More commonly, check Flutter logs at startup — engine prints which backend is active.

# In CI: regression test that scrolls a complex page and asserts shader-warmup
# behaviour by counting frames > 16ms in the first 2 seconds of interaction.

questions.question: How do you keep startup time low — splash, deferred work, deferred components?
questions.answer:
Time-to-interactive on cold start is one of the most-watched mobile KPIs. Three layers contribute: native pre-Flutter initialization, Flutter engine warm-up, and your app's first-frame work. Each has its own knobs.

Native splash (pre-Flutter):
• Use `flutter_native_splash` to render a static image as the system splash. The OS shows it the instant the user taps the icon, before anything Flutter exists.
• Without it, Android shows a blank white window for ~300–800ms while the engine boots.

Main entry — keep it lean:
• Don't `await` IO in main() unless absolutely required. Hydrate prefs, theme, and other quick reads — but kick off heavier loads (auth refresh, feature flags fetch) AFTER `runApp` so the first frame paints first.
• Avoid synchronous plugin calls that hit a platform channel before runApp — they add tens of ms.
• Splash screen widget shows immediately, hydrates state, then transitions to the real screen.

Deferred initialization:
• Heavy services (analytics, crash reporter, websocket) — initialize on the first frame post-paint via `WidgetsBinding.instance.addPostFrameCallback`. The user sees the UI render before these add overhead.
• Lazy singletons (get_it / Riverpod providers) — initialized on first use, not on app start.
• Disk caches — initialize on demand, not eagerly.

Deferred components (Android only):
• `flutter build appbundle` supports split components — modules of your app that are NOT in the initial install but are downloaded on demand from Play Store
• Useful for occasional features (advanced editor, AR scanner) that bloat install size
• Not needed for most apps — but for big apps, can shave 30–50% off the install download

questions.question: How do you build a long, smooth scrolling list without jank?
questions.answer:
The performance gap between a hand-rolled list and a properly-built one is enormous. The wrong tool gets you 10fps at 1000 items; the right one gets you 60fps at 100k.

Must-haves:
• ListView.builder / ListView.separated — never `ListView(children: [...])` for any list bigger than what fits on screen. Builder lazily creates only visible widgets.
• Stable item heights when possible — `ListView.builder(itemExtent: 72, ...)` lets Flutter skip per-item layout. Even better: precise height enables instant jump-to-index on scroll.
• Use `Sliver`-based lists (CustomScrollView + SliverList) when you mix sections; they share a single Scrollable and render more efficiently than nested ListViews.

Item-level discipline:
• `const` constructors throughout the item subtree — Flutter skips identical const widgets entirely
• Avoid creating closures or new objects in itemBuilder — pre-extract handlers to fields/static
• Use `cacheExtent` to render slightly off-screen items — smoother scroll, slightly more memory
• Don't rebuild items on global state changes — use selector patterns so only the items whose data changed rebuild

Image discipline (lists are usually image-heavy):
• `cacheWidth`/`cacheHeight` — decode at the displayed size, not the source size
• `cached_network_image` for memory + disk cache
• `RepaintBoundary` around image tiles to isolate raster work

When scrolling 1000s+ items:
• Add a search/filter UI so the user doesn't have to scroll through all of them
• Paginate with `infinite_scroll_pagination` — fetch next page when the last item appears, not all up front
• Virtualization-related tools: `flutter_list_view` and other community alternatives if you need anchor-based jumps

questions.question: Explain Riverpod: providers, WidgetRef, and AsyncNotifier.
questions.answer:
Riverpod is a complete Provider rewrite with compile-time safety.

Key advantages over Provider:
• No context needed to read providers outside widgets
• Compile-time type safety — no runtime ProviderNotFoundException
• Auto-dispose — no memory leaks from forgotten providers
• DevTools integration — view provider state

Provider types:
• Provider<T> — read-only derived value
• StateProvider<T> — simple mutable state
• NotifierProvider — complex sync state (Notifier class)
• AsyncNotifierProvider — async state with loading/error/data
• StreamProvider — stream of values
• FutureProvider — one-time async value

questions.question: Explain Riverpod family modifier and how to override providers in tests.
questions.answer:
family — parametrize a provider by an argument. Creates a separate provider instance per parameter value. Each instance is cached and auto-disposed when no longer watched.

Use for: fetching by ID, filtering by category, locale-specific providers.

Testing with Riverpod — override any provider in ProviderScope for unit and widget tests. This replaces the real implementation with a mock.

questions.question: What's the difference between ref.watch, ref.read, and ref.listen?
questions.answer:
All three live on `Ref` (in providers) and `WidgetRef` (in widgets), but they have very different semantics. Picking the wrong one is the #1 source of subtle Riverpod bugs.

ref.watch(provider):
• Subscribes — when the provider changes, this provider/widget rebuilds
• Use in `build()` and inside provider definitions to express dependencies
• Reading multiple times in one build returns the same value — it's idempotent

ref.read(provider):
• One-shot read — does NOT subscribe
• Use in callbacks (button onPressed, gesture handlers, lifecycle hooks) where you don't want a rebuild
• Never call inside `build()` — you'll get the value once and then miss updates

ref.listen(provider, callback):
• Subscribes for SIDE EFFECTS — does NOT cause a rebuild
• Use for navigation, snackbars, dialogs, logging — anything that should fire on state change but isn't part of the UI itself
• Callback receives `(previous, next)` — useful for transition logic

Mnemonic: watch = render, read = act, listen = react.

questions.question: How does autoDispose work, and when do you need keepAlive?
questions.answer:
By default a Riverpod provider is cached forever in the ProviderContainer. `.autoDispose` flips that: the provider is disposed as soon as no widget or other provider is watching it. This is the right default for screen-scoped state — when the user leaves the screen, the cache goes with them.

Lifecycle:
• When watcher count drops to zero, autoDispose runs `ref.onDispose` callbacks immediately, then frees the state
• When watched again, the provider's build runs from scratch — fresh fetch, fresh state

When you DON'T want disposal:
• A long task should outlive a transient navigation (downloading a file, ongoing upload)
• You want to debounce — don't wipe on a one-frame gap when the user pops/pushes

Knobs:
• `ref.keepAlive()` — call once to make THIS instance immortal until manually invalidated. Returns a `KeepAliveLink` you can `.close()` later to allow disposal.
• `ref.onDispose(() {...})` — register cleanup (cancel subscriptions, close streams)
• Without `.autoDispose`, the provider is kept forever; this is fine for app-wide singletons (the auth notifier, an analytics client).

questions.question: How does Riverpod code generation (riverpod_generator) work?
questions.answer:
riverpod_generator is a build_runner code-gen layer over flutter_riverpod. You annotate functions or classes with @riverpod and the generator emits the matching provider declaration. It's optional but quickly becoming the recommended way to use Riverpod 2.

What it does:
• `@riverpod String greeting(GreetingRef ref) => 'hi'` → generates `greetingProvider`
• Function with parameters becomes a family automatically — no `.family` boilerplate
• A class annotated with `@riverpod` becomes a Notifier/AsyncNotifier; methods you add are public mutators
• `@Riverpod(keepAlive: true)` makes it non-autoDispose; default is autoDispose

Why use it:
• Eliminates the boilerplate of choosing the right provider type — the generator picks Notifier/AsyncNotifier/Stream from your return type
• Refactor-safe — rename a function and the generated provider name follows
• Encourages best practices (autoDispose by default, family inferred from args)

Setup: add `riverpod_generator` and `build_runner` to dev_dependencies, then `dart run build_runner watch -d`.

questions.question: When should you pick Notifier vs AsyncNotifier vs StateProvider?
questions.answer:
All three hold mutable state. The choice is about how the state is created and what consumers should see during transitions.

StateProvider<T>:
• Trivial mutable values — a search string, a checkbox, a tab index
• Direct setter via `ref.read(p.notifier).state = newValue`
• No place to put logic; if you need any, you've outgrown StateProvider — move to Notifier

Notifier<T> (sync):
• Synchronous, in-memory state with logic — a cart, a filter set, a wizard step
• Methods on the class become the mutators (`add`, `remove`, `next`)
• `build()` returns T directly — no AsyncValue wrapping
• Use when state isn't loaded from IO

AsyncNotifier<T> (async):
• State is loaded asynchronously and consumers should see loading/error/data
• `build()` returns Future<T>; the provider's value is `AsyncValue<T>`
• Mutators typically wrap `AsyncValue.guard(() async {...})` to keep the type safe
• Use for any state hydrated from an API/DB and updated by user actions

Rule of thumb: start with the simplest. StateProvider for plain values, Notifier for synchronous logic, AsyncNotifier the moment IO is involved.

questions.question: How do you control rebuilds with select() and Equatable-style equality?
questions.answer:
By default, watching a provider rebuilds the consumer on ANY change to the state. For coarse state (a big User object, a list, a settings bag) that's wasteful — most fields don't matter to a given widget.

Two levers:

1) `ref.watch(provider.select((s) => s.field))`:
• Computes a derived value and only rebuilds when THAT changes (by ==)
• Works on any provider whose state is a value type with stable equality
• On AsyncValue: `provider.select((s) => s.valueOrNull?.field)` — handle null/loading explicitly

2) Stable equality on your state:
• Plain Dart classes use identity equality — every rebuild creates a new instance, every selector "changes"
• Override `==` and `hashCode`, or use `freezed` / `equatable` — the state class becomes value-typed
• Lists/Maps are still by reference — wrap them in BuiltValue or store as IList/IMap (fast_immutable_collections) for content equality

When .select isn't enough: split your provider. A coarse provider with many unrelated fields is a code smell — break it into focused providers and let watchers pick exactly the one they care about.

questions.question: How do ProviderScope overrides work — at app start vs at runtime?
questions.answer:
Overrides replace a provider's implementation within a scope. The scope is the ProviderContainer (in tests / pure Dart) or the nearest ProviderScope ancestor (in widgets). Overrides are how you inject mocks for tests, how you swap an implementation per environment, and how you scope state to a subtree.

Flavors of override:
• `overrideWithValue(value)` — replaces the produced value with a constant
• `overrideWith((ref) => ...)` — replaces the provider's build with a different builder; the only option for Notifier/AsyncNotifier providers
• Mark the overridden provider with the same shape — e.g. an `Provider<ApiClient>` can be overridden with another Provider<ApiClient>; type mismatches are compile errors

App-start vs runtime:
• At app start: pass `overrides:` to the root ProviderScope. This is how you inject the env config, the dependency graph for the chosen flavor, or test-only fakes
• At runtime / per-subtree: nest a ProviderScope deeper in the widget tree. Children use the overridden version; everything outside still sees the parent's. Useful for multi-tenant or sandboxed sub-apps
• Switching at runtime by remounting the ProviderScope with a new key forces a clean reset of all autoDispose providers

Gotcha: overriding a provider at the root affects every dependent. If you override only at a child scope, providers UP the dependency chain are NOT recomputed under the override unless they themselves are also overridden.

questions.question: How do you fire side effects (navigation, snackbars, dialogs) from Riverpod state?
questions.answer:
Putting Navigator.push or showSnackBar inside `build()` is a bug — build can be called many times and side effects must fire exactly once per state transition. Riverpod gives you `ref.listen` for exactly this: subscribe to changes, run an imperative effect, do not rebuild the widget.

Pattern:
• Use `ref.listen` inside `build()` — it's safe to call there because it doesn't make build dependent on the value
• The callback receives `(previous, next)` — branch on transitions, not on snapshots
• For one-shot success/error states, model them as a transient `AsyncValue<Result>` and react to the new error/data — clear the state afterwards if you want to allow re-trigger
• In a Notifier, prefer not to call Navigator from inside the notifier itself — it makes the notifier coupled to the widget tree. Surface a state and let a widget react to it.

For app-wide effects (deep links, push notifications), expose a stream from a provider and let a top-level ConsumerWidget listen on it.

questions.question: What are the key security best practices for Flutter mobile apps?
questions.answer:
Mobile app security layers:

1. Data at Rest:
• flutter_secure_storage for tokens/passwords (Keychain/Keystore)
• SQLCipher for encrypted databases
• Never SharedPreferences for sensitive data

2. Data in Transit:
• HTTPS only
• SSL/Certificate pinning (prevent MITM)
• Certificate validation

3. Code Security:
• Code obfuscation (--obfuscate flag)
• Don't log sensitive data
• No hardcoded secrets (use .env or remote config)

4. Authentication:
• Short-lived access tokens + refresh tokens
• Biometric auth (local_auth package)
• Jailbreak/root detection

5. OWASP Mobile Top 10: Improper Credential Usage, Insecure Data Storage, Insecure Communication, Insufficient Input/Output Validation

questions.question: What is SSL/certificate pinning and how do you implement it in Flutter?
questions.answer:
Certificate pinning prevents Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks by hardcoding the expected server certificate or public key in the app. Even if an attacker installs a custom CA, the app rejects their certificate.

Two pinning approaches:
1. Certificate pinning — pin the full certificate (changes when cert renews ~1 year)
2. Public key pinning — pin just the public key (survives cert renewals, more flexible)

Implementation in Flutter:
• Override HttpClient's badCertificateCallback
• Or use http_certificate_pinning package
• Or custom Dio adapter

Warning: If you pin incorrectly or the certificate changes without updating the app, your app loses network access. Always include a backup pin.

questions.question: How does OAuth 2.0 with PKCE work for mobile apps and why is it required?
questions.answer:
Public clients (mobile apps, SPAs) cannot keep a client_secret confidential — the secret would be embedded in the app binary, trivially extracted. PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange, RFC 7636) replaces the client_secret with a per-flow proof, so a stolen authorization code is useless without the matching verifier.

The flow:
1. App generates a random `code_verifier` (43–128 chars, URL-safe)
2. App computes `code_challenge = base64url(SHA256(code_verifier))`
3. App opens the auth URL with `code_challenge` + `code_challenge_method=S256`
4. User signs in; the IdP redirects back to the app with `code`
5. App exchanges the code at the token endpoint, sending the original `code_verifier`
6. The IdP recomputes SHA256(verifier), compares to the stored challenge, issues tokens only if they match

If an attacker intercepts the redirect URI (registered scheme hijack) and steals the code, they cannot exchange it because they don't know the verifier.

Other requirements for mobile:
• Use the system browser (ASWebAuthenticationSession on iOS, Custom Tabs on Android) — never WebView. WebView can be inspected, drained for credentials, and triggers anti-phishing flags.
• Use claimed/universal links for the redirect URI when possible — survives OS updates and beats most scheme-hijack attacks.
• Bind tokens to the device when the IdP supports DPoP or mTLS.

questions.question: How does code obfuscation work in Flutter, and how do you symbolicate crashes after enabling it?
questions.answer:
Dart compiles to AOT machine code on release builds, which is harder to read than JS/Java but still leaks identifier names in the snapshot. `--obfuscate` strips those names; `--split-debug-info=<dir>` writes the symbol map to a separate file you keep server-side.

What obfuscation does:
• Replaces class, method, and field names with short opaque tokens in the binary
• Reduces the value of static analysis for an attacker reverse-engineering your APK/IPA
• Does NOT encrypt assets, does NOT prevent decompilation, does NOT secure secrets — pair with other defenses

The symbol-map file:
• Contains the mapping from obfuscated tokens back to your real symbols
• MUST be archived per release/version — it's the only way to make a future crash readable
• Treat it like source: keep it private, ideally in your CI artifact store

Symbolicating crashes:
• On a stack trace from an obfuscated build, frames look like `package:_oz/abc123.dart 15:7`
• Run `flutter symbolize -i <stacktrace.txt> -d <split-debug-info-dir>/app.android-arm64.symbols` to recover real names
• Crashlytics, Sentry, and similar uploaders accept the symbols file and symbolicate automatically — upload from CI on each release
questions.code_example:
# 1) Build a release with obfuscation + split debug info
flutter build apk --release \
  --obfuscate \
  --split-debug-info=build/symbols/v1_2_3

flutter build ipa --release \
  --obfuscate \
  --split-debug-info=build/symbols/v1_2_3

# 2) Archive the symbols by app version, e.g.
#    s3://acme-mobile/symbols/android/1.2.3/
#    s3://acme-mobile/symbols/ios/1.2.3/

# 3) Upload to your crash reporter (Crashlytics example)
firebase crashlytics:symbols:upload \
  --app=1:123:android:abc \
  build/symbols/v1_2_3

# 4) Symbolicate a stack trace by hand if needed
flutter symbolize \
  -i ./crash.txt \
  -d build/symbols/v1_2_3/app.android-arm64.symbols

# ── CI gate (GitHub Actions example) ─────────────────
# Fail the release pipeline if symbols aren't archived
# - run: |
#     test -f build/symbols/v${VER}/app.android-arm64.symbols
#     test -f build/symbols/v${VER}/app.ios-arm64.symbols
# - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
#   with:
#     name: symbols-v${VER}
#     path: build/symbols/v${VER}
#     retention-days: 365

questions.question: How do you detect a jailbroken / rooted device, and what should you actually do about it?
questions.answer:
Jailbreak (iOS) and root (Android) give the user — and any malware on their device — privileges that defeat sandboxing, secure storage, and pinning. Detection is heuristic, never definitive. Treat it as a signal, not a verdict.

What you can check:
• flutter_jailbreak_detection / safe_device packages bundle the common heuristics
• iOS: presence of /Applications/Cydia.app, write access to /private, suspicious schemes (cydia://), substrate-style hooks
• Android: su binary in PATH, BusyBox, magisk artifacts, dangerous system properties (ro.debuggable, ro.secure), test-keys signing
• Both: app running with a debugger attached (`Process.isRunningInTestMode` won't catch this — you need platform calls)

What to actually do — graduated response:
• Banking/health: refuse to launch sensitive features; show "this device is not supported"
• High-stakes consumer (payments, identity): degrade — disable biometrics, force re-auth more often, require hardware-attested keys
• General consumer: log a signal, lower the trust score for risk decisions on the server side, but DON'T break the app — false positives are common (custom ROMs, dev devices)
• Don't show a leaked detection message that helps an attacker bypass the check; deny silently and rely on server-side enforcement

Key principle: client-side detection is bypassable in minutes by anyone with Frida/Objection. The real defense is server-side: device attestation (Play Integrity, App Attest), short-lived tokens, anomaly detection on transactions.

questions.question: What are App Transport Security (iOS) and Network Security Config (Android), and when do you have to touch them?
questions.answer:
Both platforms ship a system-level network policy that runs BEFORE your code. They block plaintext HTTP, weak ciphers, and self-signed certs by default. They also let you opt in to stricter policies (HTTPS-only by domain, custom CAs, debug-only relaxations). If your traffic looks fine in dev but mysteriously breaks in production, this is the first place to look.

iOS — App Transport Security (ATS):
• Configured in Info.plist under `NSAppTransportSecurity`
• Blocks HTTP, TLS < 1.2, and weak forward-secrecy ciphers by default
• Granular exceptions per domain via `NSExceptionDomains`
• Apple actively rejects apps that turn off ATS globally without justification
• Use `NSAllowsArbitraryLoadsInWebContent` if you specifically need cleartext WebView content but want strict native networking

Android — Network Security Config:
• XML at `res/xml/network_security_config.xml`, referenced from AndroidManifest with `android:networkSecurityConfig`
• `cleartextTrafficPermitted="false"` is the default on API 28+
• Define `<domain-config>` to whitelist self-signed certs for staging, or custom CAs for enterprise environments
• `<debug-overrides>` lets you accept user-installed CAs (Charles, Proxyman) for debug builds only — never in release

When you must touch them:
• Talking to a service that doesn't have proper TLS (legacy IoT, sensor) — narrow exception per domain
• Using a corporate root CA that the OS doesn't trust
• Debugging with a man-in-the-middle proxy in development
• Allowing cleartext during gradual TLS rollout

Never globally permit cleartext or arbitrary loads in production. Reviewers and security audits will flag it.
questions.code_example:
<!-- iOS: ios/Runner/Info.plist — narrow ATS exception per domain -->
<key>NSAppTransportSecurity</key>
<dict>
  <key>NSAllowsArbitraryLoads</key>
  <false/>
  <key>NSExceptionDomains</key>
  <dict>
    <key>legacy-sensor.example.com</key>
    <dict>
      <!-- Plaintext only for THIS host, only because we can't fix the device -->
      <key>NSExceptionAllowsInsecureHTTPLoads</key>
      <true/>
      <key>NSIncludesSubdomains</key>
      <false/>
    </dict>
  </dict>
</dict>

<!-- Android: android/app/src/main/res/xml/network_security_config.xml -->
<network-security-config>
  <!-- Default: HTTPS only, system-trusted CAs only -->
  <base-config cleartextTrafficPermitted="false">
    <trust-anchors>
      <certificates src="system"/>
    </trust-anchors>
  </base-config>

  <!-- Staging: pin a self-signed cert -->
  <domain-config>
    <domain includeSubdomains="true">staging.example.com</domain>
    <trust-anchors>
      <certificates src="@raw/staging_ca"/>
    </trust-anchors>
  </domain-config>

  <!-- Debug-only: accept proxy-installed CAs (Charles, Proxyman) -->
  <debug-overrides>
    <trust-anchors>
      <certificates src="system"/>
      <certificates src="user"/>
    </trust-anchors>
  </debug-overrides>
</network-security-config>

<!-- AndroidManifest.xml:
     <application
       android:networkSecurityConfig="@xml/network_security_config"
       ...> -->

questions.question: How do you avoid leaking sensitive data through logs, screenshots, and clipboard?
questions.answer:
Most production leaks aren't fancy attacks — they're tokens in crash logs, customer PII in analytics events, payment forms visible in the iOS app switcher, and passwords copied to a synced Universal Clipboard.

Logs:
• Never `print` or `debugPrint` tokens, refresh codes, or full request/response bodies in release. Configure a log filter that strips Authorization headers and known PII keys before reaching the sink.
• Crash reporters (Crashlytics, Sentry) capture device state — set scrubbing rules for keys like password, otp, ssn, card.
• Don't ship a debug log file to the user via share/email without redacting first.

Screenshots / app switcher / screen recording:
• Android: `WindowManager.FLAG_SECURE` blocks screenshots, screen recording, and the recents-thumbnail. Apply only on screens that need it (login, card entry, secret notes) — global FLAG_SECURE breaks accessibility tools.
• iOS: there's no equivalent of FLAG_SECURE. Standard practice is to overlay a blurred image when the app resigns active so the recents preview doesn't expose the screen.

Clipboard:
• iOS Universal Clipboard syncs across the user's devices — secrets pasted on phone end up on Mac. Avoid putting tokens or OTPs there. If you must copy a secret, mark it expiring with `localOnly: true` (UIPasteboard.LocalOnly) and clear after a short timeout.
• Android: pre-13 the clipboard is readable by any foreground app. Same advice — don't copy secrets unless required, clear quickly.

Keyboard:
• `TextField` with `autocorrect: false, enableSuggestions: false` and `obscureText: true` for password-like fields prevents iOS/Android from caching content in their dictionaries.

questions.question: How do you implement a robust access-token + refresh-token rotation strategy?
questions.answer:
Long-lived bearer tokens are the most common credential-leak vector in mobile apps. The standard defense is short-lived access tokens (minutes) plus a refresh token that mints new ones — but the refresh path itself has subtle pitfalls.

Design principles:
• Access token in memory (or short-TTL secure storage). Refresh token in flutter_secure_storage. Never put either in SharedPreferences or in the URL.
• Access token short — 5–15 minutes. Refresh token longer (days/weeks) but EVERY refresh issues a NEW refresh token (rotation). The IdP invalidates the old one. If an attacker steals an old refresh token and uses it, the legitimate device gets a 401 on its next refresh — that's your detection signal.
• Single-flight refresh: under concurrent requests, multiple 401s shouldn't all kick off concurrent refreshes. Funnel them through one in-flight Future.
• On server-side revocation (logout, password change, suspicious activity): the IdP rejects the refresh token; the client must wipe credentials and force re-auth.
• Bind the refresh token to the device when possible — DPoP, mTLS, or device-attested certificates make a stolen token unusable on another device.

Flow:
1. Request returns 401
2. Acquire the refresh lock (single-flight) → call /refresh with the refresh token → receive new pair
3. Update storage atomically; release the lock; replay the original request
4. If /refresh returns 401: wipe storage, navigate to login

questions.question: What are the security pitfalls of using WebView in a Flutter app?
questions.answer:
WebViews host arbitrary web content with the trust of your app process. Everything you've protected with secure storage, certificate pinning, and platform sandboxing can be bypassed if you mis-configure a WebView. Several apps have shipped account-takeover bugs through WebViews.

Things to nail down:

1) Don't load untrusted URLs at all. If the source isn't a domain you fully control, prefer the system browser (url_launcher with `mode: LaunchMode.externalApplication`).

2) Disable JavaScript bridges unless required. `JavaScriptChannel`s let web code call your Dart code. Treat any channel as a public API: validate inputs ruthlessly, allow only specific origins, and never expose anything that opens secure storage, the file system, or device APIs by name.

3) Restrict navigation. Use `NavigationDelegate` (or onNavigationRequest) to block redirects to non-whitelisted hosts. Phishing pages love a forgotten redirect.

4) Cookies and storage: WebViews share cookies with the app's WebKit/WebView session. Don't reuse the same web view for an in-app webview and an OAuth page — clear cookies between flows or use a session-only configuration.

5) Mixed content + cleartext: ensure your WebView can't load HTTP subresources from an HTTPS page. Android's WebView allows mixed content by default in some versions; set `setMixedContentMode(MIXED_CONTENT_NEVER_ALLOW)`.

6) File access: `setAllowFileAccess(false)` and `setAllowFileAccessFromFileURLs(false)` unless you specifically need them. file:// URLs in a WebView can read app sandbox files.

7) Don't put auth in WebView. OAuth/OIDC flows must use the system browser (ASWebAuthenticationSession / Custom Tabs), not an embedded WebView. Apple actively rejects apps that put their sign-in into a WebView.

8) Keep the WebView engine up to date. On Android, system WebView is updated by Google Play; you still need to test against the version range your install base runs.

questions.question: How do you set up CI/CD for Flutter with GitHub Actions?
questions.answer:
CI/CD pipeline structure:

CI (every PR):
1. Checkout → setup Flutter → pub get
2. dart format --check
3. flutter analyze
4. flutter test --coverage
5. Upload coverage to Codecov

CD (merge to main/release):
1. Build signed artifact (APK/IPA)
2. Upload to Firebase App Distribution (beta) or Play Store/App Store (prod)

Secrets management: Store keystore, certificates, API keys as GitHub Secrets. Never commit them.

Fastlane: Automate signing, versioning, screenshots, and store submissions.
questions.code_example:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: Flutter CI

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, develop]
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with:
          flutter-version: '3.22.0'
          cache: true

      - run: flutter pub get

      - name: Check formatting
        run: dart format --output=none --set-exit-if-changed .

      - name: Analyze
        run: flutter analyze --fatal-infos

      - name: Test with coverage
        run: flutter test --coverage

      - name: Upload coverage
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
        with:
          file: coverage/lcov.info

  build-android:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0', cache: true }

      - name: Decode keystore
        run: |
          echo "${{ secrets.KEYSTORE_B64 }}" | base64 --decode > android/app/release.jks

      - name: Build release APK
        env:
          STORE_PASS: ${{ secrets.KEYSTORE_PASS }}
          KEY_ALIAS: ${{ secrets.KEY_ALIAS }}
          KEY_PASS: ${{ secrets.KEY_PASS }}
        run: flutter build apk --release --obfuscate --split-debug-info=debug-info/

      - name: Deploy to Firebase App Distribution
        uses: wzieba/Firebase-Distribution-Github-Action@v1
        with:
          appId: ${{ secrets.FIREBASE_APP_ID }}
          token: ${{ secrets.FIREBASE_TOKEN }}
          groups: beta-testers
          file: build/app/outputs/flutter-apk/app-release.apk
          releaseNotes: "Build from commit ${{ github.sha }}"

questions.question: What are Flutter flavors and how do you set up dev/staging/production environments?
questions.answer:
Flavors (called "schemes" on iOS, "build types/product flavors" on Android) let you build multiple variants of your app from the same codebase — with different:
• Bundle IDs / package names
• API endpoints
• App names and icons
• Firebase projects
• Feature flags

This enables separate dev/staging/prod apps installable side-by-side.

Setup steps:
1. Configure Android productFlavors in build.gradle
2. Configure iOS schemes in Xcode
3. Create Dart entry points per flavor (or use --dart-define)
4. Use flutter_flavorizr package to automate the setup

questions.question: How do you sign Android and iOS builds in CI without leaking the keys?
questions.answer:
Signing is the single highest-stakes secret in mobile CI. Lose the upload key and you can't publish updates; leak the .p12 and someone can ship a malicious update under your identity. The discipline is: keys stay encrypted at rest, decoded at build time into a temporary file, and shredded when the job ends.

Android:
• Generate a release keystore (.jks) once, store it Base64-encoded in GitHub Secrets / your CI's secret store
• A keystore.properties file holds storeFile, storePassword, keyAlias, keyPassword — generated on the fly in CI from secrets
• `signingConfigs.release` in build.gradle reads from keystore.properties
• Use Play App Signing — let Google hold the upload key. You sign with an upload key, Google re-signs with the actual app signing key. Lose the upload key and you can recover via Play Console.

iOS:
• Two artifacts: a Distribution Certificate (.p12) and a Provisioning Profile (.mobileprovision)
• fastlane match is the standard — store both, encrypted, in a private git repo; CI calls `fastlane match appstore --readonly`
• Alternative: store .p12 + profile as Base64 in CI secrets, decode in a temp keychain that's deleted after build

Always:
• Mark the secrets as masked in CI output
• Restrict who can read or rotate them; keep an audit log
• Rotate after any team member with access leaves
• Do not commit keystore.properties or .p12 — put them in .gitignore explicitly
questions.code_example:
# .github/workflows/release.yml — Android signing
jobs:
  build-android:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0' }

      - name: Decode keystore
        run: echo "${{ secrets.KEYSTORE_BASE64 }}" | base64 --decode > android/app/release.jks

      - name: Write keystore.properties
        run: |
          cat > android/key.properties <<EOF
          storeFile=release.jks
          storePassword=${{ secrets.KEYSTORE_PASSWORD }}
          keyAlias=${{ secrets.KEY_ALIAS }}
          keyPassword=${{ secrets.KEY_PASSWORD }}
          EOF

      - name: Build app bundle
        run: flutter build appbundle --release --obfuscate --split-debug-info=symbols/

      - name: Cleanup
        if: always()
        run: |
          rm -f android/app/release.jks android/key.properties

# build.gradle reads the properties
# def keystoreProps = new Properties()
# keystoreProps.load(new FileInputStream(rootProject.file("key.properties")))
# signingConfigs { release { storeFile file(keystoreProps['storeFile']); ... } }

# ── iOS: fastlane match — recommended ────────────
# Fastfile
# lane :ci_release do
#   setup_ci   # creates a temporary keychain
#   match(type: 'appstore', readonly: true,
#         git_url: 'git@github.com:acme/match-certs.git')
#   build_app(scheme: 'Runner', export_method: 'app-store')
# end

# CI step:
#   - run: bundle exec fastlane ios ci_release
#     env:
#       MATCH_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.MATCH_PASSWORD }}
#       APP_STORE_CONNECT_API_KEY_KEY: ${{ secrets.APP_STORE_API_KEY }}
#       MATCH_KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD }}

questions.question: How do you manage app versioning in Flutter — version, build number, semver?
questions.answer:
Three numbers travel together in every build: the public version, the build number, and the semantic intent of the change. They serve different audiences.

pubspec.yaml `version` field — `1.4.2+87`:
• `1.4.2` is the public version (semver-style: major.minor.patch). Visible in stores, in About screens.
• `+87` is the build number — strictly increasing integer. Stores require it to be higher than any previously uploaded build.
• Flutter expands this into Android `versionName`/`versionCode` and iOS `CFBundleShortVersionString`/`CFBundleVersion`

Rules to live by:
• Build number must always go up. The cleanest source is the CI build counter (GitHub Actions `${{ github.run_number }}`) or a monotonic timestamp
• Don't let two CI runs produce the same build number — even on a re-run
• Public version follows semver intent: bump patch for fixes, minor for additive features, major for breaking UX/API changes that users care about
• On hotfixes branched from a tagged release, bump only the patch and the build number — don't accidentally release `1.5.0+old` ahead of `1.4.2`

Driving it from one source:
• Manual: edit pubspec.yaml on every release. Simple, fragile.
• `flutter build --build-name=1.4.2 --build-number=$RUN_NUMBER` overrides pubspec at build time. Recommended for CI.
• Generated build-info file in code so the running app can show its own version (`PackageInfo.fromPlatform()`).
questions.code_example:
# pubspec.yaml
version: 1.4.2+87
# major.minor.patch+build_number
#  ↓             ↓
#  Android versionName     versionCode = 87
#  iOS CFBundleShortVer    CFBundleVersion

# .github/workflows/release.yml
name: Release
on:
  push:
    tags: ['v*']      # e.g. v1.4.2 triggers a release build

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0' }

      - name: Derive version
        id: ver
        run: |
          # Strip leading 'v' from the tag
          VERSION="${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}"
          BUILD="${{ github.run_number }}"
          echo "version=$VERSION"   >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "build=$BUILD"       >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Build (override pubspec values)
        run: |
          flutter build appbundle --release \
            --build-name=${{ steps.ver.outputs.version }} \
            --build-number=${{ steps.ver.outputs.build }} \
            --obfuscate --split-debug-info=symbols/

# ── Read version inside the app at runtime ──────────
# import 'package:package_info_plus/package_info_plus.dart';
#
# final info = await PackageInfo.fromPlatform();
# print('${info.version}+${info.buildNumber}');  // 1.4.2+87

questions.question: How do you automate Play Store and App Store releases from CI?
questions.answer:
Both stores expose APIs for unattended uploads. Reaching them safely from CI is the standard final step of a release pipeline.

Google Play:
• Generate a service account in Google Cloud, grant it Play Console access
• Download its JSON key — store as a CI secret
• Use `fastlane supply` or the Gradle `play-publisher` plugin to upload an .aab and bump tracks (internal → alpha → beta → production)
• Tracks let you stage rollouts: upload to internal first, promote when smoke tests pass, then ramp production from 1% → 10% → 100%

Apple App Store:
• Generate an App Store Connect API Key (.p8 + key id + issuer id) — replaces username/password / 2FA pain
• Use `fastlane pilot` to upload to TestFlight, `deliver` to push metadata + binary to App Store, `submit_to_app_review` to start review
• TestFlight build availability typically takes 5–30 minutes after upload
• App Store metadata (screenshots, descriptions, what's new) can be checked into the repo (fastlane metadata) so each release ships with version-controlled copy

Guardrails:
• Always upload to internal/TestFlight first; gate prod promotion on a manual approval step or a green smoke job
• On a build pulled from review, your CI should be able to re-promote a previous artifact rather than rebuild — keep .aab/.ipa artifacts retained for at least 90 days
• Never let a CI matrix accidentally upload the same build to production twice; idempotency check on the build number is cheap insurance
questions.code_example:
# fastlane/Fastfile
default_platform(:android)

platform :android do
  desc 'Upload AAB to Play Console internal track'
  lane :internal do
    upload_to_play_store(
      track: 'internal',
      aab: '../build/app/outputs/bundle/release/app-release.aab',
      json_key_data: ENV['PLAY_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON'],
      release_status: 'completed',
    )
  end

  desc 'Promote internal → production with staged rollout'
  lane :promote do |options|
    upload_to_play_store(
      track: 'internal', track_promote_to: 'production',
      rollout: options[:rollout] || '0.1',  # 10%
      json_key_data: ENV['PLAY_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON'],
    )
  end
end

platform :ios do
  desc 'Upload IPA to TestFlight'
  lane :beta do
    api_key = app_store_connect_api_key(
      key_id: ENV['ASC_KEY_ID'],
      issuer_id: ENV['ASC_ISSUER_ID'],
      key_content: ENV['ASC_API_KEY_P8'],
    )
    upload_to_testflight(
      api_key: api_key,
      ipa: '../build/ios/ipa/Runner.ipa',
      skip_waiting_for_build_processing: true,
    )
  end
end

# .github/workflows/release.yml — release stage
# - run: bundle exec fastlane android internal
# - run: bundle exec fastlane ios beta
# - name: Manual approval for prod ramp
#   uses: trstringer/manual-approval@v1
#   with: { approvers: alice,bob }
# - run: bundle exec fastlane android promote rollout:0.1

questions.question: How do you set up beta distribution with Firebase App Distribution and TestFlight?
questions.answer:
Beta channels are how you get a build into the hands of real users without going through the public store review and ramp. Each platform has a different best-practice channel.

iOS — TestFlight (Apple-only, no alternative):
• Internal testers: up to 100, no review needed, instant access — invite by Apple ID
• External testers: up to 10,000, requires Apple's beta review (~24h on first build, automatic on subsequent ones in the same group)
• Build expires after 90 days, so testers always have a fresh artifact
• Crash reports flow into Xcode Organizer / your APM

Android — Firebase App Distribution OR Play Console internal/closed testing:
• Firebase App Distribution: by-email invitations, OTA updates, supports APKs (faster turnaround for ad-hoc testing)
• Play Console internal track: tied to the store identity — closer to what users actually get; required if you want Play Integrity attestations to work in beta
• Many teams run both — Firebase for engineering smoke tests, Play internal for product/QA

Process hygiene:
• Auto-publish every main-branch build to a private internal channel — catches build issues before they touch a tester
• Promote tagged release builds to a wider beta group; keep release notes per build
• Wire crash reports / analytics back to a build_id so you can correlate beta crashes to commits
• Have a recovery path: if a beta is broken, you can roll back testers to an older artifact without going through a full release
questions.code_example:
# fastlane/Fastfile — Firebase App Distribution
platform :android do
  desc 'Distribute APK to internal smoke channel'
  lane :smoke do
    firebase_app_distribution(
      app: ENV['FIREBASE_ANDROID_APP_ID'],   # 1:NNN:android:xxx
      service_credentials_file: 'firebase-cd.json',
      android_artifact_path: '../build/app/outputs/flutter-apk/app-release.apk',
      android_artifact_type: 'APK',
      groups: 'engineering-smoke',
      release_notes_file: 'release_notes.txt',
    )
  end
end

# .github/workflows/beta.yml
name: Beta
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  android-beta:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0' }
      - run: flutter build apk --release --obfuscate --split-debug-info=symbols/
      - name: Distribute to Firebase
        uses: wzieba/Firebase-Distribution-Github-Action@v1
        with:
          appId: ${{ secrets.FIREBASE_ANDROID_APP_ID }}
          serviceCredentialsFileContent: ${{ secrets.FIREBASE_SA_JSON }}
          groups: engineering-smoke
          file: build/app/outputs/flutter-apk/app-release.apk
          releaseNotes: |
            Build: ${{ github.sha }}
            Branch: ${{ github.ref_name }}

  ios-testflight:
    runs-on: macos-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0' }
      - name: Build IPA
        run: flutter build ipa --release --obfuscate --split-debug-info=symbols/
      - name: Upload to TestFlight
        uses: apple-actions/upload-testflight-build@v1
        with:
          app-path: build/ios/ipa/Runner.ipa
          issuer-id: ${{ secrets.ASC_ISSUER_ID }}
          api-key-id: ${{ secrets.ASC_KEY_ID }}
          api-private-key: ${{ secrets.ASC_API_KEY_P8 }}

questions.question: How do you avoid flaky integration tests in Flutter CI?
questions.answer:
Integration tests on emulators in CI are notorious for flakes that block green builds for reasons unrelated to your code. The solution is a mix of test design (eliminate timing assumptions) and infra design (eliminate environment surprises).

Design the tests:
• Never `Future.delayed` to wait for UI — use `await tester.pumpAndSettle()` for animations, or pump until a finder appears with a polling helper. Sleeps are the #1 source of flake.
• Replace network with deterministic test doubles. The test should not depend on a real backend, ever. Use a recorded fixture or an in-process fake server (`shelf` works well).
• Stub time. If your code reads `DateTime.now()` or starts timers, expose a Clock abstraction and inject a fake; otherwise tests behave differently at midnight.
• Make IDs stable. Use Keys for finder-by-id rather than text. Translations and rebuilds break text-based finders.
• Reset state between tests. Wipe localStorage/secure storage in setUp; set a fresh ProviderScope (Riverpod) or recreate the Cubits.

Design the infra:
• Use one device per test, not parallel devices on the same emulator — emulators sharing GPU on CI runners stutter
• Boot the emulator with `-no-snapshot-save -no-window -no-boot-anim -gpu swiftshader_indirect` for headless reliability
• Pre-warm: open a dummy Flutter app once before running the suite to avoid first-build cold-start variance
• Quarantine flakes: tag a test `@Tags(['flaky'])` and run it nightly with retries instead of blocking PRs
• Capture screenshots and logs on failure — re-running blindly hides flake patterns

questions.question: What is fastlane and what does it actually buy you over a hand-rolled CI script?
questions.answer:
fastlane is a Ruby tool that turns the long, error-prone sequences of cert/profile/build/upload commands into named lanes. You write a Fastfile once, then `fastlane release` does the right thing on any machine — local, CI, or a teammate's laptop.

What lanes wrap:
• `match` — synchronizes signing certs and provisioning profiles across the team via an encrypted git repo
• `gym` — builds an iOS .ipa with the right scheme, configuration, export options
• `pilot` / `upload_to_testflight` — uploads to TestFlight and waits for processing
• `supply` / `upload_to_play_store` — uploads .aab/.apk to Play and manages tracks
• `deliver` — manages App Store metadata, screenshots, what's new
• `screengrab` / `snapshot` — automated localized screenshots

What you actually buy:
• Reproducibility: any clean machine with the right secrets can produce the same artifact
• Auditability: lanes are checked into the repo, code-reviewed like any other code
• Smaller-blast-radius secrets: fastlane can use App Store Connect API keys (no human 2FA), match (read-only mode in CI)
• Recovery patterns: lanes that re-promote already-built artifacts, lanes that roll back, lanes that bisect a regression by version

When NOT to bother:
• A solo project shipping monthly — bash + `flutter build` + a couple of GitHub actions is enough
• If you're already on Bitrise/Codemagic that abstracts this — adding fastlane doubles the layers without much benefit
questions.code_example:
# fastlane/Fastfile
default_platform(:ios)

before_all do
  ensure_git_status_clean
  ensure_git_branch(branch: 'main')
end

platform :ios do
  desc 'Sync signing — read-only in CI'
  lane :certs do
    match(type: 'appstore', readonly: ENV['CI'] == 'true')
  end

  desc 'Build, archive, upload to TestFlight'
  lane :beta do
    certs
    increment_build_number(build_number: ENV['CI_BUILD_NUMBER'] || latest_testflight_build_number + 1)
    build_app(
      scheme: 'Runner',
      export_method: 'app-store',
      output_directory: 'build/ios/ipa',
    )
    upload_to_testflight(
      skip_waiting_for_build_processing: true,
    )
  end

  desc 'Promote latest TestFlight build to App Store, with phased release'
  lane :release do
    deliver(
      submit_for_review: true,
      automatic_release: false,
      phased_release: true,                 # 1% / 2% / 5% / 10% / 20% / 50% / 100%
      force: true,                          # skip metadata HTML preview
      precheck_include_in_app_purchases: true,
      submission_information: { add_id_info_uses_idfa: false },
    )
  end
end

# Usage
# bundle exec fastlane ios beta             # nightly
# bundle exec fastlane ios release          # tagged release
# Override env: ASC_KEY_ID / ASC_ISSUER_ID / ASC_API_KEY_P8 set in CI

questions.question: How do you do staged rollouts and quick rollbacks safely?
questions.answer:
Mobile releases ship to thousands of devices in minutes once you push the button. A staged rollout caps the blast radius if something is broken; a clean rollback path lets you stop bleeding fast.

Staged rollouts:
• Play Console — production track supports percentage rollouts (1% → 5% → 20% → 50% → 100%). Bake at each stage for at least 24h while watching crashlytics + key metrics.
• App Store — Phased Release ramps 1/2/5/10/20/50/100% over 7 days. You can pause from App Store Connect at any stage.
• Both let you halt and resume. Use the pause button when in doubt — the cost of a 12h delay is negligible.

What to monitor during a rollout:
• Crash-free user rate vs. previous version (set a guardrail: <0.5% drop is fine, >1% drop pause)
• Critical funnel metrics: sign-in success, checkout completion, push opt-in
• Backend errors that spiked with the rollout
• Reviews / support tickets in real time

Rollback options (in order of preference):
• Halt the staged rollout — Play Console immediately stops new users from getting the bad build; Phased Release pauses the ramp
• Server-side feature flag — hide the bad feature without an app update. Always ship risky features behind a remote flag.
• Re-release the previous build with a new version number — slow (review queue), but works. Keep the previous artifact warm in CI for 30+ days.
• Force-update screen — the app calls a config endpoint that says "please update". The current build still runs but a soft block escalates to hard if needed.

Guardrail: ship every release behind at least one feature flag for the new code paths. "Roll back" by toggling the flag is 1000x faster and safer than re-releasing.

questions.question: Explain Flutter's three trees: Widget, Element, and RenderObject.
questions.answer:
Flutter maintains three parallel trees for efficient UI rendering:

1. Widget Tree — immutable blueprints (what you write in build())
   • Created fresh on every build() call
   • Lightweight value objects — cheap to recreate
   • Describes WHAT to show

2. Element Tree — mutable, long-lived instances
   • Bridges widgets and render objects
   • Holds widget + mounted state
   • Performs reconciliation (diffing old vs new widget tree)
   • Created once; updated when widget changes

3. RenderObject Tree — actual layout, paint, hit-testing
   • Expensive to create, persists long-term
   • Handles constraints (layout), sizes, and painting
   • Updated only when layout/paint properties change

Reconciliation: Same type at same position → update element (fast). Different type → unmount old, mount new (creates new RenderObject).

Keys help Flutter identify elements when the position changes (e.g., reordering lists).

questions.question: How does Flutter's rendering pipeline work? Explain the frame lifecycle.
questions.answer:
Flutter's rendering pipeline for each frame (must complete in <16ms for 60fps):

1. Animation tick — Scheduler notifies that a new frame is needed
2. Build phase — dirty Elements call build(), creating new Widget subtrees
3. Layout phase — RenderObjects compute sizes and positions (constraints go down, sizes go up)
4. Paint phase — RenderObjects paint onto Layers (display list)
5. Compositing — Layer tree composited into a scene
6. Rasterization — Skia/Impeller rasterizes the scene on the GPU thread

The layout protocol (constraints go down, sizes go up, parent sets position):
• Parent passes BoxConstraints (min/max width/height) to child
• Child determines its own size within constraints
• Parent positions the child

questions.question: What are slivers in Flutter and how do you build custom scroll effects?
questions.answer:
